ON aft m. 'id.', Far. alvur, a/vi m. 'fireplace; forge' (IE?) -Hitt. gen. bappenas, loc. bappeni 'baking kiln, fire-pit, broiler (oven)'. The Germanic and Hittite words are formally and semantically close, which makes it attractive to reconstruct an old l/n-stem \*h₂ep-[o}l, gen. \*h₂p-n-6s. The Hittite word can alternatively be compared to \*ufna- 'oven', which may be an old Wanderwort.
ON eftir prep., adv. 'id.', Far. eftir prep., adv. 'id.', OE refter prep. 'id.', E after, OFri. efter prep., adv. 'id.', Du. achter prep. 'id.', OHG aftar prep. 'id.', MHG after prep. 'id.'

OS agaleto adv. 'eagerly, diligently', OHG agaleizi adj. 'importunate' (GM). A haplologized compound of the elements \*agla- 'shameful' and \*/aitja( continued as G Rhnl. leizig adj. 'annoying; excited'). The word has formerly been analyzed as \*agla- + \*ait-, but the second element is neither a word nor a suffix, which leaves the analysis unsatisfactory (cf. Lehmann 1986: 10). Also cf. Go. aglaiti n. 'debauchery, licentiousness, dissipation', QHG agaleizi n. 'eagerness, industry, importunity' < \*agla-laitja-. The sometimes adduced OE aglrec n. 'oppression, distress', apparently from \*aga-laikja-, could theoretic.ally be a folk-etymological distortion of \*aglaitja-, but is more likely to…

Go. ogan, 3sg. og pret.-pres. 'id.' (IE) - Gr. li)coμm 'to grieve, lament' < \*h₂egh-e-; Olr. -agadar 'to fear' < \*h₂e-h₂09h·e-. The Gothic verb is conjugated as a preterite-present of the reduplicating class, PGm. 3sg. \*oge, 3pl. \*iigun < \*h₂e-h₂ogh-e, \*h₂e-h₂ogh-nt, which is formally close to Olr. -agadar < \*h₂e-h₂ogh- (Matasovic 2009: 26). The full-grade root \*iig- served as the basis for the Gothic infinitive form ogan sis, while the original zero grade survived in the petrified ptc. un-agands adj. 'fearless'. Also cf. \*agiz-, Go. agei, OHG agf, egf f. 'fear' < \*agfn-, \*ogjan- 'to scare' and OE oga m. 'terror' < \*iigan-.
ON agn n. 'id.', Far. agn n. 'id.'
OE egede f. 'id.', OFri. eide f. 'id.', OS egitha f. 'id.', MDu. egede, eegde, eg f. 'id.', OHG egida f. 'id.', MHG egede, egde f. 'id.'

OE age, agu f. 'id.', WFri. akke c. 'id.', OHG aga(?) f. 'id.', MHG age f. 'id.' (GM). The co-occurrence of OE age < \*agon- and WFri. akke < \*akkon- could point to an old n-stem paradigm nom. \*ago, gen. \*akkaz < \*H6gh-on, \*Hogh-n-6s. The word further occurs in a compound with \*star(r)a(n)- in the other WGm. languages, cf. OS agastria, MLG he(g)ister, MDu. exter, aecster, Du. ekster 'id.' < \*aga-str-jon-. OHG agalstra, egilistra, G Elster, on the other hand, was created to a diminutive \*agila-. Further note OHG agaza < \*agatiin-.
Go. agls\* adj. 'disgraceful'

Go. aglus\* adj. 'difficult, toilsome', OE egle adj. 'difficult', E obs. ail adj. 'loathsome' (IE). The original u-stem that is attested as Go. aglus\* resurfaces as the ja-stem eg(e)le in OE. No clear etymology, but probably related to \*ag/a-.

grammatical_label: - \*h₂eCk'l-6n-eh₂-. The origin of the Verner alternation is unclear, as accentual mobility is unexpected in secondary eh₂-stems. It is possible, however, that the feminine developed from a neuter collective form \*h₂eCk'l-on-eh₂- (cf. Lat. agna) created to the n-stem \*h₂efk'l-on- as supported by Gr. CiKwv. It must then be assumed that the Verner alternation was adopted from this n-stem, which no doubt did have an accentually mobile paradigm. See also \*ahila-

Go. ahjan w.v. 'to assume, think' (GM). No clear etymology. The verb is obviously related to Go. aha m. 'mind, understanding' < \*ahan-, Go. ahma m. 'spirit' < \*ahman- and PGm. \*ahto'attention' ( q.v.), but it lacks clear extra-Gm. cognates. The connections with \*augon- 'eye' < \*h₃e'J<w- (Osthoff 1910: 175) and Gr. oKVo<; m. 'hesitation, shrinking' (Pokorny IEW: 774) do not convince, at any rate. Theoretically, the verb could be identical to ON reja w.v. 'to graze' < \*ahjan- (through a meaning 'to ruminate'), but it is preferable, in my view, to reconstruct this verb as \*aiwjan- (q.v.).

ON pxl f. 'shoulder', Far. 11ksl f. 'id.', OE eaxl f. 'armpit', OS ahs/a f. 'shoulder', OHG ahsa/a, ahs/a f. 'id.' (IE) - Lat ala f. 'armpit, wing', W echel f. 'axis' < \*h₂eks-(i)/-eh₂-. Derived from PIE \*h₂eKs-i- with an /-suffix, cf. \*nab/an- 'navel'.
Nw. dial. oks f. 'tip of the hub', OE eax m. 'axle', MDu. asse f. 'id.', Du. as c. 'id.', OHG ahsa f. 'id.', G Achse f. 'id.'

Also cf. OE eahtian, OS ahton, Du. achten, OHG ahton, G achten w.v. 'to mind, deem' < \*ahtojan- and ON, Far. ret/a w.v. 'to intend' < \*ahtilon-.

'id.' (DRV). An adjective derived from \*aigan- (q.v.), whence Go. ga-aiginon, eigenen w.v. 'to make one's own, to appropriate' < \*aigeniijan-. Also cf. Go. aigin, ON eigin, OE iigen, OFri. ain, ein, OS egan, OHG eigan n. 'property'.

Go. aihts f. 'property', ON rett f. 'family', Far. rett f. 'lineage, descent, family', OE reht f. 'possessions, property, lands', OHG eht f. 'possession, goods, wealth' (DRY). A ti-stem created to the root of \*ai9an- (q.v.). Note that the sometimes connected Ir. icht m. 'progeny, tribe' (quasi < \*h₂il<-ti-) is more likely to have been derived from root \*iek- 'to speak' (see \*jehan-) in view of the related MW ieith f. 'language, nation, race' < \*iek-ti-.
Go. aihtron wv. 'id.' (GM). Etymology uncertain: perhaps somehow derived from \*aikan- 'to appropriate' (q.v.). Unrelated to Gr. oiJCTp6׵ adj. 'miserable'.

Go. afaikan s.v. 'to deny, abjure', OHG in-eihhan s.v. 'to sacrifice; dedicate' (DRV). The fact that Go. aikan and OHG eihhan (cf. ChWdW9: 245) are reduplicated verbs, which typically have an o-grade of the root, could imply a pre-Gm. formation \*HoiCB1-e-. One could then compare Latv. afcinfit 'to call, invite' < \*Hoig-n(e)h₂- and Olr. eigid 'to cry' < \*h₁dg1-e-. From the Germanic perspective, however, it is more likely that the root \*aik- continues \*aikk- ( < \*Hoik-n-1, and developed from the preterite-present \*aigan- 'to own' (q.v.) with a semantic shift 'to make one's own' > 'to accept' (cf. EWAhd: II, 972). This is supported by OHG ur-eiche adj. 'own' < \*uz-aikja- and OHG (gi-)ei…
Nw. eikja w.v. 'to annoy, pester'
ON fkorni m. 'id.', Far. fkorni m. 'id.', Elfd. aikuonn m. 'id.', OE iicweorn(a) m. 'id.', MDu. eecoren m. 'id.', Du. eekhoren c. 'id.', OHG eihhurno, eihhorno m. 'id.', G Eichhornchen n. 'id.'

ON e/dr m. 'id.', Far. e/dur m. 'id.', Elfd. jiild m. 'id.', OE ii!led m. 'fire; fire blight' (DRY). A formation derived from \*ailjan- (OE re/an w.v. 'to burn') which in turn is a factitive to the noun \*aila- as implied by OE al n. 'fire' < \*ai/la- < \*aid/a- < \*h₂eidh-/o-, for which cf. \*aida-. W aelwyd f. 'fireplace' and Bret. oelad 'id.' were borrowed from early Anglo-Saxon.

ON eimyrja f. 'id.', OE remyrie f. 'id.', E embers, MDu. amere f. 'id.', OHG eimuria f. 'id.', MHG eimer(e) f. 'id.' (DRY). A compound of \*aima- 'steam, smoke' and \*usjon-, cf. ON ysja f. 'fire' < \*Hus-ieh₂-, See also \*uznan-.

Einbeerbaum 'id.' ::} \*h₁oi-n-io- (IE) - Hitt. ejan- n. 'a certain evergreen tree' < \*h₁ei-n-; Lat. iilni-pe/irus m. 'juniper' < \*h₁oi-no- (with unexplained j- and etymologically obscure second member). A word that is likely to have been a collective formation, especially in view of the neuters Far. eini and Elfd. iene. The word is usually reconstructed as PGm. \*jainja- on the basis of the comparison with Lat. iiini-perus, but the West Germanic forms exclude initial \*j. If Hitt. ejan- is related (Kroo…

ON cir adv. 'id.', Far. (margin ciri 'early in the morning', OE <ir adv. 'before', OFri. er prep., conj. 'id.', OS er adv. 'id.', Du. eer conj. 'id.', OHG e(r) adv., conj. 'before, earlier', MHG e(r) adv. 'earlier, sooner' ::}

Av. aiiara, gen. aiiqn n. 'day' < \*h₂ei-r/n-; Gr. ƀpl 'in the morning' < \*h₂ei-er-i; Gr. Tjtplo<; adj. 'matutinal' < \*h₂eier-io-. PGm. \*airi is identical to Gr. ƀpl, continuing the locative of an old r/n-stem meaning 'day' (or 'daybreak'?), cf. Av. aiiara. The e of \*ajeri was regularly raised to \*i after \*j, and triggered the merger of \*aji- with \*ai- (cf. \*aiza- 'oar' < PIE \*h₂ei-es-). Note that ON, Icel. aria, Sw. aria, Da. cirle adv. 'early' developed from cirliga by some sort of syncope, cf.fastl(ig}a 'firmly', framarl(ig)a 'in a forward position', ill(ilig)a 'badly, hideously' and gerla, gprla 'fully', harola 'very', var/a 'hardly'.

Go. airis adv. 'id.', ON cior adv. 'before, earlier' ::} \*h₂ei-er-is (IE).

Go. airus m. 'id.', ON árr m. 'messenger, servant', Icel. árr m. 'id.', OE ār m. 'id.', OS ēru m. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. Go. airinōn w.v. 'to be an emissary, to be a messenger', ON árna w.v. 'to intercede, to pray' < \*airinōn-. No further etymology.

Go. aiþs m. 'id.', ON eiðr m. 'id.', Far. eiður m. 'id.', OE āþ m. 'id.', E oath, OFri. āth, ēth m. 'id.', OS ēth m. 'id.', Du. eed c. 'id.', OHG eid m. 'id.', G Eid m. 'id.'

Go. uf-aiþeis\* adj. 'bound by an oath', OE āðe adj. 'qualified to swear an oath' (DRV). The meaning of the Old English form is clearly gerundival. Usually, gerundives are created to verbs, but in this particular case, the derivational base appears to have been \*aiþa- 'oath'.

OE āðum m. 'son-in-law; brother-in-law', OFri. āthum, āthom, āthem m. 'son-in-law, father-in-law', OHG eidum, eidam m. 'son-in-law', MHG eidem m. 'son-in-law, father-in-law' (GM). No certain etymology. The word is often linked to \*aiþa- 'oath', implying that the original meaning of the word was "son/father-in-oath". I personally prefer to derive the word from the same root as found in \*aiþin- - \*aiþōn-, i.e. with an original meaning "relative on the mother's side". Note that the word is usually reconstructed as \*aiþuman-, even though there is no compelling evidence for the u-vowel; Old High German, for instance, has both eidum and eidam, which means that the second vowel could be anaptyc…
Go. aiws m. 'id.', OFri. ewe f. 'id.', OS ewa f. 'id.', Du. eeuw c. 'century', OHG ewa f. 'eternity'

OE re, rew f. 'law, religion, marriage', OFri. ewe, e f. 'law', OS eo, eu m. 'law', OHG ewa, ea, eo f. 'law, right, will, contract', MHG ewe, e f. 'marriage, alliance' (IE). Probably related to Lat. jiis, jiiris n. 'law' < \* Hieu-es- and etymologically identical to \*aiwa/ii- 1 (q.v.). Hardly to Lat. aequus 'level, equal' < \*h₂eikwoor to Skt. eviis m.pl. 'conduct, habit, usage, custom' < \*h₁oi-uo-, which is derived from the root \*h₁ei- 'to go' (see \*aidja- and \*idiijan-).
MHG eisch adj. 'horrible'

ON <Eja w.v. (pret. aoa) 'to rest, eat', Du. eeuwen w.v. 'to feed, graze' (GM). Usually interpreted as a causative formation to the root \*h₂eKH- 'to eat', cf. Skt. iifayati 'to make eat' (for which cf. \*agana-), starting from a proto-form \*ahjan-. Still, the implied PIE causative \*h₂o/{H-eie- should have given PGm. \*agjan-, ON \*egja, which is not the case. The connection with Du. eeuwen (also cf. MDu. eeusel n. 'pasture') < \*aiwjan- (cf. De Vries 1962: 681) is therefore preferable.

Go. ais, gen. aizis n. 'ore', ON eir n. 'brass, copper', Ice!. eir n. 'brass, copper', OE iir, rer n. 'ore, brass, copper', E ore, OS er n. 'ore', MDu. eer n. 'copper, metal', OHG er n. 'ore', MHG er n. 'ore, iron'

Skt. ayas-, Av. aiiah- n. 'metal, copper', Lat. aes, aeris n. 'ore' < \*h₂ei-es-. A thematicized s-stem. The Germanic form developed from \*aizathrough \*ajez- < \*h₂ei-es- with regular loss of the medial e after j (cf. \*airi 'early' and \*laiza- 'clay'). The original PIE s-stem \*h₂ei-os, \*h₂(e)i-es-os may have been derived from the root \*h₂ei- 'to burn', cf. \*aida- and \*aison-. Also cf. OE reren, OHG erfn, G ehern adj. 'iron, brazen' < \*aizfna-.
ON eir f. 'mercy', OE ii.r f. 'honor, dignity; kindness, mercy', OFri. ere f. 'honor; tribute', Du. eer c. 'honor', OHG era f. 'id.', G Ehre f. 'id.'

Go. aqizi f. 'id.', ON !i1X f. 'id.', Far. fiJks f. 'id.', Elfd. okse f. 'id.', OE rex f. 'id.', E axe, OFri. axe f. 'id.', OS akus f. 'id.', Du. aks c. 'id.', OHG ackus f. 'id.', G Axt f. 'id.'
Go. a/an s.v. 'to grow on, feed on', ON ala s.v. 'to give birth to, nourish, support'

MLG alse, e/se(ne) 'id.', MDu. alsene, e/sen(e}, e/s{e) 'id.', Du. els c. 'id.', OHG a/ansa, alunsa f. 'id.' (DRV). A formation apparently created to \*ala- with the suffix \*-asno-, cf. Go. arfuazna 'arrow' and h/aiwazna 'grave'. The Middle Germanic forms with e-vocalism are sometimes assumed to point to a variant \*a/isno-, but the derivationally less problematic \*a/asnjo- would work, too.
ON e/fr f. 'id.', Elfd. ove f. 'id.', MLG elve 'riverbed' (GM). Likely to be related to the name of the river Elbe.
ON plpt, gen. alptar, elptr f. 'id.', Ice!. a/ft f. 'id.', OE ielfetu f. 'id.', E obs. elk(?) 'swan, goose', MHG a/biz, e/biz f. 'swan'
Go. alpeis adj. 'old' ( < \*alpja-), ON ellri comp. 'id.', OE eald adj. 'id.', E old, OFri. aid, auld adj. 'id.', OS aid adj. 'id.', Du. oud adj. 'id.', OHG alt adj. 'id.', G alt adj. 'id.'

ON elj'an n./f. 'endurance, energy', OE el/en n. 'courage', OS el/ean n. 'courage, power', OHG ellian, el/an n. 'courage', MHG el/en n. 'zeal, courage' (IE) - Hitt. balai - balijanzi 'to set in motion' < \*h₂l-6i-ei, \*h₂/-i-enti; Gr. laMw 'to send off, to stretch forth' < \*h₂i-h₂l-ie-. In view of the original meaning 'zeal', I assume a connection with the aforementioned Hittite and Greek forms, but others have compared e.g. Lat. a/acer adj. 'lively'. Also cf. ON e/ta w.v. 'to pursue' < \*a/-atjan-.

ON alka f. 'razor-bill', Far. ci/ka f. 'id.', OE ea/ce f. 'auk', E auk (EUR?) - To Lat. olor m. 'swan' < \*h₁el-or-; Olr. elu 'id.' < \*h₁el-on-; W a/arch 'id.' < \*h₁el-r-sk-o-? A formation apparently derived from the root \*al{/)- as continued by Sw. al-/6.gel 'long-tailed duck', dial. al m. 'id.' < \*ala- and Ice!. haf-el/a, hciv-ella, Far. 6gv-e/la f. 'id.' < \*a/ljon- (Torp 1909: 22; Magnusson 1989: 11 ). For the k-suffix, see \*balika/on- 'coot' and \*habuka- 'hawk'. If E obs. elk 'swan' belongs here, too, the connection with Lat o/or 'swan' gains some credibility. Modern E auk can be analyzed as a Norse loan.

Go. ala-brunst'OA.6KaucrTov'. •a/up- n. 'ale, beer' - ON {JI n. 'beer', OE ea/op n. 'ale', OS alo-fat n. 'ale-vat', MDu. ale, ael n. 'id.', MHG al-schaf n. 'ale vessel'

OE kmette f. 'id.', E ant, MDu. amete, emete, eemt f. 'id.', OHG ameiza f. 'id.', G Ameise f. 'id.' (DRY?). Origin unclear. The word is usually assumed to be a compound with the root of \*maitan- 'to cut', but the form and origin of the first element is difficult to establish. One could reconstruct \*uz-maitjo- "out-cutter" in which unstressed \*uz would give PWGm. \*a. I personally find it more attractive to connect \*emon- 2 'larva'. The word may then be a haplologized compound \*emo-maitjo- "larva cutter". A third option is to assume a substrate word connected with Gr. μtoa<; 'destructive insect in beans'. The Germanic word must then be analyzed as having an a-prefix (cf. \*arut- 'ore') c…

It is to be noted that ON aror is ambiguous regarding the dental, \*p and \*d having merged into o in this position. Cf. with a different suffix MHG arl m. 'small plow'.

ON erendi, erendi, eyrendi n. 'errand, message, business', Far. erindi n. 'id.', OE cerende n. 'id.', E errand, WFri. eernje w.v. 'to talk' ( < OFri. \*erendia, cf. Brandsma 1936: 68), OS iirundi n. The reconstruction of the word is problematic, because the different languages point to different proto-forms that cannot be reconciled with each other nor with the supposedly obvious cognate \*airu- 'messenger' (q.v.). OE cerende, OS arundi, OHG iirunti (also cf. WPhal. <Eren with a from fronted WGm. \*a) point to \*erundja- with PGm. \*e, while ON erindi and erindi imply a form \*arundja- with \*a. ON esja f. 'soapstone', Nw. esje f. 'soapstone; embers', OSw. «!Sia, <I:!Ssia f. 'smith's fire', Sw. dial. iissje 'embers', Da. esse 'smith's fire', OHG essa f. 'forge, melting-oven' (GM). Soapstone has high latent heat retention, and the Vikings therefore used to put large blocks of it around the fire so that they could radiate back the heat after the fire had gone out (Rapp 2009: 125). Soapstone was also used for pottery and casting molds because of its softness and heat resistence. Both of these uses may explain the semantic overlap of 'soapstone', 'fireplace' and 'embers'. Note that the correlation with \*ask6n- 'ashes' (q.v.) is uncertain.

'to fight, struggle' ˄ ݷbhiH-eh₁-ie- (EUR) - Olr. benaid 'to strike, hit', MW kymynu 'to hit, cut down', MBret. benajf'to cut' < \*bhiH-neh₂-; OCS biti, Ru. bit' (b'iu) 'to beat', SCr. bl'ti 'to beat, kill'; Ru. bitva, SCr. bl'tva f. 'battle' < \*bhiH-tueh₂-; OCS uboi m. 'murder', Ru. boj m. 'battle, fight, beating', SCr. baj m. 'battle, war' < \*bhoiH-o-. 1 reconstruct Ice!. bja as \*bilj)en- in view of the similar development of PGm. < \*filj)en- (q.v.) to ON fja 'to hate'. first_attestations: OSw. bisa w.v. 'id.', Sw. besa w.v. 'id.', Du. Flem. biezen w.v. 'to run around (of cattle)', OHG bison w.v. 'id.', MHG bisen w.v. 'id.', G biesen w.v. 'id.' (IE?) . Also cf. \*bison-.

'bug' ( < \*bel/on-?), but the origin of the latter is uncertain.

ON bitr adj. 'biting, sharp; bitter', Far. poet bitur adj. 'sharp, keen', Elfd. bitter adj. 'bitter; angry', OE bitor, bitter, bittor adj. 'bitter', E bitter, OS bittar adj. 'id.', Du. bitter adj. 'id.', OHG bittar adj. 'bitter, harsh, spicy', G bitter adj. 'bitter' (DRV). A ra-stem created to \*bftan- (q.v.). The o-grade of Go. baitrs adj. 'bitter' is remarkable, as ro-stems usually take the zero grade. It is substantiated, however, by ON -beitr in slfiJr-beitr adj. 'sharp as a razor'. Also cf. OE bite/a adj. 'biting' < \*bitala-.
ON bI1dr m. 'axe', MDu. bijl f./n. 'id.', Du. bijl c. 'id.', OHG bfhal n. 'id.', G Beil n. 'id.' (DRV). An instrumental noun derived from \*bftan- (q.v.).

'id.' (DRV). Derived from the strong verb \*bloan- 'to bloom', quasi PIE \*bh/h₁-t6-; as this proto-form would regularly develop into PGm. \*bulda-, the formation must have been created at a late stage, i.e. within Germanic itself. For an archaic meaning, cf. MDu. blad n. 'yield, profit, interest'.

ON blceja f. 'fine colored cloth; cover of a bed; burial sheet', OHG blaha f. 'coarse linen', G Blache, Blahe, Plane f. 'id.' (GM). ON blceja (the spelling blcegja is probably hypercorrect) presupposes a form \*blahjon-, which is corroborated by the North Frisian form bleix < OFri. \*blehhe (LOfstedt 1928-1931: 241). EDa. bla, pl. b/ar, Da. blar 'waste material when hemp or flax is processed' has been compared to G Blahe, as if continuing \*blahon-, but the variant Blache (with -ch- < -hh-) proves that the German word, too, must be reconstructed as \*blahjon- (with j-gemination but blocking of the umlaut before -h- as in lachen < \*hlahjan-). No further etymology: unrelated to Lat.floccus m.…

ON blakkr adj. 'black, dun-colored', Elfd. blokk adj. 'pale', Du. blank adj. 'white, transparent; inundated', OHG blanc adj. 'shining, white', G blank adj. 'shiny, sheer' (DRV). An adjective created to the poorly attested strong verb \*blinkan-, cf. Du. b/inken s.v. 'to shine', G Pal. blinken s./w.v. 'to blink; to shine', a secondary nasalized variant of \*b/fkan- (q.v.). Note that Elfd. blokk instead of \*\*blakk proves that the Nordic form continues \*blanka- rather than \*blakka-. The Germanic adjective spread to Romance, cf. VLat. b/ancus, Italian bianco, Spanish blanco, Fr. blanc, and from there into English as blank 'empty'. English must originally have had the native adjective, howeve…

Lat. flos,floris m. 'flower' < \*bh/ehros-; Olr. bl<ith, MW blawd m. 'flower' < \*bh/(e)h₃-tu-. A verb only attested in Germanic. The laryngeal of the underlying root has been identified as \*h₁ in view of Olr. blath and PGm. \*b/oman- (Schrijver 1991: 131). lndeed, PGm. \*bloman- is likely to contain an e-grade in view of the original mn-suffix. OE blci!d m.(?) 'flower, blossom, fruit' has contrarily been adduced to reconstruct a formation \*bledu- < \*bh/ehi-tri- with h₁, but it is more likely that this form represents a variant of the ti-stem bled m./f. 'id.' (for which see \*blodi-). It therefore seems safe to reconstruct the Germanic strong verb as \*bhfeh₁-e- rather than an o-graded \*…

Go. blop n. 'id.', ON b/60 n. 'id.', Elfd. bluoo n. 'id.', OE blocl n. 'id.', E blood, OFri. blod n. 'id.', MDu. bloet n. 'blood; blushing', Du. bloed n. 'id.', OHG bluot n. 'id.', G Blut n. 'id.' (DRV). A neuter collective created to either \*blean- 'to blow' or \*bloan- 'to flower'. The latter connection is based on the idea that the color of the blood served as the Benennungsmotiv (cf. MDu. b/oet n. 'blushing'), the former on the assumption of a semantic primitive 'to gush' (cf. EWAhd: II, 212). Alternatively, it is possible to start from a meaning 'life' derived from 'breath', cf. OHG bliit m. 'blowing', OE blci!d m. 'breath, spirit, life' < \*b/eda-.

ON b6k, pl. brekr f. 'id.', Far. b6k, pl. bekr f. 'id.', Elfd. buok, pl. byoker f. 'id.', OE Mc, pl. bee f. 'id.', E book, OFri. bOk f. 'id.' (DRY). Also cf. Go. boka f. 'letter', OHG buoh m./n./f. 'book, script, scripture, letter', Buch n. 'book', OS bok n. 'book, tablet', Du. boek n. 'book', OFri. bOk f./n. 'id.' < \*boka/o-. Related to \*bokOJo- 'beech' (q.v.); it is generally assumed that the first scriptures consisted of wooden tablets (cf. OS bOk), cf. Tacitus (Germania 10): Virgam frugiferae arboris decisam in surculos amputant, eosque notis quibusdam discretos super candidam vestem temere ac fortuite spargunt.

Gr. <pl]y6<;, Dor. <pay6<; f. 'oak', Lat. fii9us f. 'beech', Gaul. \*bii9os 'id.' (in French toponyms) < \*bhehfgLo-. An old IE tree name. Most Germanic forms straightforwardly point to PGm. \*bOk-. Ice!. beyki n. 'beech', a collective formation, has been interpreted as continuing \*baukja-, apparently with a -u- in its root, but it is more likely that beyki [peiklx] is an irregular continuant of ON \*breki (cf. breki-sk69r 'beech forest'), a form directly continued by Ice!. breki (cf. Magnusson 1989: 53). Du. beuk does not go back to a PGm. form with \*u either, but was borrowed from the eastern dialects that were affected by umlaut of PGm. \*o to o. Any direct connection to Ru. boz 'elder'…

MLG brack adj. 'id.', Du. brak adj. 'id.' (whence E brack, brackish) (GM). An adjective related to \*broka- 2 'swamp'. No further cognates. Olr. bren, MW braen adj. 'putrid, foul' < PCelt. \*bragno- does not belong here, but to the PIE root \*bhrh₁9- 'to smell' (see under \*brakkan-). The often compared Gr. Ȭpcxy6<; 'swamp' is a Hesychian gloss that in combination with the irregular variant Ȭpaxo<; m. 'shallow water' cannot possibly be derived from a PIE proto-form \*mrg-o-. This leaves us with an exclusively Germanic word.

MDu. bracke m. 'id.', Du. brak c. 'id.', OHG bracko m. 'id.', G Bracke m. 'id.' (Lw?) - Lat. fragro, -are 'to smell strongly' (derived from an adj. \*bhrh₁g-ro-?); Mir. bren adj. 'putrid, foul', MW braen, MoB brein adj. 'putrid, corrupt' < \*bhrh₁9-no-. No existing etymology: the word may be connected with PCelt. \*brokko-, cf. Olr. brace 'badger', an animal with a strong sense of smell. In view of the animal's strong odor, the word has been linked to MHG brrehen w.v. 'to smell', which when reconstructed as PGm. \*brekjan-, can be compared to Lat. fragro, -are 'to smell strongly' (derived from an adj. \*bhrh₁9-ro-?) and

Elfd. brakka f. 'id.', EDa. brakker pl. 'lower leg covers', OE braccas m.pl. 'breeches' ( < \*brakka-(?)) (GM?). The attestation in (East) Nordic is peculiar, however, and seems to imply a Germanic origin.
Go. brannjan w.v. 'id.', ON brenna w.v. 'id.', OE brernan w.v. 'id.', OHG brennen w.v. 'id.', G brennen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*brinnan- (q.v.).

OE breosa, briosa m., brimse f. 'id.', ME brese, brimse 'gadfly; locust', OS brimissa f. 'gadfly', MLG bromese f. 'id.', MDu. breemse, bremse f. 'gadfly; locust; beetle', Du. brems c. 'gadfly' (DRY). ON bresta s.v. 'id.', Far. bresta s.v. 'to break, collapse, strike', OE berstan s.v. 'to burst', E to burst, OFri. bersta s.v. 'to break; to disappear', OS brestan s.v. 'to burst, break', Du. barsten w.v. 'id.', OHG brestan s.v. 'to burst, tear; to lack', G bersten s.v. 'to crack'

ON bylgja f. 'wave', Far. bylgja f. 'id.', E billow, MLG bulge f. 'wave; hide', MHG bulge f. 'wave; leather bag' (ORV). A zero-grade formation (quasi PIE \*bh/gh-ieh₂-) to \*belgan- 'to swell' and \*balgi- 'skin bag'. The word was taken over by the Romans, cf. Lat bulga 'leather knapsack', and it seems likely that the continuant OFr. boulge, bouge was the source of ME bulge 'leather bag, wallet; hump', E bulge. As the meaning 'protuberance' is reminiscent of \*belgan-, however, it is possible that bulge at least partly continues a native word, i.e. OE \*bylge. E billow is usually taken to be a Norse loanword. Further cf. MDu. bu/gen w.v. 'to swell up' < \*bulgjan- and the derived bulge m. 'b…

Go. ga-deps f. 'id.', ON d<io f. 'id.', Far. d<io f. 'id.', Elfd. duo f. 'id.', OE diiJd f. 'id.', E deed, OFri. dede f. 'id.', WFri. died c. 'id.', OS dad f. 'id.', Du. daad c. 'id.', MHG tat f. 'deed, action, thing'
OE de/fan s.v. 'id.', OS bi-de/ban s.v. 'to bury', Du. delven s.v. 'to mine', OHG bi-telban s.v. 'to bury', MHG telben, delben s.v. 'to dig'

ON dcell adj. 'gentle, easy to deal with', Nw. dial. dee! adj. 'easy-going, good to talk to', Elfd. dii.l adj. 'trustworthy' (GM). A vṛddhi-gerundive without any etymology. The connection with OCS delo n. 'work' < \*dheh₁-lo- and the root \*dhehr 'to put, do' in general (cf. Torp 1909: 198) cannot be maintained. The Germanic material rather points to an unattested strong verb \*de/an- < \*dhe/-e-.

OE dile, dyle m. 'id.', E dill, OS di/Ii m. 'id.', Du. dille c. 'id.', OHG tilli m. 'id.', MHG tille, tiille m. 'id.' (GM). The material offers evidence for both an i-stem (OE dile) and a ja-stem (OS dilli, OHG till1l Perhaps the forms with rounded vowels (OE dyle, MHG tiille) can be adduced to reconstruct an additional ablauting pair \*duli- Ŝ \*dulja-. If so, the original paradigm probably had ablaut of the root, viz. nom. \*deliz, gen. \*duljaz < \*dhef-i-s, \*dhf-i-6s.
OE delu f. 'id.', OHG ti/a f. 'id.'
OHG tenar m., tenara f. 'id.', MHG tener m. 'id.'
ON djarfr adj. 'bold, daring', OE deorf adj. 'valiant', OS derl'Ji adj. 'powerful'

OE deorfan s.v. 'to labor, perish', MLG vor-derven s.v. 'to perish', MDu. be-derven s.v. 'to be damaged; to perish', Du. be-derven w.v. 'to spoil; to go bad', MHG ver-derben, -terben s.v. 'to be killed, to die'
OE deorc adj. 'id.', E dark
MHG diehter, tiehter n. 'id.', G Bav. Tichter n. 'id.'

Go. diups adj. 'id.', ON djupr adj. 'id.', Far. djupur adj. 'id.', Elfd. diuop adj. 'id.', OE deop adj. 'id.', E deep, OFri. diiip adj. 'id.', OS diop· adj. 'id.', Du. diep adj. 'id.', OHG tiuf adj. 'id.', G tie/ adj. 'id.'

grammatical_label: - \*hwitta- 'white'. Note that the PIE root \*dheubh- occurs in metathesized form in \*budman-
first_attestations: \*buttman- 'bottom' (q.v.).

ON dyrr adj. 'id.', Far. dyrur adj. 'id.', OE dfere, deore adj. 'id.', E dear, OFri. diure adj. 'id.', OS diuri adj. 'id.', Du. duur adj. 'id.', OHG tiuri adj. 'id.', G teuer adj. 'id.' (GM). No accepted etymology. The adjective looks like a vṛddhi-gerundive "mournable" to MHG be-tiiren w.v. 'to cost, have difficulties with', G be-dauern w.v. 'to regret, be sorry', ME douren 'to grieve, suffer' < \*duren-. It has been assumed that this verb was derived from an unattested PWGm. adjective \*dura-, a hypothetical loanword from Lat durus adj. 'tough, hard'. It is possible too, however, that the verb is etymologically identical to OHG truren, MHG truren, G trauern w.v. 'to mourn, be sad' < \*drur…

Go. dius n. 'id.', ON dyr n. 'id.', Far. djor n. 'id.', Elfd. diuor n. 'id.', OE deor n. 'id.', E deer, OFri. diiir, dier n. 'id.', OS dior n. 'id.', ODu. dier n. 'id.', Du. dier n. 'id.', OHG tior n. 'id.', G Tier n. 'id.' ì \*dheus-6- (EUR).
OSw. dfa s.v. 'id.', MHG tfen w.v. 'to suck, suckle' ì

Skt. dhciyati 'to suck, drink mother's milk', Oss. da!jyn / da!jun 'to suck' < \*dhh₁-eie-; Olr. denaid, W dynu 'id.' < \*dhh₁i-neh₂-. An i-present to the primary PIE root \*dheh₁- 'to suck, suckle', cf. Gr. &jaHm 'to suck', Latv. det 'id.', for which see \*dean-. In Germanic, the i-suffix is further found in e.g. \*dajjan- and Elfd. dise f. 'nipple' < \*dison- < \*dhh₁i-s-.

Go. digan s.v. 'id.' ì \*dheigh- (IE) - ToA tseka-, ToB tsik- 'to form', Skt. -degdhi 'to smear, anoint', YAv. pairi.daezaiiqn 3pl.caus.subj. 'to embank, construct', Arm. dizanem 'to pile up' < \*dheigh-; Lat Jingo, -ere 'to form, fashion', Oir. dingid 'to knead, form; suppress' < \*dhi-n-gh-e-. A strong verb that in view of the zero grade of Go. digands\* is likely to continue a PIE root present 3sg. \*dheigh-ti, 3pl. \*dhigh-enti (cf. uv2, s.v. \*dheigh-). See also \*daiga- and \*dika-.

ON digr adj. 'big, stout; thick; deep', Far. digur adj. 'thick, heavy, stout; rich (of milk)', Elfd. diger adj. 'thick, coarse', MDu. deger adv. 'completely', MHG tigere, tiger adv. 'id.' (DRV). An adjective apparently continuing \*dhiCglh-ro-. It may have been derived from \*dfgan- 'to knead' (q.v.), which would imply a more primary meaning 'well-formed'.

OE die m. 'dike, ditch', E dike, ditch, OFri. dik m. 'dike; road', OS dik m. 'pond', Du. dijk c. 'dam', MHG tich m. 'pond, swamp, channel', G Teich m. 'pond, pool' ì \*dheigh-n6- (IE) - Gr. Totxoc; m. 'wall', Arm. dez 'heap' < \*dh6igh-o-; SCr. zfd m. 'stone wall', OPru. seydis 'wall' < \*dheigh- (with metathesis). A formation continuing Pre-Gm. \*dfkka- < \*dheigh-n6- (with Kluge's law and geminate shortening in overlong syllables). Also cf. ON, Jee!., Far. dfki n. 'swamp, puddle', Elfd. daitje n. 'dike' < \*dikja-. Related to \*dlgan- (q.v.).

MLG diken w.v. 'to atone', MDu. diken s.v.(?) 'to try(?)', MHG tichen s./w.v. 'to get started; to try; to deal with; to undergo; to seep' ì \*dheig-e- (IE?) - To Gr. -lhyavw 'to touch, handle, injure, harm' < \*dhi-n-g-nH-e-? No certain etymology. Within Germanic, the verb appears to be closely related to the iterative \*dik(k)on-, cf. Ice!. dika 'to run', Far. dika 'to strike, hit, come at speed', Nw. dika 'to run, wander around; get moving', Sw. dial.
\*dimpandikka 'to run'. The meaning of especially the Faroese verb could point to an etymological connection with Gr. 1'hyavUl.

Nw. demba s.v. 'to smoke' (with unclear e), Elfd. dimba s.v. 'id.', MHG dimpfen s.v. 'to smoke, steam' ì \*dhembh-e- (IE). A strong verb continuing PIE \*dhembh-e-: the variant \*dimpan- is secondary, and was influenced by the iterative \*dumpon- (q.v.). The root \*dhembhprobably has a Schwebeablaut variant in \*nebala- < \*dhnebh-o/-eh₂-. See fur- · ther \*dampa- and \*dumbOn-.

ON dimmr adj. 'id.', OE dimm adj. 'id.', E dim, OFri. dim adj. 'id.' ì \*dhem(H)-n6- / \*dhembh-no- (IE? /WEUR?) - To Mlr. deime f. 'darkness' < \*dhem(H)-ieh₂-? An adjective with two possible etymologies. One option is to connect Mir. deime, and reconstruct \*dhem(H)-n6-. The alternative is to reconstruct the adjective as \*dimbna- < \*dhembh-no- (for which cf. \*dimban- - \*dimpan-), something that seems to be supported by OHG timber adj. 'dark' < \*dhembh-ro-. This adjective can also be derived from \*dhem(H)-ro-, however, as this form would result in PGm. \*timbra- as well by epenthesis. This would imply a link with Mir. deime after all.
OSw. diunga s.v. 'id.', ME dingen s.v. 'id.', MHG tinge/en w.v. 'to knock, hammer' ì \*dhengwh-e- (GM). A strong verb, probably of onomatopoetic origin. See also \*dangjan- and \*dank/gon-.

ON d!i1kkr / dpkkr adj. 'id.', Ice!. dfiJkkur adj. 'id.', Far. dfiJkkur adj. 'dull; dim, dark', Nw. dokk, dfiJkk adj. 'dark', OSw. dionker, diunker adj. 'id.', OFri. diunk adj. 'dark' ì \*dhengw·o- (IE) - Hitt. dankui- - dankuyai'black, dark' < \*dh(e/o)ngw·(o)i-. ON dfiJkkr may continue either \*dankwja- or \*dinkwa-. The latter option seems preferable in view of OSw. diunker and OFri. diunk, which both show (independent) u-infection of + before labiovelars. However, PGm. \*dankwja- should probably have given Ice!. \*dekkur (with e < ON fiJ, cf. Ice!. dekkja w.v. 'to make dark') rather than dokkur, a form directly pointing to ON dpkkr < \*dankwa- (Heidermanns 1993: 146). Did both forms occu…
ON dfs f. 'woman, girl; fairy, nymph; goddess', Far. dis f. 'goddess, nymph', Nw. dis f. 'goddess', OE ides f. 'woman', OS idis f. 'wife, woman', OHG itis f. 'woman' (GM).
Go. ga-dobs adj. 'id.', OE ge-defe adj. 'fit; mild' (DRV). A V[ddhi-adjective to the strong verb \*daban- ( q.v.).

Go. af-dobnan w.v. 'to become numb, hold one's peace' (DRV). An inchoative verb implying an unattested adjective \*doba-. This adjective appears to contain a vṛddhi-variant of the root also found in e.g. Ice!. dafna w.v. 'to grow weak; to become numb'. See further under \*dapra-.
ON dregr, dregn n. 'id.', Far. -dregur n. 'id.' (cf. javn-dregur 'equinox'), Nw. dFJger, dFJgn n. 'id.', Elfd. dyongen n. 'id.', Da. dFJgn n. 'id.', OE dogor, doger n. 'id.'
Go. af-dauips ptc. 'exhausted'
OFri. dok, dek m. 'id.', MDu. doec m./n. 'id.', Du. doek c./n. 'id.', OHG tuoh m./n. 'id.', G Tuch n. 'id.' (GM).

ON drel f. 'small valley', MDu. doele f. 'ditch', OHG tuolla f. 'pit, hollow' (EUR). A lengthened grade formation related to \*dala- 'valley' ( q.v.). With the same vowel, cf. ON drell m. 'valley-dweller' < \*doli-.
Go. doms m. 'insight, esteem', ON d6mr m. 'opinion, judgment, sentence', OE dom m. 'id.', E doom, OFri. dom m. 'id.', OS dom m. 'id.', Du. doem m. 'doom', OHG tuom m./n. 'judgement, power, situation'

OE don irr.v. 'id.', E to do, OFri. dwii, dwiin irr.v. 'id.', WFri. dwaan irr.v. 'id.', OS don irr.v. 'id.', Du. daen irr.v. 'id.', OHG tuan irr.v. 'id.', G tun irr.v. 'id.' ì \*dh6h₁-e- (IE). An athematic verb apparently continuing Pre-Gm. 3sg. \*dh6h₁-ti, 3pl. \*dhhi-enti. The a-grade of this verb is only found in Germanic, and may be parallel to that of e.g. \*gangan- - \*gungan- 'to go' (q.v.). In the other IE languages, we find a reduplicated present with e-grade of the root, cf. Skt. d<idhiiti 'to put, place, make, produce', Gr. Tl-BriμL 'to put, place', Lith. dJti (dedu) 'to lay, put' etc. < 3sg. \*dhi-dhehi-ti, 3pl. \*dhe-dhh₁-enti. See also \*dediand \*doma-.
Go. ga-draban s.v. 'to chip, hew out'
ON draf n. 'draff, husks', Far. drav n. 'draff; thin beer; rain', ODu. draf 'draff, Du. draf c./n. 'id.', OHG trebir n.pl. 'id.', .G Treber n.pl. 'id.'

ON draga s.v. 'id.', Far. draga w.v. 'id.', Elfd. draga s.v. 'id.', OE dragon s.v. 'id.', E to draw, OFri. drega s.v. 'id.', OS dragon s.v. 'to carry, bring', Du. dragen s.v. 'id.', OHG tragan s.v. 'id.', G tragen w.v. 'id.'
ON drattr m. 'pull; hesitation', Far. drattur m. 'pulling', ME draht 'id.', E draught, MDu. dracht f. 'carrying; dress', Du. dracht c. 'id.', MHG tracht f. 'id.', G Tracht f. 'id.'

ON dreif f. 'scattering', OE driiff. 'driving; flock, herd', E drove (DRV). An 6-stem derived from \*driban- 'to drive, be adrift' (q.v.). Also cf. ON dreifa w.v. 'to scatter, bedabble', which probably continues a causative \*draibjan- < \*dhroibh-eie-.
ON drangr m. '(detached) pillar of rock', Far. drangi, -ur m. 'high projecting rock (in the sea)', Nw. drange m. 'id.'

Go. dragkjan w.v. 'id.', ON drekkja w.v. 'id.', Elfd. drettja w.v. 'to drench; to ret', OE drencan w.v. 'to drench, make drunk', E to drench, OFri. drenza, drinza w.v. 'to drown', MDu. drenken w.v. 'to soak; to Jet drink', Du. drenken w.v. 'id.', OHG trenken w.v. 'id.', G triinken w.v. 'id.'
Nw. drabba w.v. 'to give a blow', OSw. drabba w.v. 'to hit, meet', ODa. drabe w.v. 'to kill'
ON draugr: m. 'id.', Far. poet. dreygur m. 'corpse; ghost, apparition'

\*drukknu- adj. 'dry' - OE dryge adj. 'id.', E dry, OFri. druch adj. 'id.', WFri. droech adj. 'id.', EFri. druuch adj. 'id.', NFri. druuge adj. 'id.', OS drukno, drokno adj. 'id.', MLG droge adj. 'dry, infertile', MDu. droeghe adj. 'id.', Du. droog adj. 'id.', OHG trockan, truckan adj. 'id.', G trocken adj. 'id.' (IE?) - Perhaps related to to Skt. druhi/a- adj. 'rough' < \*dhrugh-. An adjective whose PGm. reconstruction is problematic because of the co-occurrence of at least four different formations, i.e. 1) \*drogja- (OE dryge); 2) \*druga- (OFri. droch); 3) \*draugja- (MDu. droge); and 4) \*drukkna- (OS drocno, OHG trockan). The correlation between the different formations is unclear. The…
ON draumr m. 'id.', Far. dreymur adj. 'id.', OE dream m. 'id.', E dream, OFri. dram m. 'id.', OS dram m. 'id.', Du. droom c. 'id.', OHG troum m. 'id.', G Traum m. 'id.'
ON dreypa w.v. 'id.', OE driepan w.v. 'id.', OHG troufen w.v. 'id.', MHG troufen, troufen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to the secondary strong verb \*dreupan- \*driipan- ( q.v.).
MHG troren w.v. 'to drip, ooze, shed'

ON drcik f. 'id.', Nw. drdk, pl. drreker f. 'id.' (DRV?). A North Germanic 6-stem (the root noun inflection of Nw. drdk is secondary). It is unrelated to Skt dhr&ji-, dhrajf- f. 'draught of air, wind', which continues \*dhrog-i- with \*o rather than \*e. In all likelihood, \*dreko- was derived from \*dragan- 'to draw, pull' ( q.v.) in the same way that OFri. slek m. 'blow' < \*sleka- was created to \*slahan- 'to hit' (q.v.). The underlying form must then be reconstructed with a Pre-Gm. geminate: \*drekko-.
OS dreno m. 'id.', Du. Limb. drene c. 'id.', OHG treno m. 'id.', MHG trene, tren m. 'id.', G Swi. Jaun trena m. 'id.'

ON drepa s.v. 'to stike, beat, knock; to kill, slay', OE drepan s.v. 'to strike', MLG drepen s.v. 'to hit', MDu. drepen s./w.v. 'to hit, strike', OHG treffan s.v. 'to hit', G treffen s.v. 'to hit, touch' (DRV). A back-formation to the iterative \*drupp/bOn- 1, a zero-grade variant of \*drapp/bOn- (q.v.). As both iteratives were derived from the strong verb \*draban- 'to hew' < \*dhr6bh-e-, the strong verb \*drepan- cannot be used to

ON drjugr adj. 'substantial, lasting, ample', Far. drugvur adj. 'long-lasting, adequate', Elfd. driuog adj. 'laborious; disparaging', WFri. dreech adj. 'strong, enduring, long-lasting', NFri. F-A drech adj. 'enduring; stocky, fat'
MHG triegen s.v. 'to deceive, betray'
Skt. druhyati 'to deceit', Av. drufoiti 'to lie' < \*dhrugh-ie-; Skt dr6ha- m. 'insult, betrayal' < \*dhrough-o-. A strong verb with a clear IE etymology. See also \*drauga- and \*drauma-.
Go. driugan s.v. 'id.', OE dreogan s.v. 'to do, work; to endure, suffer', ME drlen s.v. 'to perform; to experience; to put up with, endure'

ON drjupa s.v. 'to drip, drop; to droop with the head', OE dreopan s.v. 'to drip', E to drip, OFri. driapa s.v. 'id.', OS driopan s.v. 'id.', Du. druipen s.v. 'to drip; to ooze', OHG triufan s.v. 'id.', G triefen s.v. 'id.' (DRY). A back-formation to the iterative \*drupp/bOn- 2 < \*dhrbh-neh₂-. There is no reason to postulate a PIE \*dhreub- (pace Pokorny IEW: 274-5) on the basis of the Germanic evidence, as both the radical \*u and the root-final \*p arose as a result of intra-Gm. derivational processes: in reality, the Pre-Gm. root was \*dhrebh-. This means that the often mentioned connection with Olr. drucht m. 'dew', allegedly from \*dhriJ.b-tu- (De Vries 1962: 84; Matasovic 2011: 12) c…
Go. driusan s.v. 'to fall down', Nw. drjosa s.v. 'id.', OE dreosan s.v. 'to rush, fall, perish', OS driosan s.v. 'to fall'

Go. dreiban s.v. 'id.', ON drifa s.v. 'to drift, drive like spray or snow; to crowd, throng, rush; to perform', Far. drfva s.v. 'to chase; to hit, strike; to press, force; to be carried or blow away', Elfd. draiva s.v. 'to drive; to fell; to pour, spray, flush', OE drifan s.v. 'to drive, force, pursue', E to drive, OFri. driva s.v. 'id.', OS drilian s.v. 'id.', Du. drijven s.v. 'to drive; to float', OHG triban s.v. 'to drive', G treiben s.v. 'to drive; to operate'

Nw. dribba w.v. 'to shove, push', E obs. to drib 'to lead one little by little into something', to dribble 'to keep (the ball) moving', Du. dribbelen w.v. 'to walk quickly (with small steps)', OHG tribOn w.v. 'to drive, push'

ON drift f. 'snowdrift', ME drift 'driving of cattle to pasture; drove; running; falling of rain or snow, snowdrift', MDu. drift f. 'fervor, urge; herd; district', EDu. drift c. 'urge, instinct', MHG trift f. 'pasture, herd', G Trift f. 'id.'

Go. drigkan s.v. 'id.', ON drekka s.v. 'id.', Far. drekka s.v. 'id.', Elfd. drikka s.v. 'id.', OE drincan s.v. 'id.', E to drink, OFri. drinka s.v. 'id.', OS drinkan s.v. 'id.', Du. drinken s.v. 'id.', OHG trinkan s.v. 'id.', G trinken s.v. 'id.'
ON drit n. 'id.', OE drit n. 'dirt', E dirt (DRV).

ON drfta s.v. 'to shit (esp. of birds)', Far. drfta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. draita s.v. 'id.', OE ge-dritan s.v. 'id.', MDu. driten s./w.v. 'id.' (NEUR?) - Perhaps to Ru. dial. dristat', SCr. drfskati, drftkati 'to have diarrhea' < \*dhreid-ske-(?). No good etymology. One usually compares the aforementioned Slavic forms, but the reconstruction of the root-final d is uncertain: are we dealing with an internally Germanic Reimbildun9 to \*skitan-? Another possibility is to connect Lith. trfedziu 'to have diarrhea' < \*treidh-ie- and tride f. 'diarrhea' < \*tridh-ieh₂- under the assumption of metathesis: if correct, the Germanic strong verb must have been back-formed to an iterative \*dritton- < \*dhr…

OE drof adj. 'turbid; troubled', OS drobi adj. 'cloudy, sad', Du. droef adj. 'id.', OHG truobi adj. 'turbid, dark, confused', MHG triiebe adj. 'dim, gloomy' (EUR). A u-stem (remodeled into a ja-stem in continental Germanic) related to \*drabiz- ( q.v.). Also cf. Go. drobjan 'to stir up', OE ge-drefan 'to stir up, make turbid; to trouble', OS gi-drobian 'to sadden', OHG truoben 'to dim, blur', G triiben 'id.' < \*drobjan- and Go. drobnan w.v. 'to become agitated'.

NFri. droch 'dream', OS gi-dro9 n. 'illusion', MDu. ge-droch n. 'deceit; apparition, ghost', OHG gi-tro9 n. 'id.', MHG ge-troc, troc m. 'id.' (ORV). Derived from \*dreu9an- ( q.v.). Formally and semantically close to Skt. druh- m. 'deceit, malice; demon' < \*dhru9h-.

Nw. dru99a w.v. 'to walk bowed down', ME dru99en, dro99en w.v. 'to labor, toil', E dial. to drug 'to pull forcibly' (ORV). The iterative to \*dra9an-, continuing quasi-PIE \*dhr9h-neh₂- with a secondary zero grade. Also cf. Icel. dro9i m. 'idler, lazy person' < \*dru9an- and with a different vowel - Nw. dra99a w.v. 'to trudge along' < \*dra99on-.

Go. drauhti-witop 'military service', ON dr6tt f. 'household, people; host of the king's men, bodyguard of a king', OE dryht f. 'people, multitude, army', OFri. drecht f.(?) 'wedding entourage', OS druht-folk 'army', MHG truht f. 'troop, squad, platoon', G truht f. 'troop, squad, platoon'

ON drykkr m. 'drink, drinking; draught', Far. drykkur m. 'drink', Elfd. drykk m. 'id.', OE drync m. 'drink; draught', E drink, MDu. drone m. 'drinking', Du. dronk c. 'id.', OHG trunk m. 'id.', G Trunk m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*drinkan- (q.v.).
Nw. drubba w.v. 'to give a blow', E dial. drub 'to thresh, beat, bang'
Nw. drubba w.v. 'to walk with a stoop, to fall over', MDu. druppen w.v. 'to sag, drip', Du. dial. drubben w.v. 'to hang one's head, be downcast'

OE driisian w.v. 'to become slow', E to drowse ҇ \*dhruHs-eh₁-(?) (NEUR) - Ru. dryxnut' 'to sleep' < \*druHs-ne-. A stative close to Ru. dryxnut'. Although the PSI. \*y may have been created secondarily to a PSI. root \*dn,s-, the Germanic verb seems to confirm the existence of a root \*dhruHs- with a laryngeal.
Du. dof adj. 'matt', MHG top adj. 'crazy'
ON dufa w.v. 'id.', Nw. duva w.v. 'to squat', OSw. diiva w.v. 'to squat, bob', OE diifan s.v. 'to dive', MLG be-diiven s.v. 'to become overwhelmed, become covered', MDu. be-duven s.v. 'to dip' (DRV).

MLG dove/ m. 'id.', Du. deuvel c. 'id.', MHG tilbel m. 'id.', G Diibel m. 'id.' (EUR) - Gr. Hsch. TU<pOL 'wedges' < \*dhubh-o-. Also cf. OHG gi-tubili n. 'peg, pin' < \*ga-dubilja-, MLG dovicke m. 'dowel-pin', Du. deuvik c. 'dowel-pin' < \*dubikan- and Nw. dial. dobbe m./f. 'fastening stick in a river', G Tyr. tuppe 'big piece of wood' < \*dubban-.

Go. hraiwa-dubo f. 'id.', ON dufa f. 'id.', Far. dugva f. 'id.', Elfd. dauva f. 'id.', E dove, OS duba f. 'id.', Du. duif c. 'id.', OHG tilba f. 'id.', G Taube f. 'id.' (DRV). A bird name probably derived from \*duban- 'to dive' (q.v.). Also cf. MHG tilber, G Tauber, MDu. duver, Du. doffer 'male dove' < \*dubazan-. For the suffix of the latter formation, see \*ganazan- 'gander' and \*katazan- 'tomcat'.

WFri. djerre, djirre c. 'id.', OS dodro m. 'id.', MLG dod(d]er, doder, dodder n.(?) 'id.', MDu. doder, dodre m./f. 'id.', Du. dooier c. 'id.', OHG totoro m. 'id.', G Dotter m. 'id.' (GM?). The West Germanic languages point to \*dudran- (whence also OE dydring m. 'yolk') alongside more restricted \*dudiran-, cf. WFri. djerre, MLG doder, dodder. The latter is the source of Ice!. doddur (cf. Magnusson 1989: 143). No certain etymology. It is unclear whether the word can be connected to Skt. dudhita- adj. 'dark' (Schindler 1967). ToB tute 'yellow' continues \*dhuh₁-to- (Adams 1999: 301) and therefore must be unrelated. See also \*dudra/on-.

The word appears in two different variants: WGm. \*dudra- meaning 'dodder', a parasitic plant, and Nordic \*dudron- 'camelina, false flax'. The word has no certain etymology, but may belong to Far. doddur m. 'tuft of hair, topknot, forelock', Du. dot c. 'tangle, tuft' < \*duddan-, which have been compared to Latv. duia f. 'tuft' < \*dhudh-ieh₂-(?) and Gr. fJuaavor; m. 'tassels, fringe' (but cf. Beekes 2010: 566-7). The comparison with \*dud(i)ran- 'egg yolk' (q.v.) is unconvincing in spite of the fact tha…

The Germanic reflexes of this verbal formation show a remarkable alternation between \*dwlnan-, \*swfnan-, \*pwlnanand perhaps also \*kwfnan-: apparently, the outcome of the initial cluster \*dhgwh_ was unstable in Germanic.

Civru can be reconstructed as \*enu < \*h₂inu for NWGm. \*cineu and as \*enu < \*h₂enu for Gothic inu (the latter with pretonic shortening, i.e. Dybo's law): apparently, the adverb was grammaticalized from an originally mobile u-stem, e.g. nom. \*h₂in-u, Joe. \*h₂n-eu (= Gr. Civw). Note that both proto-forms must be reconstructed with a lengthened grade (cf. Nikolaev 2007: 165) in order to explain the non-coloring by the larynge…

The form jerfr is irregular, but may be a contamination of jarfr with an unbroken form \*erfr, cf. Nw. dial. erv. Etymologically, the word has been compared to ON jarpr 'brown' < \*erpa- and to Gr. E:ptq:>oc; 'goat', but it is perhaps better to assume a link with Nw. dial.jarva w.v. 'to gnaw, chew'.

'hazel grouse' and Ice!. irpa f. 'brown mare' < \*erpjon-.

Nw. feim(e) m./n. 'thin layer of dew, dust, floating fat', MHG veim m. 'foam, dross' (IE) - Skt. phena- m. 'foam' < \*speh₃i-no-; Lat. spuma f. '(sea) foam' < \*speh₃i-m-eh₂-; OCS pena, Ru. pena, SCr. pjena f. 'foam' < \*peh₃i-no-; OPru. spoayno 'foam' < \*peh₃i-neh₂-; Lith. spaine f. 'foam

\*hr/pan- 'hoarfrost'). De Vaan 2008: 583 points to the problem that no actual mn-stem is attested, but Nw.feime m./f. < \*peh₃i-mon- can be adduced as such. Also note Nw. Jim 'layer of dust or dew' < \*fima-, which may continue the original zero-grade \*ph₃i-mon- (with laryngeal metathesis).

ON feitr adj. 'id.', Far. feitur adj. 'id.', Elfd. fiet adj. 'id.', MLG vet adj. 'id.', MHG veiz adj. 'id.', G Cimb. voaz adj. 'id.' (IE?). An adjective probably related to \*fajOJa- (q.v.) < \*poih₃-o-. The Germanic root-final t has no extra-Germanic parallel (Heidermanns 1993: 187), but can tentatively be identified as the zero grade of the root \*deh₃- 'to give', thus starting from a meaning 'to fatten up' ( < "to give to eat''). PGm. \*faitagave rise to a factitive \*faitjan- (ON feita, OHG feizzen 'to make fat'), whose ptc. \*faitida- was again lexicalized, cf. OE [ci!tt, E fat, (M)Du. vet, OHG[eizzit, G feist adj. 'fat'. See also \*fitnan-.

Du. dial. vei adj. 'fat, lush, fertile' ::) \*poih₂-o- (IE) - Skt payate 'to swell' < \*peiH-e-; Av. poWpa- 'fat' < \*peiH-tuo-. An adjective derived from the PIE root \*peiH- (cf. LIV2 s.v. \*peiH-). Probably related to \*faita- 'fat' (q.v.).

'wall, compartment', G Fach n. 'compartment, panel; discipline' ::) \*phi9-o- (IE) - Skt pdjas- n. 'side, surface(?)' < \*pehiB-os-; Gr. m']yvuμL 'to attach, join' < \*pehiB-neu-; Lat pango 'to insert firmly, fix' < \*phi9-neh₂- (with n-infixation through Thurneysen's law); Lat pti.gus m. 'district' < \*pehiB-o-. Also cf. MLG vake, MDu. vake, vaec, Du. vaak adv. 'often'.

'small, little, few', Lat. parvus adj. 'small' (with metathesis) < \*peh₂u-ro-; Lat. paucus adj. 'few, small in number' < \*peh₂u-llo-. The OHG formfoh has been adduced to reconstruct the Germanic form as \*fauha-, so as to equate it with Lat. paucus, but the h can be epenthetic in this form. This leaves \*fawa- as the only possible proto-form. It must be derived from a PIE zero-grade form \*p!J.2u-o-, with (regular) vocalization of the laryngeal, as the full-grade \*peh₂u-o- would have given \*\*Joa-.

Skt patati 'to fly; hit', Gr. nfroμm 'to fly', Lat peto, -ere 'to reach; to rush', MW eh-ed- 'to fly' and the intensive Gr. no-raoμm 'to fly around' < \*poth₂-eie-. Waigali puc, piic 'pine' < \*peul<-i-; Gr. nEuKTJ f. 'spruce' < \*peul<-eh₂-; OPru. peuse 'id.' < \*peul<-ieh₂-; Lith. pusis f. 'id.' < \*pul<-i-. A word with a largely European distribution. In view of Waigali put, piic, however, the word must also have existed in Iranian (Morgenstierne 1945: 229). From there, it probably spread into Armenian, where it surfaces as the second member of the compound t'ef-aws 'holm oak, evergreen oak; pine' (Olsen 2012). For the first element, see Arm. t'efi 'elm' under \*felwo-.

'id.' ::) \*plk-[eh₁)-ie- (GM). ON fylgja and OE fylg{e)an continue a formation \*fulgjan- that can be reconciled with OE fe[o)lan s.v. 'to proceed, go, reach' < \*felhan- < \*pelk-e-. Extra-Gm. *comparanda* are unreliable: Bret. heul 'to follow' probably continues \*se/gh- rather than \*pe/gh- and the connection with CS p!'bzati, Ru. polzat', SCr. piizati 'to crawl' must be abandoned in view of the accent pointing to \*plg-eh₂- rather than \*plgh-ehi-.

Go. fulls adj. 'id.', ON fullr adj. 'id.', Far. fullr adj. 'id.', Elfd. full adj. 'id.', OE full adj. 'id.', E full, OS full adj. 'id.', Du. vol adj. 'id.', OHG fol adj. 'id.', G vol/ adj. 'id.' ::) \*plhi-no- (IE) - Skt. purria-, Av. parana-, Olr. lan 'id.', Lith. pilnas, OCS p!'bn'b adj. 'filled, full' < \*plhi-no-. An adjective continuing the original past participle of the verbal root
\*fulu-.

ON full n. 'goblet', OE full n. 'cup' ::) \*pl-n6- (IE) - Gr. ntla f. 'milk pail; drinking bowl, goblet' < \*pel-ih₂- and Gr. nelll<;, neA.t<; 'basin' < \*pel-i-s, gen. \*pe/-i-6s; OCS po/'b 'scoop' < \*pol-a-. An Indo-European word for a certain vessel. Unrelated to \*fulla- 1.

ME folm f. 'id.', OS folmos f.pl. 'both hands', OHG fol ma f. 'palm' ::) \*plh₂-m-eh₂- (IE) - Gr. mv.aμT) f. 'palm of the hand' ( = Lat. pa/ma) < \*plh₂-em-eh₂-; Olr. ltim f. 'hand' < \*plh₂-m-ehi-. An old m-stem. Within Germanic, it is possible to connect ON fa/ma w.v. 'to grope' < \*falmojan-, a verb possibly derived from a lost mo-stem \*falmaz < \*polh₂-mo-.

OE fundian w.v. 'to try to find, to strive, intend', OS fundon w.v. 'to strive, proceed' ::) \*pnt-neh₂-(?) (DRV?). The iterative to \*ftnpan- (q.v.). PGm. \*fandon- can be an o-grade variant of the same iterative.

OE gredrian w.v. 'id.', OFri. gaduria w.v. 'id.', MDu. gaderen w.v. 'id.', Du. ver-gaderen 'id.' (ORV). A verb derived from an adverb \*gadur, cf. OE to gredere, OFri. gader, gadur adv. 'together'. It is possible that the derivational base consisted of the root \*gad- (see \*gadojan-) and the locative suffix \*-r as seen in \*hwar 'where' < \*kwo-r and \*par 'there' < \*to-r. Lith. ieflgti (iengiu) 'to stride' < \*ghengh-ie-. The verb was originally suppletive in Germanic, cf. the Gothic preterite iddja < \*ejjo- (also OE eode < \*ejj-Od- with secondary weak ending), but in NWGm. we find a reduplicated preterite \*geanga - \*geangume, cf. ON gekk - gengum, OHG gieng, giengum. OFri. gungan is probably to be analyzed as a zero-grade form \*gungan- (cf. recently Mottausch 1996), although the u-vocalism has also been explained by internal Frisian developments (cf. Seebold 1970: 213). From the PIE perspective, however, it is likely that the Gm. strong verbs with a-vocalism evolved from the class of athematic verbs corresponding to the Hittite bi-verbs with similar o/£…

'to watch, tend, take care of must be reconstructed as \*ganhatjan- rather than \*gawatjan- in view of the nasal vowel of Elfd. gf;ta. It follows that the apparently ob…

Go. fram-gahts f. 'progress', ON g<itt f. 'rabbet of a doorpost; doorway', Far. gatt f. 'threshold; doorway', Elfd. gqtt f. 'doorpost' (ORV). A ti-stem to \*gangan- - \*gungan-. The nasality of the Proto-Germanic vowel is still attested in Elfd. gqtt. Also cf. OSw. dura-gcete n. 'doorframe' <

OHG ganz adj. 'id.', G ganz adj. 'id.' (GM/DRV?). A High German word that is assumed to have spread to Frisian, Dutch and Low German, cf. OFri. gans, MDu. gansch, Du. gans, MLG gans, ganz adj. 'entire' (hence Da., Nw. ganske, Sw. ganska adv. 'quite'). Etymology unclear. Not to OLith. gandiiaus comp. 'rather, better', Lith. ne-gandas adj. 'dissatisfied' as proposed by Heidermanns 1993: 232: these nominal formations were coined to gand&i 'to be satisifed', a late derivation from gana 'enough'. Not related either to Gr. xavl>ov 'with opened mouth' (pace Schmidt 1913/14). There is a factitive OHG genzen w.v. 'to safeguard', G er-gii.nzen 'to add, complement' < \*gantjan-, and in view of the mean…

OE ganot m. 'id.', MDu. gent m. 'id.', Du. jan-van-gent 'gannet', OHG ganzo, ganazzo m. 'gander', MHG ganze m. 'id.' (IE). A formation derived from the root of \*gans- 'goose' (q.v.) < PIE ghh₂en-sby adding the animal suffix \*-uta- (for which cf. \*heruta- 'deer') or the variant \*-ita- as in MDu. gent. In addition, G Rhnl., Lorr. Gunz m. 'gander' points to a variant \*gunuta- with zero grade of the root It may preserve the oblique stem of an originally ablauting paradigm \*gans, gen. \*gunzaz < \*ghh₂en-s, \*ghh₂n-s-6s.

ON gabba w.v. 'to mock, make game of one', OE gabbian w.v. 'to mock; to chat', OFri. gabbia w.v. 'to denounce, accuse, prosecute', MLG gabben, gabberen w.v. 'to joke, monkey about', MDu. gabben w.v. 'to joke', Du.ginne-gappen 'id.' (GM). An iterative formation 3sg. \*gappopi-, 3pl. \*gabunanpi < \*ghHbh-neh₂-, no doubt related to \*gapon- 'to gape', cf. ON gapa, OE gapian, Du. gapen, (M)HG gaff en, cf. for the shift of meaning: Nw. dial. gapa w.v. 'to gape; gaze; insult'. Lith. gabli6ti and Pol. gabac 'to vex' were borrowed from Low German.

Go. gards m. 'house, household enclosure, courtyard', ON garor m. 'fence, wall; yard; courtyard, court', Far. garour m. 'fence; enclosure; farm; ring; gust of wind creating ripples in the water', OE geard m. 'id.', OS gard m. 'field, ground, house', OHG gart m. 'enclosure'
ON g9rn f. 'id.'
Far. gorpa w.v. 'to cough violently; to vomit', Nw. garpa, garva, gurpa w.v. 'to talk loudly; belch; gormandize'

ON gprr adj. 'id.', OE gearo adj. 'id.', OS garu adj. 'id.', Du. gaar adj. 'id.', OHG garo adj. 'id.', G gar adj. 'id.' (IE). Probably a prefixed variant of \*arwa-, i.e. \*ga-arwa-, for which see \*garwjan-.

ON g¢r(v)a, gera w.v. 'to prepare; do', Far. gera w.v. 'to do', Elfd. djiira w.v. 'id.', OE gierwan w.v. 'to prepare, put on, clothe, adorn', E to gear, OS garwian, gerwian w.v. 'to provide, prepare, dress', OHG garawen w.v. 'to prepare, equip, decorate', G gerben w.v. 'to tan' (DRV). A factitive verb derived from \*garwa- 'ready, quick' (q.v.). For a variant without the prefix \*ga-, cf. G Wais. arwen 'to toil' (Schott 1842: 266) < \*arwojan-? Also cf. OS gi-garwi, gi-gerwi n. 'clothing, official dress' < \*garwja-.
Go. gasts m. 'id.', ON gestr m. 'id.', Far. gestur m. 'id.', Elfd. djiist m. 'id.', OE giest m. 'id.', OFri. jest m. 'id.', OS gast m. 'id.', Du. gast c. 'id.', OHG gast m. 'id.', G Gast m. 'id.'

Go. gatwo f. 'street, main road', ON gata f. 'path, way, road', MLG gate f. 'alley, street', OHG gazza f. 'id.', G Gasse f. 'id.'

MDu. gadem m./n. 'id.', OHG gadum, gadem n. 'id.', MHG9adem, 9aden n. 'cabin, barn' (ORV?). Of unknown origin. It is possible that the formation must be analyzed as \*ga-pmo- < \*ghH-tmo- (EWAhd: IV, 6-7), but the proposed link with \*ghehr 'to go' (see \*gen-) is tenuous. It seems more attractive to assume that the WGm. form \*gapma- developed from \*gadma- by Bahder's law, i.e. the devoicing of voiced fricatives before resonants. If that is the case, the link with \*gadojan- 'to suit' (Torp 1909: 123) can theoretically be maintained in view of the parallel derivation of G Ge-mach, Du. ge-mak n. 'bower, chamber' from \*makojan- 'to match' (see \*maka-).

Go. gawi n. 'id.', OE -ge n. 'id.', OFri. gii., ge n. 'village, parish', MLG go n. 'county', MDu. gou(w), gooy n. 'id.', MHG gou, gen. gouwes n. 'id.', G Gau n. 'id.' (EUR?). Etymology uncertain. The early 19th century comparison with Arm. gawat 'district' (cf. Seebold 196 7: 128ff.) is doubtful in view of Oss. qa?w / ga?w 'village'. The link with Gr. o'(TJ f. 'village' < \*Hou-ieh₂- can theoretically be maintained by postulating a prefixed formation \*ga-auja- < \*Hou-io-, but nevertheless remains uncertain (Beekes 2010: 1055). Lehmann 1986: 153 assumes a link with \*aujo- 'wetland' in view of the frequent combination of the word with river names in toponyms.
MDu. goyen w.v. 'id.', Du. gooien w.v. 'to throw'

ON gaukr m. 'id.', OE geac m. 'id.', MDu. gooc m. 'cuckoo; fool', OHG gouh m. 'id.', MHG gouch m. 'id.' (GM) - Close to Lith. geguze f. 'cuckoo' < \*9he-9hugh-ieh₂-. An old onomatopoetic word. Also cf. Cimb. kucko m. 'id.'.

ON gaupn f. 'both hands held together', OHG gaufana f. 'hollow of the hand', MHG goufe f. 'id.' (NEUR?). In view of e.g. OE geap adj. 'crooked, bent, curved' < \*gaupa-, the word must have been derived from the strong verb underlying OE geopan 'to take up, swallow'. It follows that any direct comparison with Lith. ziupsnis 'hollow of the hand' (thus Pokorny IEW: 449) is erroneous.

ON gaupa f. 'id.', Far. geypa f. 'id.', Nw. gaupe f. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Nordic word with no certain etymology. It seems attractive to me, however, to connect the word to Swi. Rhntl. g6ppa 'to play (of cats)' < \*gaubb-. Unrelated to \*geupan- 'to be hollow' (q.v.).

Hitt. epzi, appanzi 'to take, seize' < \*h₁ep-ti, \*h₁p-enti; Skt. iipn6ti 'to reach, gain, take possession of < \*h₁e-h₁p-neu-; Lat. aplscor 'to seize, obtain' < \*h₁p-i-ske-; Alb. jap,jep 'to give' < \*h₁op-eie-. The verb is usually compared to Olr. gaibid 'to take, hold' < \*ghHbh-ie- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 407-9), but this is formally impossible because of the laryngeal. LIV2: 193 connects Lith. gebJti (gebU) 'to be able; to be used to' < \*ghebh-eh₁-ie-, but the semantics are problematic. An alternative etymology was offered by Kortlandt 1992: 104-5, who assumed a remodeling of \*ga-ef < \*lfo(m)-h₁ep-, thus establishing a direct connection with Lat. coepl 'seized' < \*lfo(m)-h₁eh₁p-. OE geopan s.v. 'to take up, swallow' (NEUR?) - Lith .gailbti 'to cover; vault' < \*ghoubh-. No clear etymology. If the frequentative E to gobble is related, it is likely that the strong verb continues Pre-Gm. \*geuppan- with a root-final geminate. The corresponding iterative 3sg. \*guppo/Ji, 3pl. \*gubunan/Ji would at any rate point to a formation \*ghubh-neh₂- with root-final \*bh. It therefore seems more likely that \*habran- was derived from \*hafra- 'goat' (q.v.), as is substantiated by the double meaning of Far. havur 'goat; unthreshed corn'. The doublet Cimb. habaro, havaro in fact suggests that the original proto-form was \*habran-.

Lat. c6nfve6 'to be tightly closed, close (of the eye)' < \*kom-kneigwh.e-(?). An etymologically obscure verb with Lat. c6nfve6 as the only formally possible, but semantically remote cognate. The vacillation between \*hnfwan- and \*hnfgan- is due to influence from the causative \*hnaigjan- _ \*hnaiwjan-, where the labialization was regularly lost (between \*o and \*i/j, cf. \*sagja- 1 'man, hero' < \*sokwH-i6-). In the strong verb, on the other hand, the change \*gwh > \*w is expected, cf. \*snfwan- 'to snow' < \*sneigwh.e-.

Gr. -KVaiw 'to scrape, scratch', Olr. imm-cna, W cnoi 'to bite, chew', Lith. kn6ti (kn6ja) 'to peel, tear' < \*knehrie-. In many cases, OHG verbs in -en ( < \*-jan-) continue older verba pura, which theoretically allows us to reconstruct a strong verb \*hn6an- < \*kn6h₂-e-. The late OHG giniiiter marchstein 'carved landmark', however, confirm that the PGm. verb really was \*hn6jan-, thus agreeing to the formation \*kneh₂-ie- as supported by the other IE languages. Also cf. OHG nuo(w)il m. 'plane' < \*hn6ila-, OS hn6a 'joint, seam', OHG nuo, hnuo f. 'joint, seam' < \*hn66- and G Nut f. 'groove, notch' < \*hn6d6-.

An old iterative that gave rise to the strong verb \*hneupan- (q.v.). In view of Nw. dial. nap pa w.v. 'to pluck' < \*hnapp6n- (also cf. E nap 'woolly material removed from the surface of cloth'), I assume that the Germanic root \*hnupp/b- has a secondary zero grade. As a consequence, the Pre-Gm. root…

'to knock on someones head with the nuckles' and Du. okker-noot 'knock on the top of the head with the knuckles' (the origin of the first element is unknown, but seems to have been inspired by okker-noot 'walnut').

'ram' < \*hriita- (for the semantics, cf. Elfd. riuota, E to rout 'to low, moo'). It follows that the alleged link of the latter word with \*heruta- 'deer' must be abandoned.

OE hropan s.v. 'id.', OFri. (h)ropa s.v. 'id.', OS hropan s.v. 'id.', Du. roepen s.v. 'id.', OHG ruofan, hruofan s.v. 'id.', MHG ruofen s.v. 'id.', G rufen s./w.v. 'id.' (GM). No extra-Gm. cognates. The deverbal formations E rudder and ruddle 'sieve' < \*hrudra-, \*hrudla- are derivationally parallel to OE hridder, hridde/, E riddle < \*hridra- (q.v.).

Lith. kurkulai m.pl. 'frogspawn', Latv. kurku/is m. 'id.' < \*kr(H)k-u/o-; Ru. krjak (= PSI. \*kn;kb) 'id.', Slov. krݮk 'id.' < \*krek-o-; SCr. krtik m. 'id.' < \*kor(h₁)k-o-(?). An n-stem thematicized as \*hrugna- in Nordic. On the basis of the Balto-Slavic forms, it is theoretically possible to reconstruct a root alternation \*krek- / \*kreh₁k-, \*kr(h₁)k-. The Germanic stem \*hrugan- should then be analogical for regular \*hurgan- < \*kr(h₁)k-6n-. In this scenario, it is further possible to compare Ice!. hrai < \*hrehan- (or \*hrahan-) 'swarm of water-fleas', a formation potentially corresponding to PSlav. \*krek-. OE gyccan w.v. 'id.', E to itch, OS Juk{k)ian w.v. 'id.', Du.Jeuken w.v. 'id.', OHGJucken w.v. 'id.', GJucken w.v. 'id.' (GM?). A WGm. Jan-verb 3sg. \*JukJepi, 3pl. \*JukJanpi. G Als., Pal.Jucken 'to jerk'…

In Greek, first of all, initial labiovelars usually lose their labialization in words with labials (cf. Kanv6c; 'smoke' < \*kwh₂p-no-). This means that the 8 of 8£.A.cpuc…

Go. kalds adj. 'id.', ON kaldr adj. 'id.', Far. kaldur adj. 'id.', Elfd. ko/d adj. 'id.', OE cea/d adj. 'id.', E cold, OS kald adj. 'id.', Du. koud adj. 'id.', OHG kalt adj. 'id.', G kalt adj. 'id.' (DRY). The original past participle of the strong verb \*kalan- (q.v.). The Germanic word is unrelated yet suspiciously similar to OCS xlad'b 'coolness, cool breeze', Ru. x6/od 'cold'. Further cf. ON, Far. kuldi, Nw., Da. kulde 'cold' < \*kuldan-.
G Swi. xa/len w.v. 'to freeze, stiffen' (DRY). The iterative to the strong verb \*kalan- (q.v.).

Skt jambha- m. 'tooth', du. 'jaws'; ToA kam, ToB keme\* m. 'tooth', Gr. y6μcpo<; m. 'peg, bolt, nail', Lith. iambas m. 'sharp edge', Latv. zuobs m. 'tooth', OCS zpbo, Ru. zub (gen. zuba), SCr. zub m. 'tooth', Alb. dhemb / dham(b) m. 'id.'. See also \*kimbon- and \*kambjan-.

ON kemba w.v. 'to comb; to card', Far. kemba w.v. 'to comb', Elfd. kemba w.v. 'id.', OE cemban w.v. 'id.', OS kembian w.v. 'id.', OHG kemben w.v. 'id.', G kiimmen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The factitive to \*kamba- (q.v.).

ON katr adj. 'id.', Far. katur adj. 'id.', Elfd. kqt adj. 'lively' (GM). This adjective has been reconstructed as \*kawata- (cf. Torp 1909: 33; Magnusson 1989: 450), so as to compare it to e.g. Lat. gaudio, -ere 'to gladden', but Elfd. kqt (cf. ON kceta w.v. 'to gladden', Elfd. kflta 'to exercise' < \*kanhtjan-) with its nasal vowel requires a reconstruction \*kanhta- (cf. Heidermanns 1993: 33). The underlying root \*kanha- 'glad' < \*gonko- is in Verner alternation with Sw. dial. kang n. 'agitation' (also cf. ON kangin-yroi n.pl. 'jeering words') < \*kanga- < \*gonk6- and ME cang adj. 'foolish, lewd' < \*kanga-. The latter may be an Old Norse loanword, however.

OE cennan w.v. 'to beget, conceive, create, bring forth', ME kennen w.v. 'id.' u \*gonhi-eie- (DRV). A causative created to the PIE root \*genh₁-. ON kenna w.v. ON kanna w.v. 'to search, find out', Far. kanna w.v. 'to inspect' (DRY). An inchoative, i.e. medial factitive formation to \*kunnan-, quasi PGm. \*kann-non- < IE \*gonh₃-nh₂- + -neh₂-.

ON kanna f. 'id.', Far. kanna f. 'id.', Elfd. kanna f. 'id.', OE canne f. 'can, cup', E can, OFri. kanne f. 'id.', MDu. canne f. 'jug', Du. kan c. 'id.', OHG kanna, kanta f. 'id.', G Kanne f. Another possibility is to compare the word to Ice!. kani m. 'bowl; prow', kanni m. 'prow', MLG kane, G Kahn m. 'boat' < \*kan(n)an- and Far. kanna f. 'point of a boat's bow' < \*kanon-. E Scot. cantle 'crown of the head; fleshy part behind the ear in a ram's head', MDu. cant m. 'edge, region', Du. kant c. 'side; edge', G Kanz m. 'mane, upper part of the neck' u \*gond-o- (NEUR) - Lith. zandas m. 'cheek, jaw', Latv. zuods m. 'chin, jaw'. The late attestation of this word has led to the assumption that it was borrowed from Romance, cf. Fr. dial. cant, It., Sp. canto. In view of the semantically similar G Kanz and Scot. cantle, however, it seems plausible that we are dealing with a Germanic word meaning 'side (of the head?)' or 'cheek'. PGm. \*kanta- can then be connected to Lith. zandas < \*gond-o-, whose root looks like a variant of \*gen(H)- as found in \*kin nu- 'cheek, ch…

OE capian w.v. 'to turn', MDu. capen w.v. 'to watch, stare at', OHG kapfen w.v. 'to stare', MHG kafen, kapfen w.v. 'to stare, be in awe' u \*gobh-neh₂- (EUR) - Ru. zab6ta f. It probably served as the basis for the stative ON k6pa 'to watch' < \*kopen- and the causative(?) OE kepan 'to watch, keep', WFri. kypje w.v. 'to look' < \*kopjan-. Also cf. OE ge-cop adj. 'fit' < \*ga-kopa- (Heidermanns 1993: 340-1).

Go. kara f. 'care, worry, concern', ON kpr f. 'bed of sickness', OE cearu f. 'suffering, sorrow; care, caution', E care, OS kara f. 'sorrow, lament', OHG kara f. 'sorrow, sadness, repentance' ::} \*9b2r-eh₂- or

MP zryg, zlyk 'sorrow, suffering', Oss. zaryn / zarun 'to sing' < \*geh₂r-; Gr. yfjpuc; f. 'voice, speech' < \*geh₂r-u-; Lat garrio, -ire 'to chatter', Olr. gairid, W garddu 'to call, cry' < \*g(e}h₂r-ie-; Olr. g<iir f. 'cry', W gawr 'crying, battle' < \*geh₂r-i-. PGm. \*karo- can have developed from both \*9b2r-eh₂- with vocalization of the laryngeal and from \*geh₂r-eh₂- or \*goh₂r-eh₂- with pretonic shortening. Also cf. Go. karon, OE cearian, OS karon, OHG karon w.v. 'to worry' < \*karojan- and OE cearig, OS -karag, OHG karag 'sad', G karg, Du. karig 'sparse' < \*karaga-.

ON karskr adj. 'brisk, bold', MLG karsch adj. 'vivid', Du. kers-vers adj. 'fresh', MHG karsch adj. 'vivid' u \*hlfJor-sko-(?) (IE?). No certain etymology. Possibly related to Skt j<irate 'to wake up' < \*hi9er-e-; Gr. fydpw 'to awaken, rouse, raise' < \*hi9er-ie-.

MDu. catere m. 'id.', Du. kater c. 'id.', OHG katere m. 'id.', G Kater m. 'id.' (NIE). A formation that is taken to be derived from \*katton- 'cat' with the masculinizing azan-suffix, for which cf. \*ganazan- 'gander'. The Gottingen dialect variant kiiz, pl. koze (Schambach 1858: 98) would then continue a non-Vernerized form \*katasan- or \*kadasan-. The single t of the implied root \*kat- still remains remarkable, as the derivational base \*katton- has a geminate. Perhaps, this alternation points to an old n-stem with a singulate/geminate alternation, nom. \*kato, gen. \*kattaz. Late OHG katere looks like a variant stem \*kadazan-, but is in spite of Cimb. kattaro usually regarded as a loan…

Nw. katte f. 'cat', OFri. katte f. 'id.', MDu. catte f. 'cat', Du. kat c. 'cat', OHG kazza f. 'cat', G Katze f. 'cat' (NIE). No Indo-European etymology. The word is usually believed to stem from an Afro-Asiatic language, cf. Arab. qitta, Nub. kadis 'cat', penetrating the European languages through Lat catta. The internally Germanic alternation of \*katton- with Nordic \*kattu-, cf. ON k9ttr, Far. kf:1ttur, kattur (whence \*kattjon- in ON, Far. ketta, Nw. kjette f. 'cat') nevertheless suggests that there must have been a root \*gat- present in Germanic before the great sound shifts, i.e. well before the Roman era. The evidence, at any rate, clearly points to an old n-stem \*kato, gen.sg. \*ka…
OE cfegan w.v. 'id.', OHG gi-kewen w.v. 'id.'

Ice!. kaun n.pl. 'id.', Far. keym(i}, kj6m 'id.' (GM?). The forms occur in the contexts Ice!. ao blcisa i kaun, Far. at blcisa f keym, kj6m 'to blow into one's folded hands (to warm them)'. It is likely that we are dealing with a Germanic cognate of Av. gauua- 'hand' < \*geu-o(Magnusson 1989: 451), but the original form of the word is difficult to reconstruct. Icel. kaun and Far. keym can perhaps be unified into an Old Norse proto-form \*kaum(r}, but Far. kj6m at least superficially points to \*kj6mr. Given these difficulties, it seems futile to attempt to project the forms further back into Proto-Germanic. They may, for instance, be more recent formations to ON kjci 'to rub(?)', Icel. kja '…
ON kaun n. 'sore', Icel. kaun n. 'boil, carbuncle', Nw. kaun m. 'blister' (GM). Also cf. Far. koyna, Nw. ktiJyne f. 'blister' < \*kaunon-. No further etymology.
MDu. coon f. 'jaw (of a fish), gill', Du. koon c. 'cheekbone, jaw, gill'

OFri. kate f. 'id.', MDu. cote f. 'hoof, fetlock', Du. kootje dim. 'phalanx', G Rhnl. kotche dim. 'dice made of sheep's knuckles' (GM). No certain etymology. The word has been compared to Sw. dial. kut m. 'hunch, hump', which, however, belongs to the unrelated etymological cluster of Sw. dial. kuta (ryggen), Nw. dial. kuuta ryggjn 'to bend one's back', Nw. dial. kut m. 'stuntedness, dwarfism' < \*kuta-, Sw. dial. kot m. 'gnarl' etc. The link with MDu. cote/ f. 'animal dropping', Du. keutel c. 'id.' < \*kuti/6- (cf. Pokorny JEW: 393-8) is not very appealing either.

OE ceo f. 'id.', E chough, OS ka f. 'id.', MDu. cauwe f. 'id.', Du. kauw c. 'id.', OHG kaha, ka f. 'id.' (DRY). A West Germanic bird name, possibly related to \*kewon- and \*kaujan- 'to cry'. The word has been compared to Ukr. gava 'crow', but since PSI. g should have changed into h in this language, this form is likely to be a distortion of Slav. \*kava (Danylenko 2006: 184), cf. Cz. kavka 'jackdaw'. It is conceivable, on the other hand, that this word together with Lith. k6vas, k6va 'jackdaw' was borrowed from Germanic, although this would probably imply a WGm. proto-form \*kawo- ( < \*kewo-) rather than \*kawo-. Da. ka, Nw. ka, kaje, Sw. kaja were borrowed from Low German.

Go. kas n. 'jar, vase, vessel', ON ker n. 'tub, vessel, goblet', Far. kar n. 'id.', Elfd. kar n. 'id.', OS bi-kar n. 'bee-hive', MDu. caer n. 'creel, bee-hive', Du. vis-kaar n. 'creel', OHG kar n. 'vessel, vat, trough', MHG kar n. 'vessel' (GM). No Indo-European etymology. The word is close to, but phonetically irreconcilable with Olr. coire, MW peir 'cauldron' < PCelt. \*kwaryo- < \*kwr-to-, for which see \*hwera-. The often assumed link with Lat. ger6 'to carry', allegedly continuing a root \*ges-, is erroneous, because this verb continues \*h₂9-es-, i.e. an s-present to ago, -ere < \*h₂eg-e- (for which see \*akan- 1).

OE ceafor, cefer m. 'beetle', E chafer, ODu. kevera f. 'grasshopper', MDu. kever(e) m. 'beetle', Du. kever c. 'id.', OHG kevar(o) m. 'id.', G Kafer m. 'id.' (EUR?). A West Germanic word probably leading back to a proto-form \*kefran- or \*kebran- (with -br- > \*-fr- by Bahder's law). The reconstruction of the vocalism is problematic, however. Swi. (App.) xt:fer (with E < \*e rather than \*ii1 or \*iiz) precludes a formation \*kabiza-, which would have given \*\*xefar in this dialect. A form \*kafra- or \*kabra- must nevertheless be reconstructed on the basis of OE ceafor. Etymologically, the word is often derived from a verbal root \*gebh- as supported by OCS o-zobati 'to spoil', Ru. zobat' …

OE cii!g f. 'key', OFri. kai, kei m. 'id.' ( < \*kega-), WFri. kaai c. 'id.' (GM). An Anglo-Frisian isogloss, perhaps with an original meaning 'peg', cf. MDu. kegghe f. 'wedge' < \*kagjo-. See further \*kagan-.

ON kjalki m. 'jaw; sledge', Far. kjalki m. 'cheekbone', Nw. kjelke m. 'sledge', dial. 'Adam's apple', Elfd. tjake m. 'sledge', OHG kelah m. 'goiter', MHG kelch m. 'crop, double chin' (IE). A formation related to \*kelon- 'throat' (q.v.). Perhaps, the k-suffix can be explained from a ur/n -stem \*gwefhrur, \*gfwJ/hru-en-s (see \*keluro-), which could regularly have developed into PGm. \*kelkur, \*kulkenaz by the velarization ofpost-sonorantal laryngeals before \*u (cf. \*taikwer-). A potential zero grade is present in Da. kulk 'gullet', dial. 'Adam's apple'. Ice!. kalkur m. 'sledge, bar on a sledge' continues an o-grade formation \*kalka-. Also cf. OHG kelh, MHG kelch m. 'crop, goiter'.

OE ceole f. 'throat, jowl', Du. keel c. 'throat', OHG kela, kel f. 'id.', G Kehle f. 'id.' :=} \*gwefh₁-eh₂- (IE) - Close to Lat. gula f. 'throat, gullet' < \*gwfhreh₂-; also cf. Skt. girati, gilati 'to devour' < \*gwJh₁-e-; Arm. ekul 'devoured' < \*h₁e-gwofhr; Gr. MA.mp (gen. -awe;, pl. oeO.arn, oeA.rupa) n. 'decoy, bait' < \*gwe/h₁-u[. The lack oflabialization in Germanic is problematic, but it may have been lost in the zero-grade form \*gul- < \*gwfhr, for which see \*keluro-. The word has a WGm. distribution, but Elfd. grq-tjyolu f. 'Eurasian siskin', lit "firtree-throat"(?), appears to point to the pre-existence of ON \*kjala, obi. \*kjplu (Noreen 1882: 66 fn. 1). See also \*kelkan-.

OE ceoler f. 'collar, throat', OHG kelur, kelero m., kelera f. 'id.' :=} \*gwefhrur- (IE). The Germanic word is usually reconstructed as \*keluza-, but the alternative \*kelura- is preferable because it favors the reconstruction of the word as a heteroclitic ur/n-stem, nom. \*gwefh₁-ur, gen. \*gfwlfh₁-en-s. In this paradigm the loss of the labialization would be regular in the genitive, which allows us to connect the word to the PIE root \*gwefhr 'to devour'. See also \*kelon-.

Ice!. kam n. 'filth', E dial. coom 'soot', EDu. kaem, kaen 'layer of mold (on fluids)', Du. kaam, kaan c. 'id.', MHG kcim, kcin(e) n. 'id.', G Kahm m. 'id.' (GM). Go. kiusan s.v. 'to put to a test, prove by trial', ON kj6sa s.v. 'to choose, select, elect', Far. kj6sa s.v. 'id.', OE ceosan s.v. 'to choose, to elect', OS kiosan, keosan, keosan s.v. OE ciosol, ceosol, cisel m. 'pebble', MDu. kesel 'id.', Du. kiezel c. 'gravel; pebble', OHG kisel m. 'pebble' :=} \*gis-o-(?) (NIE) - Lith. zizdras, dial. iigidras m. 'gravel', iiezdra, ziegidra f. (S) gjj, gjj 'grain of sand', Komi keia 'gravel', Khanty xrs 'fine sand' and Georg. kvisa 'sand'.

Etymologically, it is clearly linked with the Armenian forms. The implied base \*geid- is in violation of the PIE…
OE cfo m. 'young shoot, sprig', OS kith m. 'germ, sprout', OHG kfdi n. 'shoot', MHG kfde n. 'id.' ( < \*kfpja-) :=} \*g(e)iH-tf- (DRY). A ti-stem to the PIE root \*geiH-, for which see \*kfnan-.

OE cen m. 'pine tree, spruce', MLG ken 'pine cone, pinewood', OHG kien m./n. 'pine tree, pinewood torch', G Kien m. OE clϋne adj. 'clean', E clean, MDu. cleine adj. 'small, fine, neat', Du. klein adj. 'small', OHG kleini adj. 'small, delicate, fine, pure, clean', G klein adj. 'small' (DRY). Pokorny JEW: 366-7), for which see \*klajja- 'clay'. Its original meaning must then have been 'shining', whence 'pretty' and 'delicate' (cf. Heidermanns 1993: 332). Still problematic is the variant LG klin, Swi. xiii, which appears to continue an ablauting form \*klin- (Mitzka 1934). Heidermanns (I.e.) assumed a verbal origin for this ablaut, but the doublet \*klainja- : \*…

OE cloo n. 'id.', E cloth, OFri. klath, kleth n. 'id.', WFri. klaad n. 'id.', Du. kleed n. 'id.', OHG kleid n. 'id.', G Kleid n. 'id.' (GM). Certainly related, on the other hand, is lcel. klfo n. 'piece of woven fabric' < \*klipa- (cf. Magnusson 1989: 45). ON, Far. klc:eCJi, Elfd. kliioe n. 'cloth; garment' was borrowed from West Germanic.

OE cltifre, clϋfre f. 'id.', E clover, OFri. klti(?) m.(?) 'id.', WFri. klaver c. 'id.', EFri. kleeuwer m. 'id.', OS k/e 'id.', MDu. clavere f. 'id.', Du. klaver c. 'id.', OHG kle(o) m./n. OE clϋ.fre < \*klaiwizon- vs.

'claw' can together with OHG k/6{a) be reconstructed as \*k/6w6-, which would be an ablaut variant of OHG klawa < \*k/aw6- (hardly \*k/ewo-). Indeed, there are traces of such ablaut in the pertaining verbal formations. OE clawian, OHG klawen w.v. 'to scratch' can simply be derived from a weak stative verb \*klawen-, but ON k/Q, pret kltioa vs. k/6, Far. k/Qa, pret klciaoi, kleroi, ptc. kligid and Elfd. klti, pret. kluo, ptc. k/aio (the latter with the meaning 'to toil') in combination with OSw. kleya, Da. …

Gr. yA.u<pw 'to carve, cut out, engrave' < \*glubh-e- and perhaps also OCS g/9bok-b, Ru. glub6kij adj. 'deep' < \*glu-m-bh-o-. A verb with a European distribution. A different formation is ON klyfia w.v. 'to split' < \*klubjan- < \*glubh-ie-. Also cf. \*klaubjan-.

OCS ib/y, iely f. 'ulcer, sore', Ru. ielvak m. 'tumor', Latv. dz՚/va f. 'swelling on the skin' < \*gelu-u(e)h₂-; Gr. yA.ouT6<; m. 'buttock' < \*glouh₂-to-. ON klif n. 'cliff, protruding isolated rock', Far. kliv f. 'steep place, cliff, rock', Elfd. kliv n. 'id.', MDu. clif n. 'cliff, steep, slope', OHG klep 'id.' (DRY). ON klifa s.v. 'to climb', Far. klugva (k/fva) s.v. 'id.', Elfd. klaiva s.v. 'to climb', OS bi-klfban s.v. 'to stick', Du. be-klijven s.v. 'id.',

Elfd. klin99a s.v. 'id.', E to clink 'id.', OFri. klinga, klinka s.v. 'id.', MLG klingen s.v. 'id.', MDu. clingen, clinken s.v. 'id.', Du. klinken s.v. 'id.', OHG klingan s.v. 'id.', G klingen s.v. 'id.'

Icel. kliikkva s.v. 'to be moved, cringe, become afraid', Nw. kfgkka s.v. 'to be shocked, appalled', ODa. klykke 'to be moved', OE clingan s.v. 'to shrink', Du. in-klinken s.v. 'to settle (of sediment, clay)' (NEUR?). E to clunk 'to sound; to hit', MDu. c/ongen, clonken w.v. 'to sound'

ON knefill m. 'post, pole', MLG knevel m. 'short thick bail, toggle', EDu. knevel 'sinew, strap; fetter', Du. knevel c. 'gag', OHG knebil m. 'cudgel', G Knebel m. 'handle, toggle, gag' (DRY). The diminutive to \*knab/ppan- (q.v.).

Nw. knape m. 'guy; pin', OE cnafa, cnapa m. 'boy', E knave, OFri. knap(p)a m. 'id.', OS knapo m. 'id.', ODu. knapo m. 'id.', Du. knaap c. 'boy; coat hanger', OHG knabo, knappo m.
