The original meaning of the word may have been 'peg', cf. the closely related thematic formation ON

ON kncir adj. 'hardy, vigorous; having strength', Far. knciur adj. 'skilful, able; strong; persevering' (DRY). Also cf. Far. knava w.v. 'to manage, narrowly reach something'. The adjective has been equated with OE ge-cn<Ewe 'in agreement with' (cf. Pokorny JEW: 376-8), the gerundive to \*knean- (q.v.). Alternatively, it can be derived from ON knyja w.v. 'to knock; to press, to push, urge on' < \*knujan- (q.v.). The proto-form must then be reconstructed as \*knawa- < \*gnouH-o-.

ON knci, pl. knegum pret.-pres. 'to be able', OE ge-cniiwan s.v. 'to know, perceive, understand, recognise', OS and-kniian s.v. 'to understand', bi-kniian s.v., bi-knegan 'to attain', OHG bi-kniien w.v. 'to recognize' (IE). A verb closely related to \*kunnan- (q.v.). Its derivation is problematic. In view of OE ge-cniiwan and OHG kniien, it must be reconstructed as PGm. \*knean- (with a secondary glide in OE), but the root \*kne- can only be accounted for by reconstructing a PIE lengthened grade root \*gneh₃- (Rasmussen 1978a: 127, fn. 31; Jasanoff 1988). The alternative is to assume that the present was back-formed to the original aorist root \*gneh₃-. This root, which would regularly have …

ON knooa s.v. 'id.', Far. knooa w.v. 'id.', Elfd. knooa w.v. 'id.', OE cnedan s.v. 'id.', E knead, OS knedan w.v. 'id.', MLG kneden s.v. 'id.', MDu. cneden s.v. 'id.', Du. kneden w.v. 'id.', OHG knetan, knedan s.v. 'id.', G kneten

OE ge-cnϋwe adj. 'in agreement with, acknowledging' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to \*knean- 'to know, be able' (q.v.), with an original meaning 'to be acknowledged'. The lengthened grade is productive in this formation and does not attest to a PIE root \*gneh₃- (with Eichner's law). lt is further to be stressed that the w of OE ge-c,nϋwe is secondary: it was adopted from the infinitive cnawa n ( < \* knean-), where it regularly arose in hiatus position. There therefore cannot be a direct link with Lat. gnavus adj. 'known' < \*gnh₃-uo-. See also \*knawa-.
Go. kniu n. 'knee', ON kne m. 'knee; node', Far. kn<E n. 'id.', Elfd. kni n. 'id.', OE cneo(w) n. 'knee', OS knio n. 'id.', Du. knie c. 'id.', OHG kneo n. 'id.', G Knie n. 'id.'

ON poet. kniar m.pl. 'men', OFri. kniii m. 'relative', MDu. even-cnie c. 'of equal birth', Du. even-knie c. 'match' (DRV). An n-stem created to \*knewa- 'knee' (q.v.). The latter word also occurs with the meaning 'degree of relationship'. It has been assumed that this meaning arose due to influence from Lat. genus n. 'race, stock, descent', but since the hitherto unetymologized ON knfar 'men(?)' must belong here, it proves that the meaning 'relative' was native to Germanic. For the form of the word, cf. ON k/e, pl. kljar < \*klewan- (q.v.).
ON knifr m. 'knife, dirk', Far. knfvur m. 'id.', Elfd. knaiv m. 'id.', OE cnif m. 'knife', G Swab. Kneip( en) 'large knife', Swi. (App.) gniippa 'id.' ( < \*knfbban-)
Icel. knja w.v. 'id.'

Nw. knikka w.v. 'to kneel', MDu. cnicken w.v. 'to snap', Du. knikken w.v. 'to nod, kink', G knicken w.v. 'to snap, bend' (GM). An iterative that ultimately must be related to \*knewa- 'knee' (q.v.), even though the origin of the suffixation is obscure. Also cf. the causative ON kneikja w.v. 'to press', Nw. dial. kneikja w.v. 'to bend backwards', refl. 'to sprain', Elfd. knjetja-s w.v. 'to succumb' < \*knaikjan-.

Du. knijpen s.v. 'to pinch', G dial. kneipen s.v. 'to pinch' ( < \*knibban-) (DRY). A de-iterative verb Pre-Gm. \*knippan- derived from or at least influenced by the iterative \*knipp/bon- (q.v.). The High German dialects must at any rate have adopted the \*bb from there.
MLG cnippen w.v. 'id.', Du. knippen w.v. 'to cut', dial. 'to pinch', G Pal. knippen w.v. 'to castrate; to pluck'
Go. knops f. 'family, clan', OHG knuot f. 'nature, descent'
ON knyja w.v. 'to knock; to press, to push, urge on'
Nw. knoke f. 'bone, joint' ( < \*knukon-), MDu. cnoke, cnoc(ke) m. 'knuckle, joint, vertebra', MHG knoche m. 'bone', G Knochen m. 'id.'

MLG knocke, knucke f. 'bundle of flax', G Knocke m./f. 'id.' (GM) - Lith. gnitiuzti (gniauiiu) 'to close one's hand' < \*gneug-ie(whence Lith. gniuiis 'bundle, handful'). A rather isolated formation, probably derived from a lost verbal complex, cf. OE (ge)cnyccan w.v. 'to bind together, connect' < \*knukkjan- (whence OE gecnycce n. 'bond', ME knicche 'bundle (of brush, weeds), bunch, sheaf, E dial. knitch 'bundle'). The word is formally reconcilable with Lith. gniauzti, but the Germanic meaning was 'to bind', which is semantically remote from 'to close one's hand'.

Go. knussjan w.v. 'id.; (DRV). A verb usually derived from a lost noun \*knussu- 'kneeling' < \*gnut-tu- or \*knussa- 'kneeled' < \*gnut-to- so as to explain the double s (see e.g. Smith 1938). It is furthermore possible to compare G knotzen, knutzen 'to squat' (see DWB: 11, 1512), which points to an iterative \*knutton- < \*gnut-neh₂-. The stem of this verb may be the original PIE instrumental case of the word for 'knee', cf. Hitt. ganut 'with the knee' < \*gn-u-t.

G dial. knotzen w.v. 'to press, knead, squeeze' (DRV). The iterative to \*knedan- - \*knudan- (q.v.), apparently continuing Pre-Gm. \*gnut-nehi-. Further cf. G dial. kniitzen w.v. 'to squeeze; punch' < \*knuttjan- and Pal. knozen 'to knead' < \*knautjan-(?).

ON knutr m. 'knot; hump, protuberance', lcel. hnuoi m. 'knob, hump', Far. knutur m. 'knot', OE cnotta m. 'knot', E knot, Du. knot c. 'id.', OHG knodo, knoto m. 'id.', G Knoten m. 'id.' (DRV). An ablauting n-stem nom. \*knupo, gen. \*knuttaz, dat. \*knudeni (Kroonen 201 la: 299-300) with many different dialectal remodelings of the original
\*ku- 299 root variants. The word is likely to be a ton-formation to the PIE root \*gnu'knee; node', for which cf. \*knewa-.

ON kjr f. 'id.', Far. kugv f. 'id.', Elfd. tjyr f. 'id.', OE cii, pl. cji f. 'id.', E cow, OFri. kii f. 'id.', WFri. ko c. 'id.', EFri. kuu f. 'id.', OS ko f. 'id.', Du. koe c. 'id.', OHG kuo f. 'id.', G Kuh f. 'id.'

MDu. coeke m./f. 'id.', Du. koek c. 'id.', OHG kuohho m. 'id.', G Kuchen m. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. the diminutive OE c&cel m. 'morsel, little mouthful, cake' and OS kasi-k6kilin n. 'whole cheese'. Related to ON kaka, obi. kpku, Far. kflJka, Elfd. ktika, obi. kaku f. 'cake' < \*kakon- with ablaut.

MLG k6ke f. 'sledge runner', OHG slito-k6hha f. 'skid', G Kufe f. 'runner, blade, skid' (with -f- < -ch-?) (GM). Possibly related to \*kagan- 'shrub' (q.v.). The word must then be reconstructed as Pre-Gm. \*k6kk6n- with a geminate, for which cf. MLG kak m. 'pillory', MDu. cake f., caek m. 'scaffold' < Pre-Gm. \*kekka(n)-.

OE col adj. 'id.', E cool, MDu. coele adj. 'id.', Du. koel adj. 'id.', OHG kuoli adj. 'id.', G kiihl adj. 'id.' (DRV). A secondary vṛddhi-formation to \*kalan- (q.v.). ON kcela, OE celan, OHG kuolen w.v. 'to cool, chill' < \*k6ljan- may represent a factitive derived from this adjective, but could also continue a causative derived directly from the aforementioned strong verb.

ON kcenn adj. 'wise, skilful, expert', Far. -k{lnur adj. 'id.', Elfd. tjyon adj. 'articulate', OE cene adj. 'keen, fierce, bold', E keen, MLG k6ne adj. 'bold', MDu. coene adj. 'id.', Du. koen adj. 'id.', OHG kuoni adj. 'bold, strong, grim', G kiihn adj. 'bold, audacious' (DRV). A vṛddhi-gerundive to the root \*kann-, see \*knean- and \*kunnan-.
ON krabbi m. 'id.', Far. krabbi m. 'id.', OE crabba m. 'id.', E crab, MDu. crabbe m. 'id.', Du. krab c. 'id.' (DRV). An n-stem derived from the iterative \*krapp/biin- (q.v.).

ON krefja w.v. 'to demand', Far. krevja w.v. 'to demand', poet. 'to urge', Elfd. kreva w.v. 'id.', OE crafian w.v. 'to ask, crave, implore, demand', E to crave (GM). · A stative verb possibly related to \*kraftu-. No certain etymology. Elfd. kravel n. 'trouble, hassle, seediness, morbidity' < \*krab(a)la- may point to an original meaning 'to be in need', whence 'to require, demand'. See also \*krebi-.

OS krebit m. 'id.', MDu. crevet, creeft m./n. 'id.', Du. kreeft c. 'id.', OHG krebazzo, krebiz m. 'id.', MHG krebez, krebz m. 'id.', G Krebs f. 'id.' (NIE?). An animal name probably related to \*krabban- 'crab' (q.v.), which was itself derived from the iterative \*krapp/b6n- (q.v.). It is difficult, on the other hand, to separate the word from Mediterranean lookalikes such as Gr. Kfrpaƴo<;, Kaƴoupo<;, Kaμ(μ)apo<; 'crab' (see \*humara-), which are evidently non-Indo-European. The question thus arises of whether the PGm. ita-suffix can be compared to the non-IE suffix -Lv-8- - -1-8- as found in many Pre-Greek words (see e.g. under \*arwit- 'pea'). Within synchronic Germanic, however, it functi…

ON krpftr m. 'id.', Far. kraft f. 'id.', Elfd. krapt f. 'id.', OE crreft m. 'id.', E craft, OS kraft f. 'id.', Du. kracht c. 'id.', OHG kraft f. 'id.', G Kraft f. 'id.' (DRV). A tu-stem possibly related to \*kraben- (q.v.).
ME craue 'neck, throat; crop', E craw, MDu. crage m. 'neck, throat', Du. kraag c. 'collar', MHG krage m. 'throat; collar', G Kragen m. 'id.'
ON kremja w.v. 'to squeeze, bruise'
ON krammr adj. 'half thawed', Ice!. kramur adj. 'id.', Nw. krammr adj. 'id.'
OE crammian w.v. 'to stuff, E to cram, MHG krammen w.v. 'to grab with one's claws' (ORV?). An o-grade iterative to \*krimman- (q.v.).

ON krappr adj. 'sharp, crafty', Far. krappur adj. 'acute-angled; bent, curved', Elfd. kropp adj. 'bent, crooked; sad', MDu. cramp adj. 'convulsive', OHG krampf adj. 'id.' (ORV). An adjective derived from \*krimpan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON kreppa w.v. 'to squeeze', Far. kreppa w.v. 'to bend, bow, make curved' < \*krampjan- and OHG krampf, G Krampf, Du. kramp 'convulsion' < \*krampa-.
ON trani m., trana f. 'id.', Far. trani m. 'id.', Elfd. trdnd f. 'id.', OE cran m. 'id.', E crane, OS krano m. 'id.', Du. kraan-vogel c. 'id.', MHG krane m. 'id.', G Krahn m. 'id.'
ON krangr adj. 'weak, frail', MDu. crane ad. 'weak, ill, insignificant', Du. krang adj. 'inside-out', MHG krank adj. 'weak, thin', G krank adj. 'ill' (ORV).
E to cringe, MDu. crenghen 'to make turn'

ON kra, kr6 f. 'id.', Nw. krci, pl. krcer f. 'id.' (DRY?). A Nordic word of uncertain derivation. The root \*kranh- is formally and semantically close to the cluster of \*kringan- - \*krinkan- (q.v.), but these forms clearly continue a Pre-Gm. root \*grengh-, and therefore cannot be related. It is possible, however, that \*kranho- arose as a back-formation to the strong verb.
MLG krabben w.v. 'id.', MDu. crabben w.v. 'id.', Du. krabben, krappen dial. kraven w.v. 'id.', G krappen, kraben w.v. 'id.'

ON kartr m. 'cart' ( << OE \*cart?), OE crcet n. 'chariot', MDu. cratte m. 'wickerwork, hurdle', Du. krat c. 'crate', OHG kratto, kretto, kretzo m. 'basket', MHG kratte, kretze m. 'id.', G Kratte m. 'id.'

MDu. kroos n. 'bowels (esp. of geese); gristle, cartilage', Du. obs. kroos n. 'bowels of a calf, MHG krrese, ge-krrese n. 'guts', G Ge-krose n. 'id.' (GM). A neuter collective \*(ga-)krausja- probably related to G krosen w.v. 'to crunch, grate (of bones, teeth); to crush', Swi. (App.) xrosa w.v. 'to squeeze' < \*kruson- and MHG krosel, krossel m./f. 'cartilage' < \*krusla/o-. MLG kros was borrowed into Nordic, cf. Nw. kras m. 'paunch fat', f. 'tangle; (part of the) gizzard', Da. kras 'intestines (ofbirds); gizzard'.
EFri. kriiiif, kriiiiwe m. 'trough, crib', MLG karve f. 'creel', OHG korb m. 'basket', MHG krebe m., kreppe f., korb(e) m. 'id.', G Korb m. 'id.'

ON kr<Efr adj. 'strong', Icel. kr<Efur adj. 'tough; due for payment', Nw. dial. kr<Ev adj. 'strong, tough' (DRV). A Nordic formation \*krebi- (or \*krefi-). The Icelandic meaning 'due for payment' is clearly gerundival, which implies that ON kr<Efr arose as a vṛddhi-gerundive to the verb ON krejja 'to claim' < \*kraben- (q.v.) (Kroonen 2010a). This explains both the lengthened grade and the suffix. Still, while gerundives are usually derived from strong verbs, krejja was originally weak (in spite of dial. Nw. krevja, pret. krov). This may point to a lost strong verb \*kreban-.

Nw. dial. kryda s.v. 'to gather, flock; to crawl slowly', OE creodan, criidan s.v. 'to crowd, press, drive', MDu. cruden w.v. 'to push forth/aside', Du. kruien w.v. 'to push a wheelbarrow; to pile up (of ice floes)' (EUR?). A verb that in spite of the lack of a dental can perhaps be related to Gr. Ƿpuw 'to be full to bursting, to swell' < \*gwru- (cf. \*kriida- 'herb'). The original meaning must then have been 'to push oneself out', i.e. 'to crowd'. The strong verb gave rise to OE croda m. 'crowd' < \*krudan- and MHG, MDu. krot n. 'hindrance, bother' < \*kruda-, whence MHG kroten, MDu. kroden w.v. 'to hinder, bother'. Related to \*krutt/d6n- (q.v.).
\*krukan-

Nw. krjuka s.v. 'to cringe; to crawl', WFri. kruke w.v. 'to cringe' (WEUR) - Olr. gruc f. 'wrinkle'. This verb seems to have been associated with \*kreupan- - \*kriipan- 'to crawl' from an early stage, but its primary meaning must have been 'to fold', whence 'to wrinkle' and 'to stoop, bend down, crawl'. This meaning is implied by e.g. MDu. croke f. 'fold, wrinkle, rip, fissure', Du. kreukel c. 'fold' and also by the link with Olr. gruc. The latter word is usually derived from \*gru-n-k- with a root-final \*k, and this implies that the Germanic roots \*kreuk- and \*kriik- continue a simplified geminate \*kk. This geminate presumably spread from the iterative \*kruk(k)6n-, cf. Nw. krukka w.v.…

ON krjupa s.v. 'to creep, crouch; to kneel', Far. krupa s.v. 'to creep, crawl; to drag oneself forth', Elfd. kriuopa s.v. 'to kneel', OE creopan s.v. 'to crawl', E to creep, OFri. kriii.pa w.v. 'id.', MDu. crupen s.v. 'id.', Du. kruipen s.v. 'id.', OHG kriofan s.v. 'id.', MHG knJ.fen s.v. 'id.', G Rhnl. krufen s.v. 'id.' (DRY). In view of Cimb. kruppen < \*krubb6n- (see \*krupp/b6n-), which continues an iterative verb 3sg. \*krupp6pi, 3pl. \*krubunanpi ( < \*grubh-neh₂-ti,

\*kriipan- must represent a shortened geminate Pre-Gm. \*pp. This means that the traditional reconstruction of a PIE root \*greub- (Pokorny JEW: 385-90) can no longer be maintained, and is to be replaced by \*greubh-. Also, the notoriously problematic geminate of ON kryppill 'cripple' < \*kruppila-, which contrasts with WGm. \*krupila-, can be explained by assuming derivation from the strong verb prior to the shortening of the geminate (see under \*krup(p )ila-).

MHG kriege adj. 'reluctant, militant' (DRY?). A gerundive closely related to \*krlgan- (q.v.). Since gerundives were created to the plural preterite root of strong verbs, the form \*kriagi- (WGm. \*kriagja-) implies that there was an otherwise lost reduplicated verb \*kraigan-, pret kriag- (for older \*kre-kraig-). The closely related OHG kreg 'stubbornness', MHG kriec m. 'effort, struggle' < \*kriaga- contains the same base. MHG kriegen w.v. 'to strive, struggle, fight', G kriegen w.v. 'to get' < \*kriag6jan-, on the other hand, was created on the basis of the gerundive.
MDu. crigen s.v. 'to strive, fight; to obtain', Du. krijgen s.v. 'to get' (WEUR?).
OE crimman s.v. 'to crumb, crumble, mingle', OHG krimman s.v. 'to bite, hurt; to tear up; to squeeze, stuff, MHG krimmen, grimmen s.v. 'to grab, squeeze', G Tyr. krimmen 'to push into'
ON kreppa s.v. 'to cringe', MDu. crempen s.v. 'to shrink', Du. krimpen s.v. 'id.', OHG krimfan s.v. 'id.', MHG krimpfen s.v. 'id.'
\*krumpa-. The verb probably continues a nasal present to the root \*grebh- 'to be crooked'.

ON kringr m. 'id.', Far. kringur m. 'id.', Du. kring c. 'id.', MHG kring m. 'id.' (DRV). An a-stem derived from the strong verb \*kringan- - \*krinkan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON kringr adj. 'easy', Far. kringur adj. 'capable, dexterous', Elfd. kringg adj. 'quick' < \*kringa-.
OE cringan, crincan s.v. 'to yield, fall'

Nw. kry adj. 'id.', Sw. kry adj. 'id.' (GM?). Also cf. Sw. krya pa sig, Nw. dial. kra seg ( < \*krja) w.v. 'to get well again' < \*kriwojan-. No certain etymology. Perhaps a uo-stem to a stem \*gr-i-, cf. Skt jcirate 'to wake up' < \*h₁9er-e- and Gr. fydpw 'id.' < \*h₁9er-ie-.

ON kryfja w.v. 'id.' (GM). To Ice!. krjufa s.v. 'id.', apparently continuing \*kreufan- or \*kreuban(Magnusson 1989: 506). Also cf. ON krof n. 'cut-up carcass'. The no doubt related Nw. kropp m. 'id.' could point to a link with \*kruppa- 'compact object' (q.v.).

OS kriid n. 'id.', Du. kruid n. 'id.', OHG kriit n. 'id.', G Kraut n. 'id.' (DRV). A neuter probably derived from the strong verb \*kreudan- - \*kriidan- 'to push forward' (q.v.). The original meaning must then have been 'crowding plant', i.e. 'weed'. For a semantic parallel, cf. Du. dial. krodde 'several small weeds' (Kocks 2000), which was derived from the related iterative verb \*krutt/don- (q.v.).

Nw. krodde m. 'dregs (of curdled milk, coffee, beer, grease etc.)', f. 'boiled cheese', ME crudde, curde 'curds', E curds (DRV) - To Olr. gruth m. 'curds, cheese'? A late Nordic derivative of the verbal complex of Nw. krota 'to curdle', for which see \*krutt/don- 'to pack(?)'. The meaning 'curds' must be completely secondary in Norwegian in view of Nw. krodde, a word used for a variety of dregs (coffee, grease, cheese). As a result, the Germanic and Celtic forms can no longer be adduced to reconstruct a PIE root \*grut- (pace Pokorny IEW: 406). Olr. gruth in fact does not have to continue \*grutu- either, but rather seems to have developed from PCelt. \*gwritu- < \*gwthlr-tu- by rounding of …

MDu. erode f. 'id.', EDu. krodde 'id.', OHG krota, krotta f. 'id.', G Krote f. 'id.' (wEUR?). An n-stem that may have originated from a verbal root 'to swell, bulge', cf. G obs. krotten 'to swell up' < \*krutt/don- (q.v.). Flabbiness is a common Benennungsmotiv for toads, cf. EDu. kwabbe 'toad, frog' vs. Du. kwab 'flab'. The fronted vowel of G Krote was generalized from the plural, cf. G Bav. (Zarz) kxroute, pl. kxroute vs. leveled Swab. krote / krote, pl. kroten / kroten.
\*krumpa- 307

OE crump adj. 'id.', OS krumb adj. 'id.', Du. krom adj. 'id.', OHG krumpf, krumb adj. 'id.', MHG krumpf, krump adj. 'id.', G krumm adj. 'id.' (ORV). An adjective derived from the strong verb \*krimpan- (q.v.). In view of the variant \*krumba-, this verb must have replaced the more primary \*krimban< \*gre-m-bh·e- under the influence of the iterative, cf. G krumpfen w.v. 'to cringe' < \*krumb/p6n- < \*grmbh-neh₂-.
EDu. kronken w.v. 'id.', Du. kronkelen w.v. 'to wind, squirm'

ON kroppr m. 'body, trunk', Far. kroppur m. 'body, build; corpse', Sw. krupp m. 'body', OE crop m. 'sprout, bunch, rock', E crop, MDu. crop(pe) m. 'gnarl, bud, rosette; body; goiter', Du. krop m. 'goiter; rosette (of lettuce)', OHG kropfm. 'goiter', G Kropf m. 'id.' (oRv?). An a-stem perhaps related to \*kruppfb6n- (q.v.) through a meaning 'to be clump-like'.
MDu. cropen w.v. 'id.', G Rhnl. kruppen 'to crouch, curl up', Cimb. kruppen 'to crawl'

ON kryppill adj. 'id.', Far. krypil adj. 'id.', OE crypel adj. 'id.', MDu. cropel adj. 'id.', Du. kreupel adj. 'id.' (ORV). An adjective derived from the verb \*kreupan- - \*kriipan- (q.v.) with the usual \*-ila- suffix. The double p of ON kryppill has always been difficult to account for, because it points to \*kruppila- rather than the more widespread \*krupila-. The issue can be resolved, however, by assuming that \*kruppila- was derived from the strong verb \*kreuppan- before the shortening of the geminate. The geminate may then have been analogically removed on the basis of the singulated form \*kreupan- in all languages except Icelandic. See also \*krupp/bon-.
OE cryppan w.v. 'to bend', G krupfen, krupfen w.v. 'to bend, refl. to cringe' (DRV). A causative derived the iterative \*krupp/bt5n- 'to crawl' (q.v.).

Nw. kroda w.v. 'to huddle (around)', krota w.v. 'to coagulate, curdle', ME crudden w.v. 'to coagulate, congeal, solidify, curdle', G obs. krotten w.v. 'to swell', Swi. xrotten w.v. 'to pack, stuff (DRV?). An iterative 3sg. \*krutton, 3pl. \*krudunanj:Ji related to \*kreudan- - \*krudan- (q.v.). In view of the meaning of G krotten 'to swell', it is possible that the etymologically obscure \*krud(d}6n- 'toad' (q.v.) was derived from it The German verb is further reminiscent of W chrothi 'to bulge' and the pertaining W crwth, croth f. 'bulge, belly, womb, calf of the leg, violin', Olr. cruit 'hump; harp' < \*krutt-, which has previously been derived from VLat crotta 'crypt, vault' < crypta (Mei…

ON kofi m. 'convent cell; hut, shed', Far. kovi m. 'outbuilding', Elfd. kuvi m. 'chamber', OE cofa m. 'chamber, cave, den', MDu. cove m. 'hut, shed', MHG kobe m. 'stable' (also kobel m. 'shed, box'), G Kohen m. 'shed'

ON koddi m. 'pillow', Far. koddi m. 'id.', Nw. kodd(e) m. 'pillow; scrotum, testicle', OSw. kodde m. 'testicle', OE codd m. 'bag, scrotum; husk', EDu. kodde 'testicle' (GM). The n-stem \*kuddan- looks like a hypocorism to the full-grade formation OE ceod m.f. 'pouch', OHG kiot m. 'id.' < \*keuda-. Perhaps Swab. Kotz f. 'pim· pie, blister(?)' can be adduced to reconstruct an n-stem variant \*kutt6n-. More certain is the connection with ON koori m. 'scrotum' < \*kudran- and MDu. codde, EDu. kodde 'cudgel', Scot. cud 'staff, club' < \*kuddan-.
OFri. kedde m. 'id.', MDu. cudde n./f. 'id.', Du. kudde c. 'id.', OHG kutti n. 'id.', G Bav. kutt n./f. 'flock of birds'

Go. kukjan w.v. 'id.', LG kiikken w.v. 'id.', Du. dial. kukken w.v. 'id.' (GM). An originally onomatopoetic formation parallel to the verb \*kussjan( q.v.).

ON kol n.pl. 'id.', Far. kol n. 'id.', Elfd. kuo/ n. 'id.', OE col n. 'id.', E coal, OFri. kole f. 'id.', MDu. cole m./f. 'id.', Du. kool n./c. 'id.', OHG kol n., kolo m. 'id.', MHG kol n. 'id.', G Kohle f. 'id.'

ON kolfr m. 'bulb; bolt, arrow', Far. poet benjar-kolvur m. 'sword', Elfd. kuov m. 'arrow', OS kolbo m. 'club, cudgel, thong', Du. kolf c. 'butt; flask, ear of corn', OHG kolbo m. 'cob, cudgel', G Kolben m. 'spadix; flask'

'cup, measure for corn and fruit', G Kumpf m. 'id.' (NIE). The variation of OE cumb < \*kumba-, MDu. komme < \*kumba/on- and MHG kumpf < \*kumpa- points to dialectally divergent leveling of a Pre-Gm. n-stem \*kumbo, gen. \*kumpaz < \*gumbh-on, gen. \*gumbh-n-6s. There can be no regular relationship with Gr. J<Uμƴoc; m., J<Uμƴri f. 'hollow vessel' < \*kumb-o/eh₂-, W cwm, Bret. komm 'river-bed' < \*kumb-eh₂- and Skt kumbha-, Av. xumba-, Pers. xumb, xum 'pot, jar' < Pllr. \*kumbha- - \*khumbha-, which, in …
OFri. kummer m./n. 'sorrow, impediment, arrest, seizure', MDu. commer, comber m. 'trouble, hindrance, arrest, seizure', Du. kommer c. 'sorrow, misery', MHG kumber m. 'burden, sorrow' ʬ

ToAB kiimii- suppl. 'to carry, bring' < \*gom-; Gr. ytμw 'to be full' (cf. yoμoc; 'freight'), Lat. gemo 'to groan, moan' < \*gem-e-; Olr. gemel 'fetter' < \*gem-lo-; OCS Zfiti (ibmp) 'to press', Latv. sa-gumt'to bend' < \*gT[l-. A ro-stem to the PIE root \*gem-.

\*kununga- 311 No accepted etymology. The verb looks like the outcome of a PIE ie-present \*gwndh-ie- to a root \*gwendh-, but no such root seems to have existed. It is perhaps possible to think that formation was created to a participle \*gwh₃-nt- to the root \*gwh₃- 'to live' in view of the similar semantic shift from 'live' to 'fire' in \*kwaikwjan- (q.v.). This solution remains conjectural, however. Further cf. ON kyndill m. 'torch' and Nw. dial. kvende n. 'tinder' < \*kwandja-.

ON konungr m. 'id.', Far. kongur m. 'id.', Elfd. kunungg m. 'id.', OE cyning, cyng m. 'id.', E king, OFri. kening, keneg, kining, kinig m. 'id.', OS kuning m. 'id.', Du. koning c. 'id.', OHG kuning, kunig m. 'id.', G Konig m. 'id.'

'id.', Elfd. kunna pret.-pres. 'id.', OE cunnan, 3sg. can, 3pl. cunnon pret.-pres. 'id.', E can, OS kunnan, 3sg. kan, 3pl. kunnun pret.-pres. 'id.', Du. kunnen, 3sg. kan, 3pl. kunnen pret.-pres. kane/isfonzi 'to recognize, acknowledge' < 3sg. \*gneh₃-s-ti, 3pl. \*gnh₃-s-enti, (Kloekhorst 2008: 434ft); Gr. ytyvw(Jl(W 'to know', Lat. MDu. cute goes back to \*kiita-, but EDu. kiete together with Bav. kiitze and Scot kite continue a stem variant with an umlaut factor, possibly a collective formation \*kiitja-. The original meaning was 'soft part of the body' (Lat pulpa), which evolved into both 'calf and 'fish roe' (also cf. Teuth. cuyte 'spleen'). No certain etymology. The received comparison with Skt.guda- m. 'intestine, rectum, arse, vagina' (cf. Pokorny IEW: 393-8) is formally problematic because of its short \*u. Go. ligan s.v. 'id.', ON liggja s.v. 'id.', Far. liggja s.v. 'id.', Elfd. ligga s.v. 'id.', OE licgan s.v. 'id.', E to lie, OFri. lidza s.v. 'id.', OS liggian s.v. 'id.', Du. liggen sv. 'id.', OHG lickan, ligan s.v. 'id.', G liegen s.v. 'id.'

ON l<igr adj. 'id.', Far. l<igur adj. 'id.', Elfd. l<i.g adj. 'id.', OFri. lech, leich adj. 'id.', WFri. leech adj. 'id.', MDu. /age adj. 'id.', Du. laag adj. 'id.', MHG lrege adj. 'id.' (DRV). A lengthened grade adjective derived from \*leg(j)an- (q.v.). In view of the ja-stem forms in West Germanic, the word must probably be reconstructed as a u-stem. ME liih, E /ow is assumed to be a Nordic loanword.

grammatical_label: m. 'fleshy part of the body, calf
first_attestations: OE lira m. 'muscle, soft parts of the body', E lire 'flesh, muscle, brawn', MDu. liere f. 'fleshy part of the leg, calf
ON leka s.v. 'id.', Far. leka s.v. 'id.', E to leak, MDu. leken s./w.v. 'id.', OHG lehhan s.v. 'id.', MHG lechen s./w.v. 'id.'

Go. lekinon w.v. 'id.', ON lrekna w.v. 'id.', OE lcecnian w.v. 'id.', ODu. liiknon w.v. 'id.', OHG liichinon w.v. 'id.', MHG liichenen w.v. 'id.' (DRY). A verb derived from \*/ekja- 'doctor; leech' (q.v.). It served as the derivational base for ON, Icel. lreknir, Far. lrekni m. 'doctor' < \*lekinja-.

Go. lekeis m. 'doctor', ON lrekir m. 'id.', Da. lrege c. 'id.', OE lci!ce m. 'doctor; leech', E leech, OFri. letza m. 'doctor', ODu. lake m. 'leech', MDu. laecke 'id.', OHG liihhi, liichi m. 'id.' (Lw?). A word that is usually assumed to have been borrowed from Celtic \*legio-, cf. Olr. liaig 'doctor', before the great sound shifts. If the original meaning was 'blood-letter' ('leech'), however, the word can definitely be linked to the cluster of \*lekan- 'to leak' (q.v.), in particular to the formally close \*lekjon- 'rivulet' (q.v.). This would rather imply a Germanic origin. Also cf. Nw. lrekjar, Sw. liikare m. 'doctor' < \*lekjarjan- (= OCS /ekaro m. 'id.'), which was derived from a facti…
Far. lrekja f. 'well, waterhole, waterspout', Nw. lrekje f. 'rivulet, wooden water-pipe'
OE lippa m. 'id.', E lip, OFri. lippa m. 'id.', MDu. lippe f. 'id.', Du. lip c. 'id.'

Go. lisan s.v. 'to collect', ON lesa s.v. 'to grasp, pick up, to gather; to knit', OE lesan s.v. 'to gather, collect', E to lease ('to glean'), OS lesan s.Y. 'to gather', Du. lezen s.Y. 'to read; to select', OHG lesan

Go. letan s.v. 'id.', ON lcita s.v. 'id.', Far. lcita s.v. 'id.', Elfd. Mta w.v. 'id.', OE lii!tan s.v. 'id.', E to let, OFri. feta s.v. 'id.', OS Iatan s.v. 'id.', Du. laten s.v. 'id.', OHG lazan s.v. 'id.', G /assen s.v. 'id.'

ON lerir n. 'id.', Far. leaur n. 'id.', Elfd. leoer n. 'id.', OE leoer n. 'id.', E leather, OFri. !ether n. 'id.', MDu. /eder n. 'id.', Du. leer n. 'id.', OHG ledar n. 'id.', G Leder n. 'id.' (LW). A Celtic loanword, cf. Mir. lethar, MW l/edr m. 'leather' < PCelt. \*rplitro- < PIE \*pl-tro-. See also \*fella-.
Go. liufs adj. 'id.', ON ljufr adj. 'id.', OE /eof adj. 'id.', OS liof adj. 'id.', Du. lief adj. 'id.', OHG liub, liob adj. 'id.', G lieb adj. 'id.'
Go. liudan s.v. 'id.', OE leodan s.v. 'id.', OS liodan s.v. 'id.', OHG liotan s.v. 'id.'
ON ljor m. 'people, men', Far. undir-ljour m. 'weak-willed person', OFri. liud n. 'people', OS liud m. 'people', OHG liut m./n. 'people'

Go. liugan s.v. 'id.', ON ljuga s.v. 'id.', Far. Iugva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. liuoga s.v. 'id.', OE Ieogan s.v. 'id.', E to lie, OFri. liii.ga s.v. 'id.', OS liogan s.v. 'id.', Du. liegen s.v. 'id.', OHG liugan, liogan s.v. 'id.', G Iiigen s.v. 'id.'

Go. liuga f. 'id.' (WEUR) - Olr. luige n. 'oath' < \*lugh-io-. Also cf. Go. Iiugan w.v. 'to marry' < \*leugen- and OFri. logia, Ioia w.v. 'to arrange, allot; to join; to vouch; to marry' < \*lugen-. In view of Olr. luige, the original meaning of the root \*/ugh- should probably be reconstructed as 'to bind by oath'. It belongs to a wider range of Germanic-Celtic legal isoglosses (cf. \*aipa- 'oath'), which together are a reflection of a shared cultural sphere.
Go. liuhap f. 'id.', OE leoht n. 'id.', E light, OFri. liii.cht n. 'id.', WFri. ljocht n. 'id.', OS lioht n. 'id.', Du. licht n. 'id.', OHG lioht n. 'id.', G Licht n. 'id.'
ON lj6mi m. 'flash of light, radiance', Far. lj6mi m. 'light, glare, brilliance', OE leoma m. '(beam of) light, radiance', OS liomo m. 'shine'
Nw. ljon, lyn n. 'id.', Sw. ljung-eld 'id.', Gutn. Iiaugn n. 'id.', Da. lyn n. 'id.'
ON /joss adj. 'id.', Far. lj6sur adj. 'id.', Elfd. liuos adj. 'id.'

Go. ga-/ukan s.v. 'to close', us-lukan s.v. 'to pull (a sword)', ON Iuka, ljuka s.v. 'to close, finish', Far. Iuka s.v. 'to close; to weed, clean out', OE liican s.v. 'to plait', OFri. liika s.v. 'to close; to join; to pull', MDu. luken s.v. 'to weed; to pull; to draw a sword or knife; to stab(?)', Du. luiken s.v. 'to close', OHG liohhan s.v. 'to pull, pluck', liihhan s.v. 'to close', MHG liechen, liichen s.v. 'to close; to pull; to shirk, crouch; to slip'
Go. fra-liusan s.v. 'id.', OE for-/eosan s.v. 'id.', OFri. for-liasa, for-liesa s.v. 'id.', OS far-/iosan s.v. 'id.', Du. ver-liezen s.v. 'id.', G ver-lieren s.v. 'id.'

Ice!. lj6ski m. 'id.', OSw. liuske m. 'id.', Sw. ljumske c. 'id.', Elfd. liuoska f. 'id.', E Jeske 'flank', NFri. Hall. jaoska pl. 'id.', MLG lesche, /eische 'genitalia', MDu. liesche f. 'groin; membrane', Du. lies c. 'groin' (DRV). A word of disputed origin. Some have compared \*lawiz- (cf. De Vries 1971: 400), but in view of the formally identical Far. lj6ski m. 'light, glimmer, gleam', it is more likely that the word was derived from \*leuhsa- adj. 'light' (with the kan-suffix for body parts). The original meaning may then have been 'transparent or light skin'. MLG liese 'thin skin' is probably the same word (Teuchert 1972: 330), cf. Brandenburg /iese f. 'layer of fat around the kidneys'.

Go. liuts adj. 'deceitful, fraudulent', ON lj6tr adj. 'ugly', Far. lj6tur adj. 'ugly; bad', Elfd. liuot adj. 'bad, mean' (NEUR) - Cf. CS /ud'b m. 'foolish', Ru. lud adj. 'stupid, mad, crazy', SCr. lild 'stupid, crazy, immature' < \*loud-o- and Lith. liudnas m. 'sad' (with a secondary zero grade). A Germanic/Balto-Slavic isogloss. Also cf. ON fYti n. 'fault, blemish; disgrace' < \*leutja- and OE lot n. 'deceit, fraud' < \*luta-.
ON le, obi. lja m. 'id.', Far. lfggi m. 'id.', Nw. lja m. 'id.', sw. lie c. 'id.', Elfd. lja m. 'id.'

Go. lewjan w.v. 'id.', OE /<Ewan w.v. 'id.', ME be-lewen w.v. 'id.', OHG gi-liien, fir-Iii.en w.v. 'id.' (IE) - Close to Lith. liauti (lic1una, liauja) 'to stop', Latv. ,laiit 'to allow', refl. 'to stop, rely on', OPru. au-/aiit 'to die', Cz. leviti 'to reduce, give up, release', SCr. Jeviti 'to waste time, loaf. A factitive verb created to Go. lew n. 'occasion' < \*lewa- < \*Ieh₁u-o-. The original meaning was 'to let (go)', whence Germanic 'to give away, deliver, betray'. See also \*loma- 'betrayal'.

ON lif n. 'id.', Far. lfv n. 'id.', Elfd. laiv n. 'life', OE lifn. 'id.', E life, OFri. lifn. 'body; womb', OS lif n. 'life', Du. /ijfn. 'body', OHG lib m./n. 'body; life', G Leib m. 'body' (DRY). A neuter derived from the strong verb \*liban- ( q.v.).
Go. bi-leiban s.v. 'to stay', OE be-lifan s.v. 'to stay, remain', OFri. bi-liva s.v. 'id.', OS bi-I/ban s.v. 'id.', Du. blijven s.v. 'id.', OHG bi-liban s.v. 'to stay, stop', G bleiben s.v. 'id.'

Go. liban w.v. 'to live', ON lifa w.v. 'to live; to be left', Far. liva w.v. 'to live', Elfd. livci w.v. 'id.', OE libban w.v. 'id.', E to live, OFri. libba w.v. 'id.', OS libbian w.v. 'id.', Du. /even w.v. 'id.', OHG leben w.v. 'id.', G leben w.v. 'id.'

ON lifr f. 'id.', Far. livur f. 'id.', OSw. liver, /yr f. 'id.', Elfd. /yr f. 'id.', OE lifer f. 'id.', E liver, OFri. livere f. 'id.', Du. lever c. 'id.', OHG libara, lebara, lebera f. 'id.', G Leber f. 'id.'

Go. leih.Jan s.v. 'id.', ON lja w.v. 'to lend; to give, grant', Far. liggja s.v. 'to borrow, take out', OE /eon s.v. 'to lend', OS far-lihan s.v. 'id.', MDu. lien s.v. 'id.', OHG lihan s.v. 'id.', G leihen s.v. 'id.'
Go. leik n. 'id.', ON lik n. 'id.', Far. lik n. 'id.', Elfd. laik n. 'id.', OE lie n. 'id.', OFri. lfk n. 'id.', OS lfk n. 'id.', Du. lijk n. 'id.', OHG lih n./f. 'id.', G Leiche f. 'id.'
Go. ga-leiks adj. 'id.', ON glikr, likr adj. 'id.', Far. likur adj. 'id.', Elfd. laik adj. 'id.', OE ge-lfc adj. 'id.', E like, OFri. ge-lfk adj.

grammatical_label: 337 'id.', OS gi-lik adj. 'id.', Du. ge-lijk adj. 'id.', OHG ga-lfh adj. 'id.', G gleich adj. 'id.' ⇒ \*leig-o- (NEUR)
first_attestations: Lith. ljgus, lygus, l.atv. lidzs adj. 'flat, even, equal', OPru. po-ligu adv. 'similarly' < \*lig-o-; Lith. ljginti, Latv. lfdzinc'it 'to compare, even, equalize', OPru. liginton 'to judge' < \*lig-neh₂-. A Baltic-Germanic isogloss. The original meaning was 'equal, even', cf. OHG lfchon, EDu. lijcken w.v. 'to smoothen' < \*lfkojan- and EDu. lick adj. 'plane, even' < \*likka- < \*lig-n6-, whence EDu. licken w.v. 'to smoothen', Du. ge-likt adj. 'slick' < \*likkojan-. The latter formations, unlike the other Baltic and Germanic attestations, prove that the underlying root was \*Jig- rather than \*IiHg-. Also cf. the stative verb Go. (ga-)leikan, ON lfka, OE lfcian, OS /ikon, OHG lfchen w.v. 'to …
ON lfk n. 'id.', E leech, MLG lfk n. 'id.', Du. Iijk n. 'id.', MHG ge-leich n. 'joint, link'
OE liccian w.v. 'id.', E to lick, OS likkon w.v. 'id.', Du. likken w.v. 'id.', OHG leckon w.v. 'id.', G lecken w.v. 'id.'
MHG lecken w.v. 'to kick, jump'

MDu. /ecker adj. 'id.', Du. /ekker adj. 'id.', MHG lecker adj. 'id.', G /ecker adj. 'id.' (DRY). A ra-stem derived from the iterative \*likkon- 1 (q.v.): the original meaning seems to have been "lickable".

ON lfm n. 'lime', Far. lfm n. 'glue', OE lim m. 'lime', E lime, OS lim m. 'glue', Du. lijm c. 'id.', OHG /im m. 'clay; lime', G Leim m. 'glue; paste' Ǽ \*h₂leihi-mo- (IE) - Lat. limus m. 'mud, slime' < \*h₂leih₁-mo-. _ An mn-stem to the root \*h₂leihr 'to smear', for which cf. \*laiman-.
ON limr m. 'limb; joint', Far. limur m. 'id.', E limb Ǽ

Lith. fiemuo m. 'log, trunk, body' < \*/ei-mon-. A Nordic u-stem. In view of the closely related ON lim n. 'twig', OE lim n. 'limb; twig' and the ablauting ON /imi, Elfd. laime m. 'broom' < \*liman-, it is possible that the u-stem split off from the dat.pl. \*!imummiz < \*li-mr.;i-mis of a primary mn-stem \*liman- (cf. Kroonen 2011a: 62). See also \*lipu-.
ON find f. 'id.', Far. find f. 'id.', Elfd. find f. 'id.', OE find f. 'id.', E lime, OS /inda f. 'id.', Du. /inde c. 'id.', OHG linta f. 'id.', G Linde f. 'id.' Ǽ

Ru. dial. fut m. 'bark (of a limetree)' < \*lont-o-; Ru. lut'e n. 'young lime trees to be stripped of their bark', Ukr. fut'e n. 'bark of a lime tree, twigs of a willow' < \*lont-io-; Lith. lentil, Latv. /՚fita f. 'board, plank' < \*lent-eh₂-. A European tree name. Unrelated to \*finpa- 'mild, soft'.

ON lyng n. 'id.', Far. lyngur m. 'id.', OSw. liung n. 'id.', Sw. ljung n. 'id.', Elfd. /ingg n. 'id.' Ǽ \*lengwh.o- (NEUR) - OCS lpg'b m. 'meadow, underbrush', Ru. lug m. 'meadow' < \*longwh.o-. A Nordic word related to the aforementioned forms in Slavic (Charpentier 1906: 222). The meaning 'heather' probably developed from 'unused land, moor' (cf. \*haipi-). The alternative connection with Lith. /efikti 'to bend' and OCS lpk'b adj. 'bent' (Torp 1909: 360) is semantically unattractive. For a potential zero-grade, cf. Sw. dial. /ung-b/Ot, Nw. dial. lunge-blaut adj. 'soaked' < quasi-PGm. \*lungwa-blauta-.
OHG gi-lingan s.v. 'id.', MHG lingen, ge-lingen s.v. 'id.', G ge-lingen s.v. 'id.'

Go. /eihts adj. 'id.', ON Iettr adj. 'light, easy; nimble, active', Far. /CEttur adj. 'light, easy', Elfd. litt adj. 'id.', OE liht, leoht adj. 'id.', E light, OFri. licht adj. 'id.', Du. licht adj. 'id.', OHG liht adj. 'easy', G /eicht adj. 'id.' (DRV). A to-stem adjective derived from \*lingwan- (q.v.).

ON linnr adj. 'mild, soft', Far. linur adj. 'soft, slack, limp', Elfd. fin adj. 'soft', OE lio, /ioe adj. 'id.', E lithe, OS /ithi adj. 'id.', OHG find, lindi adj. 'mild, tender', G ge-linde adj. 'delicate, soft'

Go. lisan\*, 3sg. /ais 'id.' (EUR). A marginally attested preterite-present, 3sg. \*/aise, 3pl. \*lisunjJ, to a root \*leis-, cf. Go. lubja-leis adj. 'having knowledge about drugs' < \*-lisa-. The meaning of the verb must in view of the obviously related \*laisti- 'track; model' (q.v.) have shifted from an aspectually perfective 'to have traced'. See also \*/aizjan-, \*liznon- and \*listi-.

Go. list f. 'cunning', ON list f. 'art, craft', Far. list f. 'art; cunning', OE list f. 'art, skill; trick', OFri. list f. 'knowledge; deceit', OS list f. 'craftiness, cleverness', Du. list c. 'trick', OHG list m./f. 'knowledge', G List f. 'cunning'

Go. leitils adj. 'id.', ON liti/l adj. 'id.', Far. lftil adj. 'id.', Elfd. litn, /isl- adj. 'id.', OE ljtel, lite/, Nrth. lyttil adj. 'id.', E little, OS luttil adj. 'id.', Du. lutte/ adj. 'few', OHG liizil, luzzil adj. 'id.', MHG liitzel adj. 'id.' (GM). The default Germanic word for 'small'. The derivation of the word is still problematic. The suffix \*-ila- is paralleled by the antonym \*mekila- 'big, much' (q.v.). Equally parallel is the lack of this suffix in the closely related OE lyt, OS fut, MHG liiz adj. 'few' < \*liitja- - \*luttja- and the antonym ME mich(e), much(e}, E much < \*mekja-. The radical alternation of \*/ft-, \*liit- and \*lutt- remains unexplained, however. Until this…

Go. ga-/eipan s.v. 'to go, travel', ON lfoa s.v. 'to go, pass by; to pass away, to elapse', Far. /foa s.v. 'to move smoothly, stride; to pass (away), elapse, proceed', Elfd. /aioa s.v. 'to go by, pass', OE liOan s.v. 'to go, sail', OFri. litha s.v. 'to go', OS /fthan s.v. 'to go, leave', Du. lijden s.v. 'to suffer', OHG lidan s.v. 'to go, pass; to tolerate, suffer', G leiden s.v. 'to suffer' Ǽ \*/eit-e- (IE) - ToAB litii- 'to pass on' < \*/eit-e-; YAv. irii9iieiti 'to die, pass away' < \*lit-ie-; Gr. AOLTI'J f. 'tomb' < \*loit-eh₂-; Lat. /ftus, -oris n. 'sea-shore, coast' < \*leit-es-. A strong verb to the PIE root \*leit- 'to go (down)'. See also \*laidjan-.

Go. lipus m. 'id.', ON lirJr m. 'joint', Far. liour m. 'joint; knot', Elfd. lio m. 'id.', OE lio m./n. 'joint', OFri. lith n. 'joint, articulation', OS /ith m. 'id.', Du. lid n. 'id.', OHG lid m.fn. 'id.', G Glied n. 'id.' (NEUR). A derivation created to the same Baltic-Germanic root \*lei- as found in \*/imu- (q.v.). Also cf. ON liougr adj. 'smooth, lithe', OFri. /ethoch adj. 'free', OE lioig adj. 'flexible', MHG /edic adj. 'free', G ledig, Du. /eeg adj. 'empty'.
Go. /eipu n. 'id.', ON lio n. 'id.', OE /io n. 'id.', OFri. lfth n. 'id.', OS lith n. 'id.', OHG lid m./n. 'id.'

OE leornian w.v. 'id.', E to learn, OFri. lirna, lerna w.v. 'id.', OS lfnon w.v. 'id.', OHG lemon, lernen, lirnen w.v. 'id.', G lernen w.v. 'id.' (EUR). An inchoative verb continuing a medio-passive factitive neh₂-present \*lis-nh₂- 'to make oneself know'. See also \*lisan-.

Go. lofa m. 'id.', ON /6fi m. 'id.', Ice!. 16fi m. 'id.', Far. /6gvi m. 'palm of the hand; small glove', /abbi m. 'paw', Nw. /abb m. 'paw, big hand', ME /Ove 'palm of the hand', OHG lappo m., /affa f. 'palm of the hand; blade of an oar', MHG /affe f. 'id.'
ON lcekr m. 'brook, rivulet', Far. lږkur m. 'brooklet' (DRV). An i-stem with a secondary lengthened grade. Cf. without umlaut: Elfd. luok m. 'puddle'. Derived from the strong verb \*lekan- ( q.v.).

Ice!. 16mur m. 'id.' (DRV). A mo-stem created to the root of \*lewjan- (q.v.), to be reconstructed as \*loh₁u-mo- (with the regular development \*ou > \*o). Further cf. Ice!. luma w.v. 'to conceal' < \*lh₁um-ehr (through \*liime- < \*luh₁-m-eh₁- with pretonic shortening of the \*ii).

MDu. loem adj. 'dull', EDu. loom adj. 'sluggish', MHG liieme adj. 'dull, pliant, mild' (DRV). An adjective derived from the verbal root of \*lamjan- (q.v.) with a lengthened grade: originally a gerundive?
ON /of n. 'praise; permission', Far. lov n. 'id.', Elfd. luv n. 'id.', OE lofn. 'praise', OFri. lofn. 'id.', Du. /ofn. 'id.', OHG lob n. 'id.', G Lob n. 'id.'

Go. lubja-leisei f. 'witchcraft', ON lyf n. 'medicine, healing herb', Elfd. lyv n. 'remedy', OE lyb n. 'medicine, drug, potion', MDu. lubbe n. 'rennet', OHG luppi n. 'poison, witchcraft', G Alem. lupp n. 'rennet'

Icel. looa w.v. 'to hang down, stick to', lodda w.v. 'to dangle', Far. looa w.v. 'to hang down', Nw. loda w.v. 'to hang down, stick to' (GM). Also cf. OHG lotar adj. 'loose, light-hearted, lazy' < \*ludra- and MDu. Joi, Du. Jui adj. 'lazy' < \*lu/Jja-.
Go. ludja f. 'id.', OHG ant-lutti n. 'id.', G Antlitz n. 'id.'

Go. luftus m. 'air', ON loft n. 'air, sky; loft', Far. loft n. 'air; ceiling, loft', Elfd. /opt n. 'id.' ( < \*lufta-), OE lyft m./f./n. 'air, sky', OFri. lufte, luft f. 'id.', OS luft f. 'air', Du. lucht c. 'air, sky', OHG luft m./f. 'id.', G Luft f. 'id.'

OE lygge adj. 'id.', OS luggi adj. 'deceitful', OHG lucki adj. 'id.', MHG liige, liicke adj. 'id.' (DRY). A WGm. gerundive \*Jugja- (continuing older \*lugi-) to the strong verb \*leugan- 'to lie' (q.v.). Derivationally parallel to \*flugi- 'fledged' to \*fleugan'to fly' ( q .v.).

Far. lygn f. 'id.', Elfd. lyngen f. 'id.', OE lygen f. 'id.', OFri. leine f. 'id.', OS lugina f. 'id.', Du. leugen c. 'id.', OHG lugina f. 'id.', G Liige f. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from \*leugan- with the same suffix as in \*pakin6- 'cover', for which see \*paka-.
OSw. 16 f. 'id.', Elfd. luo f. 'id.'
OE lox m. 'id.', Du. los c. 'id.', OHG luhs m. 'id.', G Luchs m. 'id.'

ON lok n. 'id.', Far. lok n. 'id.', Sw. dial. /uk 'id.', EDa. lug 'id.' (ORV). The etymological link with Gr. t.uyo<; 'twig' (e.g. Falk/Torp 1960: 653) is formally apt, but since the Germanic word was derived directly from to strong verb \*leukan- - \*liikan- 'to pull' (q.v.), the formal and semantic match with the Greek word is likely to be coincidental.

ON /okkr m. 'lock of hair', Far. /okkur m. 'id.', OE foe m. 'id.', E lock, Du. lok c. 'id.', OHG foe m. 'id.', G Locke f. 'id.' (ORV). An a-stem that in view of Cimb. lock m. 'tuft of wool' must have been derived from the strong verb \*leukan- - \*liikan- 'to pull' (cf. also \*luka'weeds'). There is no direct link with Lith. lugnas adj. 'flexible' (pace Pokorny IEW: 685-6).

A word that was inflected as an n-stem in the singular and as a root noun in the plural. Presumably, the original paradigm \*manna, pl. \*mannaniz developed into \*manna, \*manniz by regular Proto-Germanic syncope of the unstressed vowel in the sequence \*-nnan-. The n-stem endings were then removed from the singular in most languages except for Gothic and OE manna. The etymology of the word is disputed. Some connect Skt manu'man' (cf. A plant name that in view of the plant's preference for brackish marshes was probably derived from \*mari- 'sea' ( q.v.) with the diminutive suffix \*-kan-. Since the Nordic forms are likely to have been borrowed from Old English or Low German, the word originally had an Ingvaeonic distribution. This is in accordance with the natural occurrence of the plant along the North Sea coast and the West Baltic. Nw. dial. eld-merkje m. 'willowherb, Epilobium' does not belong here. OE m<es(t)ling, m<eslen(n) n. 'id.', OFri. mesken adj. 'made of brass', MDu. messinc, missinc n. 'id.', Du. messing n. 'id.', MHG messinc, missinc, moschinc m. 'id.' (GM). Etymology unclear. It is further clear that the Germanic word is related to the derivationally more primitive MHG mess(e), MDu. mes n. 'brass' < \*masja- and MHG messe f. 'Jump of metal' < \*masjo-. first_attestations: Lat mentum n. 'chin', MW mant m. 'mandible, gums' < \*mnt-o-. A Germanic and Italo-Celtic o-stem. It is further possible that the root \*ment- continues \*stment-, for which see \*stimno-. The root may further be identified in Gr. μacraoμm 'to chew, bite' ( < quasi PIE \*mnt-i-eh₂-ie-) and Lat mando 'to chew' < \*mnt-neh₂- (with 1. dissimilation of the first n; 2. schwa secundum in the resulting form \*mt-na-; and 3. prenasalization under Thurneysen's law). The alternative connection with Hitt meni n. 'face, cheek' ( < \*men-ihi-, Rieken 1999: 56f.) can only be maintained if the root was \*men- rather than \*ment-, which seems less likely.

Another possibility is to assume that the PGm. labiovelar developed from a laryngeal before \*u, which would allow for a connection with \*no- 'ship' < \*neh₂u- (Kortlandt 1988b: 2 56).

'id.', OHG nackot adj. 'id.', MHG nacket, nackent adj. 'id.', G nackt adj. 'id.' ʀ \*nogw-e/ont- (IE) - Close to Lat. niidus adj. 'id.' < \*nogw-edh-o-(?); further cf. Hitt. nekumantadj. 'id.' < \*negw-m-ont-(?); Skt. nagna- adj. 'naked' < \*nogw-no-; YAv. maynaadj. 'id.' (from "\*nwog-no-" with labial metathesis), cf. Gr. yuμv6c; adj. 'id.' < "\*gonw-no-" < \*nogw-no-; Olr. nocht, W noeth adj. 'id.' < \*nogw-to-; Lith .. nuogas, Latv. nuogs adj. 'id.', OCS nag'b, Ru. nag6j, SCr. obs. nag adj. 'id.' …

Go. namo, pl. namna n. 'id.', ON nafn n. 'id.', Far. navn n. 'id.', Elfd. nammen n. 'id.', OE nama m. 'id.', E name, OFri. noma m. 'id.', OS namo m. 'id.', Du. naam c. 'id.', OHG namo m. 'id.', G Name m. 'id.'

Go. ana-nanpjan w.v. 'to dare, take courage', ON nenna w.v. 'to be inclined, willing', Far. nenna w.v. 'to have the heart to', OE neoan w.v. 'to have courage, dare', OFri. netha w.v. 'to dare', OS ncithian w.v. 'id.', OHG nenden w.v. 'id.', MHG nenden,ge-nenden w.v. 'id.' (DRY). In view of the root-final \*p, this verb is probably a factitive to an unattested adjective \*nanpa- 'daring' < \*n6nt-o-; the causative to \*ninpan- would have been \*nandjan- < \*nont-eie-. Further cf. OE noo, OHG nand 'rashness' < \*nanpa-.

OE nearu adj. 'id.', E narrow, WFri. near adj. 'id.', OS naro, naru adj. 'id.', MDu. nare, naer adj. 'id.', Du. naar adj. 'disagreeable, dreary' ʀ \*neh₂r-u- (NEUR) - Latv. nars m. 'clamp' < \*noh₂r-o-(?). An "Ingvaeonic" adjective, possibly related to Latv. nars. If this is correct, the wa-stem may have developed from \*neh₂r-u- by pretonic shortening. Also cf. Nw. naur m. 'wretch' < \*narwa-, ON nara w.v. 'to dwindle' < \*narojan- and the ablauting Nw. nor n. 'sound, canal' < \*nora- < \*noh₂r-o-.

ON nps f. 'nostril', Far. nf!Js f., nasar pl. 'nose', OSw. nascer f.pl. 'id.', OE nosu f. 'id.', E nose, OFri. nose f. 'id.', OS nasa-druppo m. 'cold', Du. neus c. 'nose', OHG nasa f. 'id.', G Nase f. 'id.' ʀ \*n՗2-s-eh₂- (IE) - Skt. nasci- f. 'nose', YAv. n<iT)ha nom.acc.du.(?) 'id.', OP nciham acc. 'id.', Lat. naris f. 'nostril', pl. 'nose', Lith. n6sis, Latv. nass f. 'id.' < \*neh₂-s-; CS nos'b, Ru. nos, SCr. nos m. 'id.' < \*nh₂-es-. The IE word for 'nose'. It is traditionally reconstructed as PIE \*nas- with a-vocalism (Pokorny IEW: 755), but Kortlandt 1985a: 119 postulated an ablauting s-stem nom. \*neh₂-s, gen. \*nhrs-6s, acc. \*nhres-m in order to account for the ablaut of e.g. Lith.…

ODu. nat adj. 'id.', Du. nat adj. 'id.', OHG naz adj. 'id.', G nafi adj. 'id.' (IE?) - Skt. uncitti, 3pl. undcinti 'to well up, moisten' < \*u-n-ed-ti, \*u-n-d-enti. Also cf. the factitive Go. natjan, OHG nazzen, nezzen, G netzen w.v. 'to make wet, wetten'. The origin of the word is uncertain, but it is possible that it continues \*ynod-o-, i.e. an o-stem adjective derived from the n-present 3sg. \*uned-ti, 3pl. \*und-enti (Weigand 1909-10: II, 276), as the initial sequence \*yn- could have been simplified to \*n in PGm.
Go. nati n. 'id.', ON net n. 'id.', Far. net n. 'id.', Elfd. m;t n. 'id.', OE net n. 'id.', E net, OS netti n. 'id.', Du. net n. 'id.', OHG nezzi n. 'id.', G Netz n. 'id.'
ON netja f. 'id.', Far. netja f. 'id.', OFri. nette, nitte f. 'retina', EDu. net 'id.', G Netzen f. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from \*natja- or the underlying root \*nat-.
ON nata f. 'spear', Far. nota f. 'nettle', Nw. nata f. 'id.', Gutn. nata f. 'id.'
ON naust n. 'id.', Far. neyst n. 'id.', Nw. naust(r) n. 'id.' (DRV). Derived from \*no- < \*neh₂u- 'boat' (q.v.) with the suffix \*-sta- (variant \*-stra-) < PIE \*-sth₂-o-.

ON naut n. 'cattle, ox', OE neat n. 'ox, cow, cattle', OFri. ncit m. 'animal', OS notil n. 'domestic animal' ( = dim. \*nauti/a-), MDu. noot n. 'cattle, cow', OHG noz n. 'cattle', G dial. Nofl n. 'id.' (DRV). An a-stem usually derived from \*neutan- 'to make use of (q.v.), an etymology that implies a primary meaning 'utilizable cattle', cf. G Nutz-vieh 'domestic cattle'. Another possibility is to connect MDu. noten w.v. 'to mate (of animals)' < \*(h)nuton-.
Go. naups f. 'id.', ON nauo f. 'need, difficulty, distress', Far. neyo f. 'id.', OE nied f. 'id.', E need, OFri. ned f. 'id.', OS nOd f. 'id.', Du. nood c. 'id.', OHG not f. 'id.', G Not f. 'id.'
Go. nous m. 'id.', ON mfr m. 'id.'

OE nci!dre, nceddre f. 'id.' (also Nrth. neder m. 'id.' < \*nedra-), E adder, WFri. njirre c. 'id.', EFri. needer f. 'id.', OS niidra, niidara f. 'id.', MDu. nadre, addre, adder f. 'id.', Du. adder c. 'id.', OHG nat(a)ra f. 'id.', G Natterf. 'id.'

ON nefi m. 'relative', OE nefa m. 'nephew, grandson, stepson', OFri. neva, newa, nia(u)wa, nowa m. 'nephew', MDu. neve m. 'relative, nephew, cousin', Du. nee/ c. 'nephew, cousin', OHG nefo, nevo m. 'grandson, descendant, cousin, nephew', G Neffe m. 'nephew'

Go. nelu adv. 'id.', ON nci- pref. 'id.', OE neah adv./prep. 'id.', E nigh, OFri. nei prep. 'to', OS niih adv. 'id.', Du. na adv. 'id.', OHG niih adj. 'id.', G nahe adv./prep. 'id.' (DRV). A lengthened-grade adjective derived from the PIE root \*h₂ne/(- 'to reach', for which see \*nugan- 'to suffice'.

Go. niman s.v. 'id.', ON nema s.v. 'to take (possession of); to touch; to learn', Far. nema s.v. 'to touch; to learn', OE niman s.v. 'to take', OFri. niman s.v. 'id.', OS neman, niman s.v. 'id.', Du. nemen s.v. 'id.', OHG neman s.v. 'id.', G nehmen s.v. 'id.'

Go. anda-nems adj. 'pleasant', ON n<Emr adj. 'quick at learning; deprived of, Far. n<Emur adj. 'apt, quick', MDu. ge-name adj. 'pleasant', OHG niimi adj. 'acceptable', G ge-nehm adj. 'acceptable, convenient' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to \*neman- (q.v.).
