ON nykr m. 'hippopotamus, water being', Far. nykur m. 'water elf, OE nicor m. 'hippopotamus; water-monster', MDu. nicker m. 'water-sprite', OHG nihhus, nichus m.fn. 'water-sprite, crocodile', MHG niches, nickes m./n. 'water-sprite', G Nix m. 'river merman' (IE). The geminate of OHG nickus points to \*nikwasa- < \*nigw-os-o- with a vowel behind the labiovelar. In combination with the Verner variant ON nykr, OE nicor < \*nikwaza-, it can therefore be surmised that the original inflection was athematic, e.g. PIE nom. \*nfgw-os, gen. \*nigw·s-6s, acc. \*nigw-6s-m > PGm. \*nikwo, \*nikuzaz, \*nikwasun. The stem \*nikwas- gave rise to the feminine derivative OHG nickessa, MHG nixe, G Nixe f. 'fema…
OHG gi-nindan s.v. 'id.'
OE nest n. 'id.', E nest, Du. nest n. 'id.', OHG nest n. 'id.', G Nest n. 'id.'
ON nior adv. 'id.', Far. niour adv. 'id.', Elfd. nioer adv. 'id.', OE niper adv. 'id.', OFri. nither adv. 'id.', OS nidar adv. 'id.', Du. neer adv. 'id.', OHG nidar adv. 'id.', G nieder adv. 'id.'
ON n6r m. 'id.', Icel. n6r m. 'tempering trough', Nw. no, nu 'wooden bowl'

Go. ga-nohs adj. 'id.', ON gn6gr, n6gr adj. 'id.', Far. n6gvur adj. 'much', OE ge-noh adj. 'id.', E enough, OFri. enoch adv. 'id.', OS gi-noh, gi-nog adj. 'id.', Du. ge-noeg adv. 'id.', OHG gi-nuog adj., adv. 'id.', G ge-nug adv. 'id.' (DRY). A lengthened grade adjective derived from \*nugan- 'to suffice' ( q.v.).
MDu. noemen w.v. 'id.', Du. noemen w.v. 'id.', MHG be-niiemen w.v. 'id.' (DRY).
Go. no ta\* m. 'afterdeck, stem, rear of a ship'

ON ncera w.v. 'id.', Far. mJra w.v. 'to bring up, rear; to increase' (DRV). A causative created to an unattested o-grade intensive verb \*nasan- < \*n6s-e-, cognate of \*nesan- 'to be saved, cured' and \*nazjan- 'to save, cure, feed' ( q.v.). The ablaut proportion was productive, cf. the creation of \*forjan- 'to lead' to \*faran- 'to go' and \*goljan- 'to greet' to \*galan- 'to shout' (q.v.). Unrelated to ON nara w.v. 'to dwindle' \*naro(ja)n- (pace De Vries 1962: 405) as the required \*nazojan- would have given ON \*\*nera by R-mutation.

Go. nu adv. 'id.', ON nu adv. 'id.', Far. nu, obs. nugv adv. 'id.', Elfd. nq adv. 'id.', OE nii adv. 'id.', E now, OFri. nii adv. 'id.', MDu. nu adv. 'id.', Du. nu, nou adv. 'id.', OHG nu, nun adv. 'id.', G nun adv. 'id.'
Go. ga-nauhan, 3sg. ga-nah pret.-pres. 'id.', OE ge-nugan, 3sg. ge-neah pret.-pres. 'id.', OHG gi-nugan\*, 3sg. ge-nah pret.-pres. 'id.'
ON noror adv. 'id.', Far. norour adv. 'id.', Elfd. norter adv. 'id.', OFri. norther adv. 'id.', MDu. norder adv. 'id.', MHG norder adv. 'id.'

Go. un-nuts adj. 'useless, unavailing', OE nyt adj. 'useful', OFri. nette adj. 'id.', OS nutti adj. 'id.', MDu. nutte adj. 'id.', OHG nuzzi adj. 'id.', MHG niitze adj. 'id.' (ORV). An i-stem adjective (gerundive?) to the strong verb \*neutan- (q.v.). In WGm., it was remodeled into aja-stem, which again gave rise to OE nyttian, OFri. bi-netta, OHG nuzzen w.v. 'to make use of < \*nutjojan-. Also cf. OHG nuz, G Nutzen m. 'use' < \*nutja- and ON nyt, OE nytt f. 'id.' < \*nutjo-.
Nw. not(t)a w.v. 'to drive (a horse)'

OS obian w.v. 'to celebrate', MDu. oeven w.v. 'to practice', OHG uoben, uoppen w.v. 'to practice, exercise; to celebrate', G iiben w.v. 'to practice' (ORV). A causative with a secondary long \*6, derived from the PIE root \*h₃ep- 'to labor, work'. With the same vocalism, cf. OHG uoba f. 'festivity' < \*6b6- and OHG uobo m. 'farmer' < \*oban-. See also \*ajla-.
OE ofer, i5for m. 'edge, border, margin', MDu. oever n./m./f. 'shore', Du. oever c. 'shore', MHG uover n. 'shore', G Ufer n. 'bank, shore'
Go. ogjan w.v. 'id.', ON regja w.v. 'id.', OE on-cegan w.v. 'to fear' (IE). The causative to \*agan- (q.v.).

OE oxn f. 'id.', OHG uohsana f. 'id.' (IE). The dialects contain a variety of different formations. In my view, the no doubt closely related ON 6st f., 6str m. 'armpit' and especially OE 6xta, i5custa m. 'id.' < \*ohsta(n)- show that OHG uohsana and OE i5xn developed from \*i5hstni5-, a feminine formation derived from a primary n-stem \*i5hstan-; the heavy cluster \*-hst(n)- must then have become simplified to \*-hsn- in West Germanic, while Nordic \*i5hsta-, on the other hand, lost the h. The additional variants OHG uohisa, MHG uochse, uechse f. 'armpit' < \*i5hsji5n- and MHG uohsn(e}, uehsen n. 'id.' < \*i5hs(t)nja- are secondary. The formations mentioned here all have long \*o, which prob…
ON 6ss m. 'id.', Far. 6si m. 'id.', Nw. os m./n. 'estuary, hole in the ice' (cf. dial. gse n. 'id.' < \*osja-)

\*oz- would be a clear indication of an originally mobile s-stem, e.g. \*h₁;3eh₁;3-S, \*h₁13h₁;3-es-6s. Such a paradigm is directly supported by Hitt. ajis, ais, gen. isfas < \*h₁eh₃-es (secondary for \*h₁eh₃-s), gen. \*h₃h₁-es-6s. Note that the lack of an initial b in the nominative points to \*h₁eh₃-(e}s, while the vocalism of the genitive at least superficially seems to reflect \*h₃hi-es-6s (cf. Rieken 1999: 186) with the reversed order of the laryngeals. Kloekhorst 2008: 166, on the other
ON 6oal n. 'ancestral property, patrimony, inheritance', OE ooel m. 'home, native country', OS othil n. 'home', OHG uodal n. 'ancestral property' (DRY). A vṛddhi-formation to \*ajJala- (q.v.). p

ON padda f. 'id.', Far. padda f. 'id.', Elfd. padda f. 'id.', ME pade, padde 'toad; toadstool, mushroom', E paddock, MDu. padde f. 'toad', Du. pad c. 'id.' (GM). The variation of ME pade with padde (continuing OE \*pade, \*padde) may point to an n-stem with a consonantal alternation, nom. \*pado, gen. \*paddaz. It gave rise to the feminine formation \*pad(d)jon-, cf. MDu., WPhal. pedde f. 'toad' < \*pad(d)jon- and probably also to the verb Du. dial. padden w.v. 'to crawl', if not the other way around. The word cannot be related to Lat. buf6 'toad' as if continuing \*bodh-on-. In agreement with known folkloristic notions concerning the animal, the latter word was actually derived from \*gwh₃-…
OE pcegel 'wine-vessel', E pail, MDu. pegel, peil m./f. 'knob in vessel for liquid goods to measure their contents', Du. pegel c. 'peg, pin, icicle'
Go. paida f. 'tunic, vest, shirt', OE piid f. 'coat, cloak', OS peda f. 'garment', OHG pfeit f. 'coat', MHG pfeit f. 'shirt'

ME packe 'id.', E pack, MLG pack n. 'id.', MDu. pac n. 'id.', Du. pak n. 'id.' (EUR/NIE) - Gr. q>UKEAO<) 'bundle' < \*bhak-elo-; Lat. biiiulus m. 'porter, carrier' (cf. Prov. baga 'bundle', Span. baga 'capsule of flaxseed') < \*bagi-e/o-(?); ?W beich m. 'load, weight, burden' < PCelt \*bakki- 1 \*baski-. A "Middle Germanic" word with an obscure prehistory. The restriction of the word to the westernmost Indo-European languages speaks against a PIE origin. This is confirmed by the irregular correlation between PGm. \*pakkan- < \*bakk-, Gr. q>cXKEAO<) < \*bhak- and Lat. \*baga < \*bag-. Even within Germanic itself, the word corresponds irregularly to ON baggi m. 'pack, bundle', which points to …
OE prefJ, pafJ m. 'id.', E path, OFri. path, pad n. 'id.', MDu. pat m./n. 'id.', Du. pad n. 'id.', OHG pfad m./n. 'id.', G Pfad m. 'id.'

OE pipa m. 'pith', WFri. piid 'pith, stone (of a fruit)' (< \*pidan-?), EDu. pee 'skirwort, skirret, (edible) root', peen 'bent grass, tall oat grass', Du. peen c. 'carrot' (Lw?). A West Germanic word with an indirect attestation in Far. piaa w.v. 'to pick the meat from the bone' < \*pipojan-. The alternation of the stem variants \*pipan- and \*pittan- (\*pippan-?) points to an n-stem nom. \*pipo, gen. \*pittaz < \*bft-on, \*bit-n-6s. In view of the suffixation of Du. peem 'root (of grasses)', dial. pessem, pettem 'root, field horsetail' (\*-pp- > -ss-/-tt- in the Du. dialects), it is further conceivable that this n-stem continues an older mn-stem \*pipmon, \*pittaz from \*bft-mon, \*bit-mn-…

ON plagg n. 'cloth', MDu. placke, plagge f. 'rag; sod, turf, Du. plag c. 'sod', plak c. 'slice, patch', MHG pjlacke m. 'rag, patch, stain' (GM). A number of nominal formations probably belonging to an iterative verb \*plakk/gon-, cf. MDu. placken w.v. 'to slap', Sw. plagg 'beating', LG plegel, G Swab. Pjlegel m. 'flail' < \*plagila-. If the meaning 'sod' as attested in MDu. is old enough, the root may have served as the basis for \*ploga- 'plow' (q.v.), whose etymology is usually considered to be obscure.
OS plegan s.v. 'id.', MDu. plien, plegen s.v. 'to guarantee, take care of, be accustomed to', OHG pjlegan s.v. 'to take care of, maintain, nurse', G pjlegen w.v. 'id.'

OE pliht m./f. 'danger, damage', E plight, OFri. plicht f. 'caution, responsibility', MDu. plicht f. 'obligation', Du. plicht c. 'id.', OHG pjliht f. 'fervor, caution, practice, manner', G Pjlicht f. 'obligation, responsibility' (ORV). A ti-stem to the root of the strong verb \*plegan- (q.v.). The sometimes compared OS pletta, MLG plicht, MDu., Du. plecht, OHG pjlihta f. 'small (cabin on a) fore-deck' (also cf. OE plihtere 'look-out man in the prow') is unrelated. I would instead like to compare this word to the obscure MLG pligen 'to steer carefully(?)' and E obs. ply 'to beat up against the wind; to tack, work to windward; to direct one's course (in a ship), steer', which ultimately go bac…

ON pl6gr m. 'id.', Ice!. pl6gur m. 'id.', Far. pl6gv n. 'jointer plane; groove in a board; plow'; pl6gv f. 'plow', OE plOh m. 'id.', E plow, OFri. ploch m. 'id.', MDu. ploech f./m. 'plow; team, group', Du. ploeg c. 'id.', OHG pjluog m. 'plow; livelihood', G Pflug m. 'plow' (DRV). The etymology of 'plow', first attested in the enigmatic plaumorati mentioned by Pliny, is disputed (see e.g. Puhvel 1964 ). It is believed by many to be derived from the verb PGm. \*plehan- - \*plegan- ( q.v.), in which case there must have been a shift of meaning from 'to be used to' to 'live' to 'to plow' (cf. OHG pjluog 'livelihood'). However, the opposite semantic evolution from 'plow' to 'livelihood' cannot be…

OE plyccean w.v. 'to pluck', ME plicchen w.v. 'to pull, snatch, pluck', E obs. to plitch 'id.', MDu. p/ocken, plucken w.v. 'to pluck', Du. plukken w.v. 'to pick, pluck' (GM). Ajan-verb derived from the iterative \*pluk{k)on- (q.v.).

OE ploccian w.v. 'to pull off, cull', E to pluck, OFri. her-plokkia w.v. 'to pluck out hair', WFri. ploats(j)e, pl6k(j)e w.v. 'to pluck', LG ploken 'to pluck' (Diefenbach 1867: 77) (GM). An iterative verb whose paradigm in view of the derived MLG plugge, plucke m. 'wooden nail, peg', EDu. plugge 'wooden nail, plug, bung, stopper', Du. plug c. 'id.', MHG pjloc m. 'stake, peg', G Pjlock m. 'id.' < \*plugg/kkan- is to be reconstructed as 3sg. \*plukkopi, 3pl. \*plugunanpi. OSw. trce-p/iJgger 'peg' and Nw. pligg m. 'peg, pin' have been adduced in support of an ablauting formation \*plegja- / \*plegwa-, but these forms are more likely to have been adopted from Low German. See also \*plukkjan-.
OE pol m. 'id.', E pool, OFri. pol m. 'id.', MDu. poel m. 'id.', Du. poel c. 'id.', OHG pfuol m. 'id.', G Pfuhl m. 'id.'

LG prakken w.v. 'to squeeze', Du. prakken w.v. 'to mash' (NEUR). An iterative verb that can hardly be separated from the strong verb \*prangan- (q.v.) with the same meaning. I therefore reconstruct the formation as \*brogh-neh₂- without the n, which was apparently an infix. Also cf. LG pranke 'claw' < \*prankan- < \*brongh-n-.
Go. ana-praggan s.v. 'to press upon, oppress, trouble', MLG prangen w.v. 'to press, clamp', MDu. prangen w.v. 'to press, squeeze; to wrestle, fight', Du. prangend ptc. 'urgent'

Ice!. prj6nn m. 'knitting needle', Far. pr6nur m. 'large pin, fixing pin', Nw. prj6na f. 'fixing pin', Da. pryne c. 'needle for stringing fish; ring for pigs; eel prong', OE preon m. 'pin, brooch, fastening', E preen 'decorative pin, brooch, clasp', MLG pren(e), preme m. 'awl', MDu. priem(e) m. 'needle, awl, pin', Du. priem c. 'awl', MHG pfrieme m. 'id.', G Pfriem m. 'id.'

OE prician w.v. 'id.', E to prick, MLG pricken w.v. 'id.', Du. prikken w.v. 'id.', MHG pfrecken w.v. 'id.' (GM). A NWGm. word, perhaps ultimately an onomatopoeia. The variation of \*prikon- vs. \*prikkon- points to an original iterative 3sg. \*prikkopi, 3pl. \*prikunanpi < \*brig-neh₂-, but the root of this formation cannot possibly be

ON poki m. 'id.', OE pocca, pohha m. 'purse', E pough, MDu. poke 'bag (for wool)', EDu. poke 'hairshirt, crop', Du. obs. pook 'bag', G Pfoch m. 'id.' (GM?). An exclusively Germanic word that surfaces with many different shapes in the different dialects. The variation of the stems \*puhhan-, \*pukkan- and \*pukan- can nevertheless be resolved by reconstructing a paradigm nom. \*puho, gen. \*pukkaz < \*bUk-on, \*buk-n-6s. No further etymology. The link with Pol. buczyc sit; 'to puff oneself up' < \*bouk-eie- is technically possible, but remains uncertain in view of the restriction of this form to Slavic. In addition, the possibly related Nw. poka f. 'pigskin, sward' < \*pukon- could point to a…

MLG puche, pughe f. 'blanket, covering', LG puch 'bed', MDu. puuc n. '(high quality) sheeting' (GM). An apparently WGm. word with no clear etymology. The origin of the variation between \*puk- and \*pug- is unknown; it is theoretically possible that the former continues Pre-Gm. \*pukk- with a geminate, but it is unclear where this geminate would have come from. As for the semantics of the cluster, MDu. puuc n. 'high quality textile' and Du. puik adj. 'fine' have been adduced in order to start from a meaning 'handpicked', cf. Du. dial. puiken 'to pick' (Kluge/Seebold 2002: 702). However, this leaves the variant \*pugunaccounted for. Related to \*puh/kkan- 'bag' (q.v.)?

Go. puggs m. 'id.', ON pungr m. 'id.', OE pung m. 'id.', ODu. pung, pong m. 'id.', OHG scaz-pfung m. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically obscure word restricted to Germanic. It is tempting to connect it to the semantically similar \*puh/kkan- 'bag' (q.v.), but this comparison requires the assumption of a proto-form \*pu-n-k-o- with a nasal infix, which is far-fetched.

MLG pars m. 'id.', MDu. pors[e) m. 'id.' (NIE). A resinous shrub that was used as an insect repellent and abortifacient. It is native to northern and western Europe and typically grows in acidic peat bogs. The geographic distribution is in keeping with the linguistic spread of the word; like MHG horse and G Porst, ON pars (cf. Nw., Sw., Da. pars) was

Ice!. pata w.v. 'to prod/tuck into', Far. pata w.v. 'to thrust, stick; to prod, poke', Elfd. puatci w.v. 'to poke', OE patian w.v. 'to push, strike, butt', MLG paten, patten w.v. 'to plant in the ground, implant', MDu. paten w.v. 'id.', Du. paten w.v. 'id.' (GM). A weak verb closely related to OE pjitan w.v. 'to push, poke, thrust, put', E to put < \*putjan- and Nw. dial. pauta w.v. 'to stick' < \*pauton-. The secondary nature of the root-final t is revealed by the frequentative MLG podderen 'to bicker', MDu. poderen, puederen 'to root; to bother', Du. peuren 'to blend' (vs. Du. peuteren 'to poke'). Together, all these forms point to an iterative verb 3sg. \*puttopi, 3pl. \*pudunanpi < \*but…
Go. raps adj. 'effortless, facile, easy', OE rCEd adj. 'fast', Du. rad adj. 'fast'
ON rpa f. 'id.', Elfd. rao f. 'row, line', MLG rat f. 'line, order(?)'
Go. ragin n. 'opinion, law, decree, task, responsibility', ON regin n.pl. 'the ruling powers, the gods', OS regina-giskapu 'fate'
ON ran n. 'robbery, plundering; booty', OHG rahan 'booty'

ON ra f. 'berth in a ship', Far. ra f. 'yard; stick, cudgel', MDu. ra f. 'stick; yard', Du. ra c. 'yard', MHG rahe f. 'stick; yard' (WEUR). A word included in nautical terminology, the original meaning being 'upright stick'; together with Nw. dial. rage m. 'thin post', Sw. dial. raga m. 'thin tree' < \*ragan- related to the iterative \*rakk/gon- 'to jut out'.
ON reifar f. pl. 'swaddling bands'

Go. ga-raij:Js adj. 'determined, arranged, commanded', ON greior adj. 'cleared, ready, settled', Far. greiour adj. 'straightforward; gentle', OE rrede, ge-rrede adj. 'ready, easy', E ready, OFri. rede, red, reid adj. 'ready; available; payable', MDu. ge-rede adj. 'ready; considerable', Du. ge-reed adj. 'id.', OHG gi-reiti adj. 'ready', MHG reite, ge-reite adj. 'id.'
ON ra f. 'id.', Far. ra-dj6r n. 'id.', Elfd. rci-diuor n. 'id.', OE rii, riiha m. 'id.', E roe, ODu. reho m. 'id.', Du. ree c. 'id.', OHG reh n., reho m. 'id.', G Reh n. 'id.'

Nw. rd f. 'border between fields', OE rawe f. 'row' (NEUR?). A formation possibly ablauting with \*rfh6-. It is usually assumed that the -w- in OE rawe (for which also cf. OE r.i?w f. 'row' < \*raiwi-) points to PGm. \*raiwo- < \*Hroik-ueh₂-. The alternative is to connect Lith. reive f. 'stripe' < \*Hrei-u-ieh₂-(?).
ON rein f./n. 'marge, strip of land', Far. rein f. 'edge of a field, piece of land', Elfd. rien f. 'id.', MHG rein m. 'marge, elevation', G Rain m. 'id.'
Go. skauda-raip n. 'shoelace', ON reip n. 'rope', OE rap m. 'id.', E rope, OFri. rap n. 'id.', OHG reif m. 'strap', G Reif m. 'id.'

Go. ur-raisjan w.v. 'id.', ON reisa w.v. 'id.', Far. reisa w.v. 'id.', OE r.i?ran w.v. 'id.', E to rear, MDu. reren w.v. 'to make fall, make shed', OHG reren w.v. 'to cause to fall', MHG reren w.v. 'to cause to fall; to make shed'
ON rakr adj. 'id.', LG rak adj. 'straight, correct'

Go. uf-rakjan w.v. 'to stretch out, extend, reach out', ON rekja w.v. 'to wrap', OE reccan w.v. 'to stretch, extend', OFri. rekka, retsa w.v. 'to reach', EFri. rii.kke w.v. 'id.', OS rekkian w.v. 'to tell, explain', Du. rekken w.v. 'to stretch', OHG recken w.v. 'to stretch, reach, explain', G recken w.v. 'id.'

ON rakki m. 'id.', Far. rakki m. '(young) dog', Elfd. rakke m. 'dog', OE rcec, racca m. 'sleuth dog', E dial. rake 'sheepdog', G Alem. rache 'sleuth dog (Canis Brittanicus)' (GM). The dictionaries usually reconstruct \*rakkan-, but E dial. rake and G Alem. rache point to a variant \*rakan- that is further substantiated by the diminutive MDu. rekel m. 'bad dog, male dog', Du. rekel c. 'villain' < \*rakila-. Both variants split off from an n-stem nom. \*rakii, gen. \*rakkaz (cf. thematicized OE rcec < \*rakka-). This n-stem has no certain etymology, but may be related to \*rakjan- 'to reach out', cf. OE racian 'to direct; to take a course'. Also cf. the conspicuously close \*brakkan- 'sleuth d…
ON rakki m. 'id.', Far. rakki m. 'parrel; jaw rope, gaffe parrel', OE racca m. 'parrel', Du. rak n. 'id.' ( < Scand.?)

Ice!. raga w.v. 'to stroll', Far. ragga w.v. 'to walk slowly', Nw. rakka w.v. 'to swerve, roam', MDu. raggen w.v. 'to run, be wild', G Pal. racken w.v. 'to rock, swing' (IE?). An o-grade iterative related to \*rukk/giin- 1 (q.v.).

E to rake 'id.', EDu. racken w.v. 'to turn, be bent, incline' (GM). Also cf. EDu. racke 'twist'. No certain etymology. Perhaps rather to be reconstructed as \*hrak(k)6n- in view of the potential link with \*hrag/kkiin'to jut out' ( < 'to be uneven'?).

OE ram m. 'id.', E ram, MDu. ram m. 'id.', Du. ram c. 'id.', OHG ram, ram mo m. 'id.', G Ramm m. 'id.' (DRV?). The original meaning of this word may have been 'to batter', cf. (M)Du., MHG rammen 'to batter; be in heat', E to ram < \*rammiin-. It may secondarily have come in use for the male sheep in view of its characteristic butting. The primary nature of the verb is further supported by the iterative variant \*ramiin- attested as G Rhnl. ramen 'to batter, have sex'.
ON rakkr adj. 'id.' (also hug-rakkr 'bold'), OE ranc adj. 'proud, arrogant', E rank, MDu. ranc adj. 'slim', Du. rank adj. 'tall'

Lith. rqzyti 'to stretch' < \*h₃r-on-g-eie-; Skt rnjciti 'to stretch, direct (oneself)' < \*h₃r-n-g-e-. An adjective derived from the nasal present \*rinkan- 'to be straight' < \*h₃r-en-g-e-, which is possibly attested as Far. rRJkka s.v. 'to reach'.

Go. ur-rannjan w.v. 'to cause to rise', ON renna w.v. 'to make run', OS rennian w.v. 'id.', Du. rennen w.v. 'to run', OHG rennen w.v. 'id.', G rennen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). The causative to \*rinnan- (q.v.).

Go. rasta f. 'mile', ON rpst f. 'id.', OE rrest f. 'rest, peace, grave', E rest, OS rasta f. 'rest, resting place, grave', OHG rasta f. 'rest, length (of three hours)' (DRV). A teh₂-stem to the root of ON rasa w.v. 'to rush' (Bjorvand 2011: 11-2), cf. ON rcis f. 'running; course' < \*reso-. Unrelated to \*r66- 'rest'.
OFri. reth n. 'id.', ODu. rath n. 'id.', Du. rad n. 'id.', OHG rad, hrad n. 'id.', G Rad n. 'id.'
OE rret m. 'id.', E rat, WFri. rot c. 'id.', OS ratta f. 'id.', MDu. ratte, rotte f. 'id.', Du. rat c. 'id.', OHG rato, ratto m. 'id.', MHG ratze m. 'id.', G Ratte f. 'id.'
Go. rapjo f. 'counting, account, explanation, number', OFri. rethia f. 'talk, acount', OS rethia f. 'id.', OHG red(e)a f. 'account, reasoning, speech', G Rede f. 'speech'
OE reaf n. 'spoil, booty; garment, robe', OFri. raf n. 'booty', OS nod-rofm. 'violent robbery', Du. roof c. 'theft, robbery', OHG roub m. 'robbery, booty', G Raub m. 'id.'
ON reyfa w.v. 'to break, tear; to rob', OE be-riefan w.v. 'to rob', E to bereave
ON rauf f. 'rift, hole', Far. reyv f. 'seat; behind, rump', Elfd. rov f. 'tail; behind', Da. rev c. 'behind'

Go. raujJs adj. 'id.', ON raurk adj. 'id.', Far. reyour adj. 'id.', Elfd. roo adj. 'id.', OE read adj. 'id.', E red, OFri. rad adj. 'red, yellow', OS rod adj. 'red', Du. rood adj. 'id.', OHG rot adj. 'id.', G rot adj. 'id.'

ON reykr m. 'smoke, steam', Far. roykur m. 'smoke; dust; smell', Elfd. riik m. 'smoke', OE rec m. 'id.', OFri. rek m. 'id.', OS rok m. 'id.', Du. rook c. 'id.', OHG rouh m. 'id.', G Rauch m. 'id.' (DRY). An i-stem derived from the strong verb \*reukan- - \*riikan- (q.v.). Also cf. ON ryk n. 'dust' < \*rukja-.

Go. raupjan w.v. 'to rip', OE riepan w.v. 'to loot', E to reap, EDu. ropen w.v. 'to pluck, tear off, OHG roufen w.v. 'to rip, tear' (DRY). A causative (or intensive) formation that in view of the root-final p must have been derived directly from the iterative \*rupp/bon- (q.v.), which syn·
ON rauta w.v. 'id.'

Go. raus n. 'id.', ON reyrr m., reyr f. 'id.', Ice!. reyr n. 'id.', Far. royggj n. 'reed canary grass' (with analogical removal of the -r), OFri. rer m. 'reed (stalk)', MDu. roer n. 'id.', OHG ror n. 'id.', G Rohr n. 'reed; pipe, tube' (GM). An etymologically obscure word. The formal and semantic similarities with OCS rogoz'b m. 'papyrus', Ru. rog6z m. 'bulrush; cattail', SCr. rogoz 'sedge' < PSlav. \*rogoz'b are striking (Hirt 1897: 234-5), but Slavic \*z does not regularly correspond to PGm. \*z. In addition, the Slavic formation may very well have metathesized from \*rozog- in view of OCS rozga f. 'twig' and Lith. regzti 'to plait' < PIE \*resg-. Also cf. OHG roria, G Rohre f. 'tube' < \*…
ON rpggr m., rpgg f. 'shaggy hair or wool', Ice!. rogg f. 'id.', Nw. ragg n. 'coarse hair of animals (esp. goats)'
Go. razda f. 'language, speech', ON rpdd f. 'voice', Far. rodd f. 'id.', OE reord f. 'speech, language, voice', OHG rarta f. 'sound, voice'
ON rif n. 'id.', Far. riv n. 'rib; reef, OE rib n. 'rib', E rib, OFri. rib m. 'id.', MDu. ribbe n./f. 'id.', Du. rib c. 'id.', OHG rippi n. 'id.', G Rippe f. 'id.'

Go. ga-redan s.v. 'to plan, intend', ON raoa s.v. 'to advise, counsel; to consult about; to devise, plan', Far. raoa s.v. 'id.', Elfd. niOa s.v. 'id.', OE rii!dan s.v. 'to counsel, decide, interpret', E to read, OFri. reda s.v. 'to advice, protect, help, cause, decide', OS riidan s.v. 'to counsel, plan, realize, help', Du. raden s./w.v. 'to advise, guess', OHG riitan s.v. 'to counsel, decide, help', G raten w.v. 'to advise'
ON r<ifr, r<Efr n. 'roof, Far. rava f. 'roof ridge of a house', OS riibo m. 'roof beam', MDu. rave m. 'id.', OHG riifo, riivo m. 'board'

Go. rign n. 'id.', ON regn n. 'id.', Far. regn n. 'id.', Elfd. raingen n. 'id.', E rain, OFri. rein m. 'id.', WFri. rein c. 'id.', EFri. rien m. 'id.', OS regan, regin m. 'id.', ODu. regan m. 'id.', Du. regen c. 'id.', OHG regan m. 'id.', G Regen m. 'id.'

Go. raihts adj. 'id.', ON rettr adj. 'straight; correct', Far. rCEttur adj. 'id.', Elfd. rett adj. 'id.', OE riht adj. 'id.', E right, OFri. riucht adj. 'id.', WFri. rjocht adj. 'id.', EFri. gjucht adj. 'id.', OS reht adj. 'id.', Du. recht adj. 'straight', OHG reht adj. 'straight, good, right', G recht adj. 'id.'
Go. rikan s.v. 'id.', MHG rechen s.v. 'id.'
Go. riqis n. 'id.', ON rekkr n. 'id.'
ON r<imr adj. 'id.', Nw. ram adj. 'id.'
Go. rimis n. 'id.'

OHG riizi adj. 'id.', MHG rceze adj. 'id.' (DRV) - Skt. r<idati 'to dig, furrow; to open, prepare the way' (with loss of the laryngeal as per Lubotsky 1981: 134?), YAv. rii8aiti 'to prepare (the way)', Lat. rodo, -ere 'to gnaw (at)' < \*Hreh₃d-e-. A V[ddhi-gerundive \*Hreh₃d-i- to a lost strong verb \*rotan- 'to bite'.
Go. ga-riuds adj. 'honorable', ON rj6or adj. 'red (in the face), ruddy', OE reod adj. 'red'
ON rj6oa s.v. 'id.', OE reodan s.v. 'to redden, stain with blood'
ON rj6oa s.v. 'to clear', OE reodan s.v. 'to kill', MHG rieten s.v. 'to exterminate, wipe out'
ON rjufa s.v. 'to break (open)', Far. poet rjuva s.v. 'to tear apart', OE reofan s.v. 'to break, tear'

ON rjuka s.v. 'to smoke, steam; to be driven, be flung, fly', Far. ruka s.v. 'to smoke; to fly off; to run, dash', OE reocan s.v. 'to reek, send forth smoke or steam', OFri. ricika s.v. 'to smoke', MDu. rieken, ruken s.v. 'to smell', Du. ruiken s.v. 'id.', OHG riohhan s.v. 'to smoke, steam', G riechen s.v. 'to smoke' (GM). A word without a generally accepted etymology. The connection with e.g. Lith. rukas m. 'haze' and Lith. rake 'cloud of dust' (Fraenkel 1965: 705-6) is highly unlikely as these may have been borrowed from Low German. The alternative link with Alb. re 'cloud' (Torp 1909: 349) is even more doubtful, as the alleged proto-form \*rougi- is only one of many possible reconstructio…

\*rukan- may represent a shortened geminate (Kroonen 2011a: 109). If so, it is possible that the strong verb arose as a back-formation to the formally identical iterative \*rukk/gon- 1 'to move to and fro' (q.v.).

OE reoma m. 'belt; ligament', OS riomo m. 'thong', Du. riem c. 'belt', OHG riomo m. 'id.', G Riemen m. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology. The word is usually mechanically reconstructed as \*reuman-, but this formation has no links outside Germanic. Related to
\*rauma(n)- 41 1 \*rewan- 'to jerk' (q.v.)? ON reim f. 'lash, thong' was borrowed from Low German (with the substitution of -e- by -ei-).
ON rj6mi m. 'id.', Far. r6mi m. 'id.', OE reoma, reama m. 'membrane', WFri. rjemme c. 'id.', MDu. rome, room m./f. 'id.', Du. room c. 'id.', OHG roum m. 'id.', G Rahm m. 'id.'

ON rjupa f. 'id.', Far. rypa f. 'id.' ( « Da. rype?), Nw. rjupe, rype f. 'id.', Elfd. rjoppa f. 'id.' (DRY). A North Germanic word often connected to \*erpa- 'light brown' and outside Germanic - to Lith. jerube f. 'hazel grouse', Latv. rubenis 'black grouse', RuCS rebb 'partridge' (cf. Derksen 2000; Kroonen 2010b: 257-258). Within the parameters of Germanic derivational history, however, it is clear that \*reup6n- was derived from the verb \*rup(p)on- attested as e.g. ON ropa w.v. 'to belch; to shout (esp. of grouse)' and MDu. roppen w.v. 'to belch'. The connection between the verb, an onomatopoetic iterative related to OSw. rapa, Nw. dial. rapa w.v. 'to belch', and the bird name further fol…
OE reotan s.v. 'to make a noise; to cry'
Ice!. rja w.v. 'to wrestle, fight; to move, tear'

gloss: to ride; to move, swing, rock
first_attestations: ON r[aa s.v. 'to ride', OE rfdan s.v. 'to ride; to move, rock', E to ride, OFri. rfdan s.v. 'to ride, drive', OS rfdan s.v. 'id.', Du. rijden s.v. 'id.', OHG ritan s.v. 'id.', G reiten s.v. 'id.'

ON rear, erar n. 'id.', Ice!. reaur m. 'id.', Far. reaur n. 'id.' (GM). Magnusson 1989: 746 connects Gr. pf.-8oc; n. 'face, body, limb', but the initial rho in Greek can only be derived from \*ur- or \*sr-, which precludes the link with ON rear. I therefore propose to link the word to Icel. ric'J 'spawning spot', Nw. rid 'spawning' and the primary strong verb Icel. rfaa s.v. 'to fuck', Nw. rida s.v. 'to wobble; to mate (of male animals)', which may be identical to \*rfdan- 'to ride; to move, rock' (q.v.). PGm. \*ridra- may then be reconstructed with an instrumental suffix: \*ridra- < \*Hridh-dhro-.
ON rifa s.v. 'to tear; to be rent, give way', OE rfpan s.v. 'to reap; to harvest', ME riven s.v. 'to tear', OFri. rfva s.v. 'id.'

OGutn. ri f. 'pole', Gutn. riij f. 'bar', MDu. rie f. 'slat, line', Du. rij c. 'row', MHG rfhe f. 'line', G Reihe f. 'line, row' (GM). A word with many closely-related variants, e.g. OHG rfga f. 'line' < \*rfgo(n}-, MLG rege, G Riege f. 'id.' < \*rigo(n}-, MDu. ree f. '(marcation) line' < \*riho(n)-, MDu. reke f. 'row, line' < \*riko(n}-, and G Recke f. 'series' < \*rikko(n)-, which may all go back to an ablauting n-stem nom. \*rfho, gen. \*rikkaz, dat. \*rigeni < \*Hreik-on, \*Hrik-n-6s, \*Hrik-en-i (Kroonen 201la: 239ff). Also cf. Nw. rja, Sw. rie m. 'pole on which grain is placed to dry' < \*rfhan-.
Go. reiks m. 'id.' (LW). A PGm. loanword from Celtic \*reg-, cf. Olr. ri, OW ri, MW ri m. 'king' < PIE \*h₃reg-, cf. Skt. rlij-, Lat. rex m. 'id.'. Also cf. Go. reiks, ON rfkr, OE rice, OFri.
Du. rekke c. 'id.', G Ricke f. 'id.'

ON rim n. 'computation', OE rim n. 'number', OHG rim m. 'number, calculation' (IE) - Gr. vdpLTO<) adj. 'countless' ( < \*r;i-h₂ri-to-; Gr. apLflμ6c; m. 'number, payment' < \*h₂ri-t-smo- (for the suffix -flμo-, see Milani 1987); Lat. ritus 'use, rite' < \*h₂rei-tu-; Olr. rim f. 'number, count', W rhifm. 'number' < \*h₂rei(dh}-mo-. The root \*h₂rei- has an extension \*h₂reidh- (for which see \*raida-) and it is possible that this extended root also gave rise to PGm. \*rima- and W rhif < \*h₂rei(dh)-mo-, a clear Germanic-Celtic isogloss (cf. Hyllested 2010). The root \*h₂rei- may in turn have arisen from \*h₂rhi-ei-, i.e. an i-present to the root \*h₂rehi- 'to count', for which see \*rapjon-.

ON rim f. 'rail; rung', Far. rim f. 'slat, narrow, board', E rim 'rung; edge', Du. dial. rim 'edge, rim' (GM). Cf. ON rimi m. 'bar; strip of land, ridge', OE rima m. 'edge, verge; strip of land', MDu. reme m. 'frame', MHG reme m./f. 'frame, support' < \*riman-. Not to be reconstructed with \*e in the root (as if related to \*remiz-, q.v) in view of Sw. dial. riiimu f. 'thin bar' < \*rim6n (Magnusson 1989: 762). The latter connection furthermore precludes the link with Lith. rJme f. 'frame' < \*h₁reh₁-m-ieh₂-.

ON skj6r f. 'id.', Ice!. skj6r m. 'id.', Far. skj6ra f. 'id.', Nw. skjor, skj<Ere f. 'id.', Elfd. stjier f. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Nordic bird name (WGm. has \*ag/kkon-, q.v.). The word has been compared to OE higora m., higore f. 'magpie' < \*hig{V)ra/6n- and OHG hehara f. 'jay' < \*hih{V}r6n- under the assumption of an s mobile, but the underlying form \*hig(V)r6- is incompatible with the Nordic word: the alternation between West Nordic skj6r and East Nordic \*ska!ra points to a proto-form \*skaiwar6- rather than \*\*skehuro-. No generally accepted etymology. In view of the weak iridescence of the bird's darker feathers there could be a connection with Nw. skjoma w.v. 'to variegate', c…

ON skaka s.v. 'to shake', Nw. skaka s.v. 'id.', OE scacan s.v. 'to shake, shiver; to run, leave, flee', E to shake, OFri. skeka s.v. 'to run away from home', OS skakan s.v. 'to leave', MDu. schaken s./w.v. 'to leave, escape, run away with someone (a woman)', OHG unt-scahhon w.v. 'to be driven' (DRV). An o-grade intensive verb back-formed to the iterative \*skakk/gon- (q.v.), an o-grade variant of \*skukk/gon- (q.v.). The original meaning was 'to shake', whence 'to shake off and 'to leave, rob', as attested for OFri. skeka. In both Dutch and Frisian, \*skakan- became conflated with the originally weak \*skekojan-, which was derived from \*skeka-, cf. OHG scah, MHG schach m. 'robbery' (cf. OE …

Far. skaka w.v. 'to shake', Elfd. skakci w.v. 'to shake, shiver', ME shaggen w.v. 'to shake, cast off, tremble', E to shag w.v. 'to toss, shake, waggle', LG skaken w.v. 'to bump, shake', MHG schacken w.v. 'id.' (EUR) - Olr. scuichid 'to move, start, go', MW ysgogi 'to move, stir, tremble', OCS skoCiti, SCr. skoCiti 'to jump, leap' < \*skok-eie-. An o-grade iterative to \*skakan- (q.v.). Etymologically, the iterative seems to replace the PIE intensive \*skok-eie-, as attested in Baltic and Slavic. Within synchronic Germanic, it functions as the iterative to \*skehan- 'to move fast' (q.v.), however, which implies that both \*skakan- - with unetymological \*k - and the zero-grade iterative \*sk…

ON skald n. 'id.' (DRY?). An a-stem probably derived from \*ske/dan- (q.v.), cf. OHG ske/to, MHG schelte m. 'blamer, criticizer, satirist' < \*skaldjan-. The long a of Ice!. skdld is problematic and cannot be explained by lengthening before -Id- in view of the -ce- of e.g. skceldinn adj. 'skilled in poetry'. This is not enough reason, however, to reconstruct the word as \*skepla-, thus assuming a link with Olr. see/, MW chwedl 'story' < \*sJcwetlo- (pace Liden 1891: 507).

Go. ska/ks m. 'id.', OE scealc m. 'id.', OFri. skalk m. 'servant; villain', OS skalk m. 'servant; disciple', Du. schalk c. 'mischief, OHG scale m. 'servant, slave', G Schalk m. 'rogue, mischief (GM). Also cf. MLG scha/k, MDu. scha/c adj. 'evil' < \*skalka- and ON skelkr, Far. skelkur, skjelkur m. 'fear, shock' < \*ska/ki-. No certain etymology. The adjective \*ska/ka- is suggestive of a strong verb \*ske/kan-, for which cf. the possibly related iterative ON skulka w.v. 'to mock' < \*skulkon- < \*sklk/9Ch₃_neh₂-. Can all these formations be reconciled by assuming a primary meaning 'wry, wrong' (cf. \*ske/ha- - \*skelga-)? This would be preferable, at least, over the link with e.g. Far. sk<ilk…
OE scamu f. 'id.', E shame, OFri. skome f. 'shame; genitals', OS skama f. 'id.', MDu. schame f. 'id.', OHG scama f. 'id.', G Scham f. 'id.'

Go. ga-skapjan\* s.v. 'id.', ON skapa s.v., skepja w.v. 'to shape, form, mould', Far. skapa w./s.v. 'to shape, mould; to cause, bring about', OE scieppan, sceppan s.v. 'to form, create', OFri. skeppa s.v. 'to form, make', OS skeppian\* s.v. 'id.', Du. scheppen s.v. 'id.', OHG seep/en s./w.v. 'id.', G schopfen w.v. 'id.' (GM). A strong verb of uncertain origin. One possibility is to assume that the verb was derived from the iterative \*skapp/bon- 'to scrape': the required semantic shift from 'to hollow out' to 'to form' (cf. Lehmann 1986: 149) is not evident, but finds support in OS skap, OHG scaf n. 'container' and OS ska po m. 'pan'. Also cf. Go. ufar-skafts f. 'first fruits' < \*ska/ti-.

Hardly related to Lat scato, -ere 'to gush forth, swarm' (thus Pokorny IEW: 950), which in spite of WPhal. schcid 'frogspawn' may rather …

Nw. vand-skjer 'Norway lemming; shrew', Elfd. stjiiri m. 'shrew', OHG scero m. 'mouse, mole', G Scher-maus 'water vole', Swi. (Jaun) s;;ere m. 'mole' (GM). A word for an animal that is difficult to identify. Most languages point to a type of mouse, especially the shrew or the vole. OHG scero as well as some Swiss forms refer to the mole, but with the addition of Elfd. stjiiri, which like the latter continues an n-stem, the meaning 'shrew' is likely to have at least some antiquity (also cf. Far. skersa f. 'shrew; vixen'). No further etymology. The connection with \*skeran- 1 'to cut' is formally possible in view of the semantically parallel OE scyrfe-miis (derived from OE sceorfan s.v. 'to gn…

\*skrepan- s.v. 'to scrape, scratch' - OE sceorfan, sceorpan, screpan 'to scratch, bite, vex', MHG schre.fen, schreven s.v. 'to scratch' u \*skrebh·e- (EUR) - Ru. skrestf (skrebU) 'to scratch' < \*skrebh-e-; Latv. s[<irba f. 'fissure' < \*skrbh-eh₂-; Lith. skifbti 'to become sour' < \*skrbh-; Lat scrobis m./f. 'hole in the ground, pit' < \*skrobh-i-. A strong verb created to the root \*skrebh-: the primary form \*skreban- is not attested, but can be postulated on the basis of the clearly secondary variants \*skerban-, \*skerpan- and \*skrepan-, which were influenced by the iterative \*skrapp/bon- - \*skarbOn- (q.v.).

Du. dial. scharten w.v. 'to scratch', MHG scherzen s.v. 'to cut off, shorten(?)' (EUR). A strong verb to the root \*skerdh-, for which see \*skarda-. The root-final t points to influence from an iterative \*skurton- < \*skrdh-neh₂-, cf. OHG, MHG schurz, OE scort, sceort adj. 'cut off; short' < \*skurta-. There is no compelling reason to assume that the latter adjective was borrowed from an unattested Lat. \*excurtus (thus Kluge 1897: 338).

'to tremble' < \*kseubh·e-; Pol. dial. chybac 'to sway, rock; to run, rush' < \*ksubh-eh₂- (with secondary \*y); Lith. skubus, Latv. skubrs adj.

grammatical_label: 'hasty' < \*ksubh-(r)o-; unrelated to OCS skubati (skubp) 'to tear' < \*skoubh-eh₂-. A strong verb related to \*skupp/bon- 1. The PIE sequence \*ks- was regularly metathesized into \*sk- (cf. PGm. \*stelan- < \*tse/-e- and \*steuban-
first_attestations: \*stiiban- < \*tseubh-e-). Also cf. OS wind-skiijla f. 'wind vane', OHG sciijla, scub/a, G Schau/el f. 'shovel, vane' < \*skub/6-.

ON skj6ta s.v. 'id.', Far. skj6ta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. stjuota s.v. 'to shoot; to jump', OE sceotan s.v. 'id.', E to shoot, OFri. skiiita s.v. 'id.', OS sciotan s.v. 'id.', Du. schieten s.v. 'id.', OHG skiozan s.v. 'id.', G schieflen s.v. 'id.' (DRV). A strong verb usually connected to Skt. skundate 'to jump', which, however, is unattested. It is more likely that the verb was back-formed to the iterative \*skut(t)on-, which in turn may have been formed to a Pre-Gm. root \*sket-, cf. Lith. skasti 'to jump' under \*skut(t)on-. This would be parallel to the creation of e.g. \*dreupan- - \*drupan- to \*drupp/bon- 'to drip/droop'. The secondary nature of the zero-grade \*u also follows from MHG scha…

Go. skeima m. 'lantern, torch', OE scfma m. 'splendor, brightness', OS skfmo m. 'shine', OHG scfmo m. 'shine, beam' (DRV). An mn-stem derived from \*skfnan- ( q.v.).

'shade, shadow' < \*skehrih₂, gen. \*skh₁-iehi-s; OCS sijati (sijajp), Ru. sijat' (sijaju), SCr. sjati 'to shine' < \*skh₁-i-ehrie-; OCS senb, SCr. sjen m. 'shadow' < \*skoh₁i-ni/o-.

\*skunkan- m. 'shank, ham' - OE sceanca, sconca m. 'id.', E shank, OFri. skunka m. 'id.', WFri. skonk c. 'id.', OHG scinco m. 'id.', G G Schinken, Car., Swab. Schunke m. 'id.'

An early loanword from Lat. scyphus m. 'drinking cup' = Gr. crKUq>oc; m. 'cup, can'. Cf. OHG scif'vessel' and scifi n. 'id.' < \*skfp-fna-.

ON skfta s.v. 'id.', OE scitan s.v. 'id.', E to shit, MDu. schiten s.v. 'id.', Du. schijten s.v. 'id.', OHG scfzan s.v. 'id.', G scheif,en s.v. 'id.' (GM). A strong verb with no certain etymology: possibly related to Gr. crxt0w 'to split' < \*skid-ie-, but at any rate associated with \*drftan- ( q.v.). See also \*skitt6n-.
Far. skitta w.v. 'id.' (GM). The iterative to \*skitan- (q.v.).

Go. skohs m. 'id.', ON sk6r m. 'id.', OE sc6h, sea n./m . 'id.', E shoe, OFri. ska m. 'id.', OS skah m. 'id.', Du. schoen c. 'id.', OHG scuoh m. 'id.', G Schuh m. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology. Perhaps derived from \*skehan- 'to move quickly' (q.v.).
ON skreor adj. 'id.' (DRv). A V[ddhi-gerundive to \*skapjan- (q.v.). With the same root vowel, cf. ON sk6o n. 'something harmful' < \*skada-.

ON skratti, skrati m. 'wizard, troll', Ice!. skratti m. 'devil', OE scrcette f. 'adulteress', ME skratte 'hermaphrodite; goblin' ( < \*skratton-), OHG scrato, scratto, scraz m. 'demon, faun', MHG schrat(e) m. 'goblin', G Sch rat m. 'id.' (GM). An n-stem originally inflected as nom. \*skrado, gen. \*skrattaz < \*skrodh-on, \*skrodh-n-6s (Luhr 1988: 252-4; Kroonen 2011a: 344-6). Perhaps with a zero grade from the genitive: OHG scrota and Sw. dial. skrutt 'devil' < \*skrut(t)an-. OHG screzzo 'larva' and G Schretz m. 'demon' have analogical umlaut from the plural.

OE ge-screncan w.v. 'to cause to shrink, destroy, supplant', screncan w.v. 'to lay a stumbling-block in a person's way, trip up, ensnare', MDu. schrencken w.v. 'to ensnare, hinder, deceive', OHG screncken w.v. 'to lay crosswise, make fall, deceive', MHG schrenken w.v. 'to place crosswise' (GM). A verbal merger of the causative to \*skrinkwan- (q.v.) and a factitive to \*skranka-, cf. WFri. skrank adj. 'oblique, diagonal'. Also cf. OHG scranc, MHG schrank m. 'bar', G Schrank m. 'cupboard' < \*skranka-.
ON skrapa w.v. 'to scrape, clatter', MDu. schraven, schrabben, schrappen, schrapen w.v. 'id.', Du. schrappen, schrapen w.v. 'to scratch', MHG schraffen w.v. 'id.'
OE screadian w.v. 'to shred, cut off, prune', E to shred, Du. obs. schroden, schrooien w.v. 'to peel', OHG scrotan s.v. 'to cut, shred', G schroten w.v. 'to crack, crush'
MLG schrecken, schricken w.v. 'to dance', MDu. schricken w.v. 'to stride; to be startled', EDu. schricken w.v. 'to jump',

\*skrimpan- Du. schrikken s.v. 'to be startled', OHG screck6n w.v. 'to jump', MHG schricken w.v. 'id.' (GM). The oldest attestations all point to a weak verb \*skrikkon-, which in MHG and MDu. seems to have become strong in intransitive use, cf. MHG schrecken, pret.3sg. schriik, 3pl. schriiken, ptc. ge-schrocken and Du. schrikken, pret. schrok, ptc. ge-schrokken. It in turn gave rise to the transitive verb MHG schrecken w.v. 'to startle', Swi. (Visp.) frekxu 'to tear' (< 'to make burst', cf. G sprengen), apparently from PGm. \*skrakkjan-. The original meaning of the verb was 'to jump', cf. OHG hewi-skrecko, G Heu-schrecke m. 'grasshopper' < \*hauja-skrikkan-, lit. "hay-hopper". No certain et…
OE scrimman s.v. 'to shrink, contract', MDu. schrimpen s.v. 'id.', MHG schrimpfen s.v. 'to shrivel'

Nw. skrl'Jkka s.v. 'to shrivel', OE scrincan s.v. 'to shrink up, dry up, dwindle', E to shrink, EDu. schrincken 'to cringe, retreat' (GM). A strong verb (cf. OSw. skrunkin, Elfd. skrukken, Nw. skrokken ptc. 'shriveled'), not to be confused with the weak Far. skrykkja w.v. 'to wrinkle; to pull, tug', Elfd. skryttja w.v. 'to shrivel; to cringe' < \*skrunkjan- (derived from \*skrunkwa-, cf. Nw. skrukk m. 'wrinkle'). G schrincken is assumed to be an adaptation of E to shrink (DWB: 15, 1754) by the poet Georg Rudolf Weckherlin (1584-1653), who worked in England for a couple of years, but EDu. schrincken appears to be native. No clear etymology. Perhaps there is a link with ME shruggen 'to shiver,…

ON skrfoa 'to creep, crawl', Far. skrfoa s.v. 'id.', Elfd. skraioa s.v. 'to pace', OE scrioan s.v. 'to go, take one's way', OFri. skrida s.v. 'to pass by', OS skrlthan, skridan s.v. 'to pace', Du. schrijden s.v. 'id.', OHG scritan s.v. 'id.', G schreiten s.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. skristi (skrindu, skridaii) 'to rush, run' < \*skridh-; Lith. skraidus adj. 'fast' < \*skroidh-u-; Latv. skriedinat 'to drive' < \*skridh-neh₂-.
ON skrior m. 'creeping', OE scrioe m. 'course', Du. schrede c. 'footstep', OHG scrit m. 'id.', G Schritt m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem created to the strong verb \*skr/pan- - \*skridan- ( q.v.).
MDu. schrompen w.v. 'id.', Du. ver-schrompelen w.v. 'id.'

OS skuddian w.v. 'id.', Du. schudden w.v. 'id.', OHG scutten w.v. 'to shake, pour, break', G schiitten w.v. 'to pour', schiitteln w.v. 'to shake' (GM). Also cf. the r-frequentative ME shod(d)re, E to shudder, MLG schoderen, schaderen, MDu. schuderen < \*skudron-. OE scudende ptc. 'shaking' could point to a strong verb \*skiidan- (cf. G schaudern 'to shudder'?), but the length of the root vowel is uncertain .(Seebold 1970: 417). No clear etymology. The connection with Lith. kutJti 'to shake up' < \*kut-ehr and Lat quatio, -ere 'to shake, toss, hurry along' < \*kwt-ie- is formally problematic because of the alternation of \*kut- vs. \*kwt- and the required assumption of the poorly understood s…
Go. skuft n. 'id.', ON skopt n. 'id.', Nw. skoft n. 'tail; stump', OHG skuft f. 'id.'

gloss: shoulder blade of cows or horses
first_attestations: MLG schuft m. 'id.', EDu. schoft, schocht 'id.', Du. schoft c. 'id.'

ME shoggen w.v. 'to shake, cast off, tremble', E to shog w.v. 'to shake, roll', obs. shock w.v. 'to go swiftly, dash', MDu. schocken w.v. 'to shake, clash', MHG schocken w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A zero-grade iterative to \*skakan-. The MHG word served as the source for Fr. choquer 'to shock', which was later borrowed back into German, Dutch and English. See also \*skakkjgon-.

Go. skulan pret.-pres. 'to owe, be indebted, be obliged to', ON skulu pret.-pres. 'to be obliged to, shall', Far. skula pret.-pres. 'id.', Elfd. ula pret.-pres. 'id.', OE sculon pret.-pres. 'id.', E to shall, OFri. skela pret.-pres. 'id.', OS sculan pret.-pres. 'id.', Du. zullen pret.-pres. 'id.', OHG scolan pret.-pres. 'id.', G sol/en pret.-pres. 'id.'

ON skuld f. 'debt', Far. skuld f. 'debt; guilt; cause', Elfd. skuld f. 'id.', OE scyld f. 'guilt, fault', OFri. skielde, skiolde f. 'guilt; tax', OS skuld f. 'id.', Du. schuld c. 'debt, guilt', OHG skuld f. 'id.', G Schuld f. 'id.' (DRV). A ti-stem to the root of \*skulan-.

OE sculdor m. 'id.', E shoulder, OFri. skolder, skulder m. 'id.', Du. schouder c. 'id.', OHG scultirra, scultera f. 'id.', G Schulter f. 'id.' ( < \*skuldrjon-) (GM?). A West Germanic word: OSw., Sw. skuldra was adopted from Middle Low Germanic. No certain etymology. Perhaps to Gr. aK£A.o<; n. 'thigh, leg, hip, shank' < \*skel-es-, but the implied assumption of an instrumental suffix -dhro- is semantically problematic.

lcel. skopa w.v. 'to swing, rock, roll', Far. skopa w.v. 'to shoot (of a star); to hurry up; to push, shove', Nw. skubba, skuppa, skopa w.v. 'to bump, shove', EDu. schobben, schoppen w.v. 'to stuff; to swing', Du. schoppen w.v. 'to kick', MHG schopfen, schoppen w.v. 'to stuff
ON skopa w.v. 'id.', MLG schoven w.v. 'id.', EDu. schobben, schoppen w.v. 'id.', MHG schopfen w.v. 'to versify'

Go. skura f. 'id.', ON skur f. 'id.', Far. skurur m. 'id.', Elfd. skaur f. 'id.', OE sciir m. 'id.', E shower, MDu. schuur m./f. 'id.', OHG skiir m. 'id.', G Schauer m. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. Nw. skl'!yre, skjere f. 'gust of wind' < \*skaurjon- and skyra w.v. 'to take off quickly' < \*skiirjan-. De Vaan 1999 compared the word to PGm. \*skur- 'to cut, tear', but in view of OS skion m. 'cloud', I propose to reconstruct a PGm. heteroclitic \*skewur, gen. skiinaz continuing a PIE ur/n-stem \*skehrur, gen. \*skhrun-6s (in view of the absence of Dybo's law, the accent must have shifted to the stem at an early stage). It was derived from the root \*skeh₁- 'shade, shadow' as found in e.g. ToB skiyo n.…
ON skuror m. 'id.', OFri. skerd m. 'cut, piece'

OE scyte m. 'id.', MDu. scote m. 'shoot; shot', Du. scheut c. 'shoot; gulp; pang', OHG scuz m. 'shot, speed', G Schuss m. 'shot' (DRV). An i-stem to the strong verb \*skeutan- (q.v.). \*skut(t)onw.v. 'to shoot' - ON skotra w.v. 'to shove, push' (r-frequentative), Icel. skota w.v. 'id.', Nw. skota, skotta w.v. 'to shoot; to shove, push, drive' (EUR) - Lith. skdsti (skantu, skataii) w.v. 'to jump, hop' <
Go. skuggwa m. 'id.', ON skuggi m. 'shadow, shade', OE scuwa m. 'id.', OHG scuwo, scu m. 'id.'
Go. slahs m. 'id.', OE siege m. 'id.', OFri. slei m. 'id.', OS slegi m. 'id.', Du. slag c. 'id.', OHG slag m. 'id.', G Schlag m. 'id.' (DRV). An i-stem derived from \*slahan- ( q.v.).

Go. slahan s.v. 'id.', ON s/6. s.v. 'id.', Far. s/6.a s.v. 'id.', OE slean s.v. 'id.', E to slay, OFri. slii., sliin s.v. 'id.', OS slahan s.v. 'id.', Du. slaan s.v. 'id.', OHG slahan s.v. 'id.', G schlagen s.v. 'id.'
ON sher, slj6r adj. 'blunt', Elfd. sliuog adj. 'id.', OE slii.w adj. 'sluggish, inert', E slow, OS sleu adj. 'slack', Du. sleeuw adj. 'blunt', OHG sleo adj. 'dull, faded'
ON slakr adj. 'id.', Far. slakur adj. 'miserable, wretched; short', OE sl<Ec adj. 'id.', E slack, OS slak adj. 'slack, cowardly', MDu. slac adj. 'slack, loose, slow', OHG slach adj. 'id.'
OS slango m. 'id.', Du. slang m. 'id.', OHG slango m. 'id.', MHG slange m. 'id.', G Schlange f. 'id.' (DRY). An agentive an-formation to the root PIE \*slenk- 'to creep', for which see
\*slinkan- and \*slunkon-.

MLG slank adj. 'flexible, limp', MDu. slanc adj. 'weak; loose; skinny', Du. slank adj. 'slim', MHG slanc adj. 'slim, skinny' (DRY) - OCS slpkb adj. 'crooked' < \*s/onk-o-. An a-grade adjective derived from \*slingan- - \*slinkan- (q.v.). Note that the root-final \*k spread from the iterative \*slunkon-.
ON s/afa-st refl.w.v. 'to slacken', Ice!. slapa w.v. 'to be slack', slabba w.v. 'to dangle', Far. slabba w.v. 'to toil', Nw. slapa w.v. 'to dangle'

Go. af-slaupjan w.v. 'to put off, lay aside', OE sliepan w.v. 'id.', OFri. slepa w.v. 'to fasten, drape around', OS slopian w.v. 'to loosen', MDu. sloven w.v. 'to roll up, slip', OHG sloufen w.v. 'to slip'

Go. slawan w.v. 'id.' (GM). No certain etymology. The comparison with OE sliima m. 'slumber' (cf. Pokorny JEW: 962-3) is possible, but uncertain. In all likelihood, the verb was derived from a lost adj. \*slawa- 'silent'. This form remains conjectural, however, in the absence of any known extra-Gm. cognates.

Go. slepan s.v. 'id.', OE slci!pan s.v. 'id.', E to sleep, OFri. slepa s.v. 'id.', OS sliipan s.v. 'id.', Du. slapen s.v. 'id.', OHG sliifan s.v. 'id.', G schlafen s.v. 'id.' (DRV?). Since its root-final \*p continues a (shortened) geminate, this strong verb is likely to have been back-formed to the iterative \*slapp/bOn- (q.v.). It is tempting to think, from this perspective, that the long \*e arose analogically on the basis of \*a in the iterative, just as \*ii in many cases was created to \*u (see e.g. \*siigan- - \*siikan- 'to suck'). This would imply, however, that PGm.

\*slupan- \*e ([ce]) was pronounced as \*ii at an early stage, thus representing the lengthened counterpart of PGm. \*a. Also cf. G sleps, OE slii!p, OFri. slep, Du. slaap, OHG sliif, G Schlaf m. 'sleep' < \*slepa/i-, and the causative Swi. (Visp.) ant-sleepfu w.v. 'to put to sleep' < \*slepjan-. The strong verb is not attested in Nordic, but we do find ON slcepa w.v. 'to laze' < \*slepjan- and ON slclpr m. 'good-for-nothing' < \*slepa-.
Go. sliupan s.v. 'to slip, slide', OE slUpan s.v. 'id.', OFri. sliapa s.v. 'id.', MDu. slupen s.v. 'to sneak', OHG sliofan s.v. 'to slip, sneak', MHG sliefen s.v. 'to slip'

OFri. sliita s.v. 'id.', OS iit-bi-sliitan s.v. 'to exclude', MDu. sluten, Limb. slieten s.v. 'id.', Du. sluiten s.v. 'to close', OHG sliozzan s.v. 'id.', G schliefien s.v. 'id.' (GM). A WGm. word parallel to \*leukan- - \*liikan- (q.v.). The meaning 'to lock' must have developed from 'to bar' in view of ME slot 'bar for a door, gate, window; bolt on a lock; iron rod', ME slot(e), E slot, obs. sloat, OFri. slot n. 'lock', (M)Du. slot n. 'id.', OHG sloz, G Schloss n. 'id.' < \*sluta- and MDu. sliet n./f. 'long rod' < \*sleuta-. OFri. slet(te) n. 'lock' (whence OFri. sletta w.v. 'to lock in') continues a formation \*slutja-. See also \*slutila- and \*sluton-.
ON sleoi m. 'id.', Du. slee c. 'id.', OHG slito m. 'id.', G Schlitten m. 'id.' (DRY). An n-stem derived from the strong verb \*slidan- (q.v.).

OE slfdan s.v. 'id.', E to slide, MDu. sliden s.v. 'id.', MHG slftan s.v. 'id.' (NEUR) - Lith. slidus, Latv. slids adj. 'slippery' < \*s/idh-u-; Lith. sljsti (sljstau, sljdau), Latv. slfst 'to slip, slide' < \*slidh-ske-; Latv. slidet 'id.' < \*slidh-ehr; OCS sledn m. 'track' < \*sloid-ro-. A strong verb created to a Germanic-Balto-Slavic root \*sleidh-. See also \*slidan-.
ON s/(m n. 'id.', Far. slim n. 'smoke, haze; slime; mucus', OE slim m./n. 'slime, mud', E slime, Du. slijm n. 'slime', MHG slim m. 'id.', G Schleim m. 'id.'

Go. fra-slindan s.v. 'id.', OS far-slindan s.v. 'id.', Du. ver-slinden s.v. 'id.', OHG slintan, slindan s.v. 'id.', MHG slinden s.v. 'to devour', G ver-schlingen s.v. 'id.' (with dialectal -ng- < -nd-) (GM). Related to OS slund, OHG slunt, G Schlund m. 'gullet' < \*slunda-. No extra-Gm. etymology. The connection with Lith. Ijsti (lendu) 'to creep, sneak (into)' through a hypothetical meaning 'to make slide down the throat' is uncompelling. See also \*landjan-.

OSw. slinka s.v. 'to sneak, crawl, slip', Elfd. slinka s.v. 'id.', OE slingan, slincan s.v. 'to creep', E to slink, MDu. slinken w.v. 'to diminish; to slump, collapse'; slingen w.v. 'to creep, crawl', Du. slinken s.v. 'to decrease', OHG slingan s.v. 'to swing, wind', G schlingen s.v. 'to loop'
OE sliw, sleow m. 'tench/mullet', MDu. slie f. 'id.', OHG silo m. 'id.', G Schleie f. 'id.'
Nw. slunka w.v. 'to stumble', G schlunken w.v. 'ro slouch'
OFri. sletel m. 'id.', OS slutil m. 'id.', Du. sleutel c. 'id.', OHG sluzzil m. 'id.', G Schliissel m. 'id.' (ORV). An instrumental noun derived from the strong verb \*sleutan- - \*s/Utan(q.v.).
Nw. slota w.v. 'to diminish, subside, stop' (DRY). The iterative to \*sleutan- - \*s/iitan- (q.v.).
OE sm<Eras m.pl. 'lips'
Go. smals adj. 'id.', ON smalr adj. 'id.', Far. smalur adj. 'narrow', OE smre/ adj. 'small, narrow', E small, OFri. smel adj. 'id.', OS smal adj. 'id.', Du. smal adj. 'id.', OHG smal adj. 'id.'
!eel. smolt n. 'id.', Nw. smolt n. 'id.', OE smolt n. 'id.', MDu. smout n. 'id.', OHG smalz n. 'id.', G Schmalz n. 'id.' (DRY). A adjective derived from \*smeltan- (q.v.).

ON smar adj. 'id.', Far. smaur adj. 'id.', Elfd. smqer, smq- adj. 'id.', OHG smahi adj. 'id.', MHG smrehe adj. 'id.' (GM). An exclusively Germanic word. The old comparison with Gr. crμLKp6<;, μLKp6<; adj. 'small', allegedly from \*smh₁-i-k-ro-, is untenable for formal reasons.
Nw. smyle, dial. smile, smele n. 'id.', OHG smelha f. 'id.', MHG smelehe, smilehe, smelwe f. 'id.', G Schmiele f. 'id.'
Nw. smelta s.v. 'id.', Elfd. smelta s.v. 'id.', OS smeltan s.v. 'id.', Du. smelten s.v. 'id.', OHG smelzan s.v. 'id.', G schmelzen s.v. 'id.'
Jee!. smcera f. 'id.', Far. smcera f. 'id.', Nw. smcere m./f. 'id.', Sw. dial. smiire m. 'id.', Da. smcere c. 'id.'
OE smeortan s.v. 'id.', E to smart, MDu. smarten s./w.v. 'id.', OHG smerzen s.v. 'id.', G schmerzen w.v. 'id.'

ON smj9r, smfiJr n. 'id.', Far. smfiJr n. 'butter', Elfd. smyor n. 'id.', OE smeoru n. 'fat, grease, tallow', OFri. smere m. 'pus', OS smeru n. 'fat', Du. smeer n. 'grease', OHG smero m. 'fat', G Schmer m./n. 'kidney fat'

ON smjuga s.v. 'to creep through', Far. smugva s.v. 'to slip through; to gnaw at, nibble away', OE smiigan s.v. 'to creep, crawl', MDu. smugen, smuken w.v. 'to crawl', MHG smiegen s.v. 'to snuggle up; to crouch' (DRV) - Lith. smaiikti (smaukiu) 'to pull, to don, to roll up' < \*smouk-ie-; Slov. smukati 'to scurry, slip' < \*smouk-eh₂-. A strong verb influenced by or back-formed to the iterative \*smukkon(q.v.).

WFri. smjunt, NFri. smiin 'id.', EDu. smiente, Du. smient, MHG smiehe f. 'id.', G Schmei(ch)-ente, Schmii-ente, Als. Schmiche f. 'id.' (GM). A word whose origin like many other duck names, e.g. teal and radge / rodge is obscure because of late attestation and considerable formal variation (see e.g. Strathmann 2008: 88-90), cf. E smee, smew, smeath, Scot. smeuth. The reconstruction of the word is further complicated by the fact that most of the continental forms derive from a compound with \*anad'duck', cf. G dial. Schmei(ch)-ente, Schmii-ente, Du. smient, Flem. smij-aande, WFri. smjunt. Spenter 1968: 304, fn. 238 and Eigenhuis 2004: 472 rightly point to the problematic t in Frisian and Dutch…
OE smeocan s.v. 'id.', MDu. smieken, smuken w.v. 'id.'

Go. ga-smeitan s.v. 'to daub', Nw. smita s.v. 'to smear over; to sneak away', OE smltan s.v. 'to daub, smear, smudge', E to smite, OFri. smita s.v. 'to throw, strike, destroy', MDu. smiten s.v. 'to throw, hit, smack', Du. smijten s.v. 'to throw', OHG smizan s.v. 'to smear', G schmeij!.en s.v. 'id.'

Nw. smita w.v. 'to stroke, smear; to spur; to flatter', OSw. smita w.v. 'to daub, smear; to hit', Elfd. smitd w.v. 'to smack; to smear, daub', OE smittian w.v. 'to befile', MDu. smetten, smitten w.v. 'to stain, befoul', MHG smitzen w.v. 'to jerk; to hit, beat; to smear, daub', G Pal. schmitzen w.v. 'to insult' (EUR). Although some of the aforementioned verbs may be denominal formations to \*smitton- (cf. OE smitte, MHG smitze, MDu. smitte, smette f. 'stain, blemish'), there is sufficient material for postulating a truly verbal iterative formation pertaining to \*smitan- ( q.v.) 'to strike', cf. Elfd. smita 'to smack' and MHG smitzen 'to hit'.

ON smfo f. '(handi)craft, smith's work', Far. smfO f. 'forging, making; (handi)craft; trade', OHG smida f. 'jewelry, metal' (GM). Also cf. the collective formation ON smfoi, Elfd. smaioe, MHG ge-smide, G Ge-schmeide n. 'jewelry' < \*smfflja-.

Go. aiza-smipa m. 'id.' (< \*smipan-), ON smior m. 'id.', Far. smiOur m. 'id.', Elfd. smiO m. 'id.', OE smip m. 'id.', E smith, OFri. smeth m. 'id.', Du. smid c. 'id.', OHG smid m. 'id.', G Schmied m. 'id.'

ON sneia f. 'id.', Far. sneia f. 'id.', Elfd. snjea n. 'id.', OFri. snethe f. 'id.', Du. snee, snede c. 'id.', MHG sneite f. 'road through a forest' (DRV). Derived from \*snfpan- (q.v.).

ON snaka w.v. 'to rummage, snuff about', Nw. snaka s./w.v. 'to snuff, look for food; to snatch', OHG snahhan s.v. 'to crouch'

Ice!. snorkinn adj. 'shrivelled, shrunken, wizened', Far. snorkin adj. 'lean (esp. of meat); scanty, scarce', Nw. snerka, snerpa s.v. 'to shrivel; to form a layer of ice', OE ge-sneorcan s.v. 'id.', MDu. snerpen w.v. 'to hurt, singe', OHG snerfan s.v. 'to shrivel', MHG snerfen s.v. 'id.' (GM). The only extra-Germanic comparandum, OPru. -staclan < \*stoklo-, is usually adduced to support the PGm. reconstruction \*stahla-. The cluster -cl- can also have developed from -ti-, however, and thus allows for the alternative reconstruction of the Germanic form as \*stapla-. 'id.' (IE). A verb in suppletion with \*sten- (q.v.). The present root \*stand- was created on the basis of the 3pl. pres. \*stanpi < \*sth₂-enti and the 3pl. pret. \*stunp, the latter continuing the PIE 3pl. aor. \*sth₂-nt. The 3sg. aor. \*stehi-t developed into \*stop, and gave rise to the secondary plural \*stodun by Verner's law after the merger of the aorist with the perfect tense (Kortlandt 1989: 102). It follows that the verb largely arose by internally Germanic analogies, and did not start ou…

Far. poet. sta suppl.v. 'id.', Nw. stci suppl.v. 'id.', OSw. stii suppl.v. 'id.', Sw. std suppl.v. 'id.', Da. std suppl.v. 'id.', OFri. stiin suppl.v. 'id.', ODu. stiin suppl.v. The verb is not attested in the oldest Germanic languages, but its occurrence in both North and West Germanic suggests that it was present already in the proto-language (for the old age of the NGm. verb, cf. Nw. dial. (Selbu) sta with Proto-Norse nasalization). The reconstruction of the original form of the word poses several difficulties, however. Many of the forms can be deriv…
OE steorfan s.v. 'to die', E to starve, OFri. sterva s.v. 'id.', OS sterban s.v. 'id.', Du. sterven s.v. 'id.', OHG sterban s.v. 'id.', G sterben s.v. 'id.'

Another possibility is to link the word to \*fleura- (q.v.) with the same meaning, but the required assumption of both ablaut with extra-Gm. \*tauro-

'to stroke, wipe (out)' (NEUR) - OCS stngati (struz9) 'to scrape, shave' < \*strugh-eh₂-; Lith. strugas adj. 'short, cropped' < \*strugh-o-. A strong verb derived from the Germanic and Slavic root \*streugh-. The received link with Gr. o-rpEuyoμm 'to be squeezed out in drops' < \*streug-e( cf. Pokorny JEW: 1028-9), is erroneous because the root-final \*k arose under the influence of the iterative \*struk(k)on- (q.v.).

Nw. str(j)upa s.v. 'to squeeze, compress; to strangle', MHG striefen s.v. 'to graze, brush' (DRY). A strong verb derived from the iterative \*struppon- (q.v.), which was probably formed to the PIE root \*strebh- 'to twist', cf. Gr. o-rp£cpw 'to twist, turn'. It gave rise to a range of nominal formations, e.g. ON str(j)upi m., strjupa n. 'throat' and Nw. strop n. 'mouth of a river'. See also the causative \*straupjan-.

OE stridan s.v. 'to stride; to pillage', E to stride, OFri. strida s.v. 'to fight', MLG striden s.v. 'to straddle the legs apart, stride', MDu. striden s.v. 'to resist, struggle', Du. strijden s.v. 'to fight', OHG stritan s.v. 'to fight', MHG striten s.v. 'to battle, endeavour; to stride(?)', G streiten s.v. 'id.' (GM) - Latv. strieties 'to resist, be unruly'; Lith. strainus adj. 'unruly' < \*stroi-nu-. A strong verb possibly continuing a compound root \*strei-dhh₁- 'to stand upright'. Also cf. \*strit(t)on- and ON streita f. 'difficulty, trouble' < \*straiton-. MHG strit m. 'pace, step' points to a formation \*stridi-.

MHG strotzen w.v. 'to bristle up'

grammatical_label: A collective formation to the root of \*steuban-

'stick, trunk', G Stock m. 'id.' (DRY). Derived from the strong verb \*stekan- (the formation looks like a Pre-Gm. ptc. \*stug-n6-). 'to shorten, trim' < \*stunjan-. No further etymology. The link with the PIE root \*steud- (Pokorny IEW: 1032-4) cannot be maintained in view the ablauting formations G stanzen w.v. 'to press, cut out, punch' < \*stantOn- and G stenzen w.v. 'to hit, beat' < \*stantjan-.

ON stupa w.v. 'id.', Nw. stupa s./w.v. 'to drop, fall down', OE stiipian w.v. 'to stoop, bend the back', E to stoop, MDu. stupen w.v. 'to bend' (EUR) - W ystum f. 'bend, turn', Bret. stumm 'id.' < \*stoup-meh₂-. A stative related to \*staupa- ( q.v.). The long \*ii probably arose secondarily, and does not have to point to a root \*\*steuHbh- with a laryngeal. In view of the remarkable similarity of Latv. stailpe 'horse track' and Nw. staup n. 'puddle; deep track; sharp turn', the original root must in all likelihood be reconstructed as \*steup- rather than \*steub(h₃_ (pace Pokorny IEW: 1032-4). The root-final p presumably spread from the iterative \*stupp/bOn-, cf. Nw. stopa w.v. 'to make a…
ON styrja f. 'id.', OE styria, styriga m. 'id.', ODu. sturio m. 'id.', Du. steur c. 'id.', OHG sturio, sturo m. 'id.', MHG stiire m. 'id.', G Star m. 'id.' (= LG)
\*str containing the a-prefix discovered by Schrijver 1997. As the prefix usually causes the root vocalism to be reduced, the expected formal proportion should admittedly have been
\*setr, but it is conceivable that the original "ablaut" was reshuffled. Note that the Baltic forms were distorted due to interference with erskژtis 'thorn' (Toporov 1975-: II, 89).

ON suga s.v. 'id.', Ice!. sjuga s.v. 'id.', Far. sugva s.v. 'id.', Elfd. sauga s.v. 'id.', OE sugan, sucan s.v. 'id.', E to suck, MDu. sugen, sucen s.v. 'id.', Du. zuigen s.v. 'id.', OHG sugan s.v. 'id.', G saugen s.v. 'id.' (DRV).
\*suw- A strong verb that in view of the \*ii and the alternation between root-final \*g and \*k must have been derived from the iterative \*sukk/gon( q.v.).

MHG sochen w.v. 'id.', G Swab. sochen, socken w.v. 'id.'

Nw. sylt f. 'brackish swamp', ODu. su/ta f. 'brackish terrain', MDu. suit f. 'head cheese', Du. zu/t n. 'id.', OHG sulza f. 'brine', G Sii/ze f. 'aspic' (ORV). A zero-grade formation to \*sa/tan- (q.v.). Also cf. Du. zilt, dial. zult adj. 'salty' < \*sultja-.

(DRY). An adverb, quasi PIE \*sh₂un-tero-, derived from \*s6el- - \*sunn6n- 'sun' ( q.v.) with the I ndo-European contrastive \*tero-suffix.

Go. sun us m. 'id.', ON sunr, sonr m. 'id.', Far. sonur m. 'id.', Elfd. sun m. 'id.', OE sunu m. 'id.', E son, OFri. suno m. 'id.', OS sunu m. 'id.', Du. zoon c. 'id.', OHG sun(u) m. 'id.', G Sohn m. 'id.'

ON supa s.v. 'to sip, drink', Far. supa s.v. 'to eat with a spoon', Elfd. saupa s.v. 'to drink; to sniff, OE siipan s.v. 'to sup, to drink', MDu. supen s.v. 'to guzzle', Du. zuipen s.v. 'id.', OHG siifan s.v. 'id.', G saufen w.v. 'to drink', intr. 'to sink' (DRY). In view of the secondary full grade \*ii (for expected \*eu) and the root-final shortened geminate, the strong verb is best analyzed as a back-formation to the iterative \*supp/b6n- 'to soak' (q.v.). Also cf. OFri. supa m. 'buttermilk', MDu. supe f. 'soup, porridge'.

Go. supon w.v. 'to season ( < \*'to dip'?)', Ice!. subba w.v. 'to soil', Nw. subba w.v. 'to dabble', OE soppian w.v. 'to dip, dunk', E to sop - sob 'to soak, dip', MDu. sopp(er)en w.v. 'to soak', Du. soppen w.v. 'to soak; be wet', OHG sofi5n, soffi5n w.v. 'to dunk' (IE?) - Skt supa- m. 'sauce, soup, broth' < \*suH-ph₃-o-. The iterative to the strong verb \*siipan- ( < Pre-Gm. \*siippan-). It is probably related to Skt. supa-, which I analyze as a compound of the PIE roots

subbanzi 'to pour') and \*peh₃- 'to drink' (cf. Ved. pfbati 'id.' < \*pi-ph₃-e-). The lack of aspiration in Sanskrit may be due to dissimilation of the second laryngeal, while the short \*u of PGm. \*supp/ban- can be due to similar loss of the first.
ON surr adj. 'sour, bitter', Far. surur adj. 'acid, sourish', Elfd. saur adj. 'id.', OE sur adj. 'id.', E sour, OFri. sure adj. 'id.', Du. zuur adj. 'id.', OHG sur adj. 'id.', G sauer adj. 'id.'

In reality, the form srefa only arose secondarily from sv<Efa < \*swebjan-, cf. ON srenskr 'Swedish' < SV<Enskr. This \*swebjan- has its long e from the pret.pl. stem \*sweb-, where it in synchronic Proto-Germanic functioned as a secondary zero grade. In Old Norse, the variation between svimma (svam, summu) vs. svima (svam, svpmu) implies that the alternation between the presentic root \*swVmm- and the preterite root \*swVm- was preserved up to a relatively late stage: svima was probably created on the basis of the preterite, otherwise the i, which in \*swemmregularly arose befo…

ON svfna s.v. 'to go down, subside', OHG swinan s.v. 'id.', MHG swinen s.v. 'id.' (IE). An n-present to the root \*dhgwhei-, cf. Nw. dial. sva w.v. 'to abate' < \*swian-. A slightly different (iterative?) formation is Ice!. svina w.v. 'to subside, disappear', Nw. svina, svena w.v. 'id.' < \*swinon- < \*dhgwhi-neh₂-. Formally, it is notable that the Germanic verbs with this root show a remarkable set of variants, viz. \*dwinan-, \*swinan- and \*pwinan-. Apparently, the outcome of the initial cluster \*dhgwh_ was unstable in Germanic.

ON tejja formally and semantically classifies as a causative, which allows us to reconstruct the verb as Pre-Gm. \*dobh-eie-. I connect this formation to the aforementioned Indo-Iranian forms, which in spite of the older literature cannot be based on a root \*dhebh- with aspirated dental (LIVZ: 132-3). grammatical_label: languages. The Germanic dialects provide evidence for four different stem variants: 1) Go. tagr, OE teagor < \*tagra-; 2) OE t<Ehher, OFri. tar, OHG zahar < \*tahra-; 3) MHG traher < \*trahra-; and 4) OS, OHG trahan < \*trahna-. Presumably, these variants all split off from a heteroclitic paradigm, e.g. nom. \*tragru (> \*tagru by dissimilation), gen. \*trahnaz. According to Kortlandt 198Sb, the original PIE word developed from a compound \*dr](-h₂e/(-ur, \*drK-h₂eK-un-h₂, identifying the first member as the PIE root \*der/(- 'to see' (cf. \*turhta- 'clear'). The second element \*h₂ek-ru- 'tear' can be retrieved from ToA ii.kiir, ToB akriina pl. 'tear', Skt. a§ru- n. 'id.', YAv. asrii n. pl. 'id.', NP ars, ask 'id.' and

A factitive to an adjective \*9a-tawa- which is only indirectly attested through the Proto-Slavic loanword as continued by OCS BOtov'b, Ru. BOt6v, SCr. BOtov adj. 'ready, finished'. The verb has been connected to ToB tsuwa 3sg.pret 'to join, adjust' and Gr. Mvaμm 'to be able' < \*du-neh₂- (cf. LIVZ: 123), which allows us reconstruct the adjective as \*douhro-. See also \*toja-.

Go. taui, gen. tojis n. 'id.' (DRY). An noun derived from \*taujan- (q.v.). The underlying proto-form, Pre-Gm. \*dou-io- developed into \*toja- by regular loss of \*u after \*o. It in turn gave Go. taui [t5i] by 1) apocope of the ending; 2) vocalization of the \*j; and 3) lowering of -o- to -5- before a hiatus.

ON t6/ n. 'id.', Far. t6/ n. 'id.', OE to/ n. 'id.', E tool (DRY). An instrumental noun to \*taujan- 'to do, make' (q.v.). The attested forms did not develop from PGm. \*towula- (pace Pokorny IEW: 218-9), but rather straightforwardly from \*to/a-. This formation, in turn, was created to the full-grade root \*to- (for which see \*toja- 'deed') by the addition of the instrumental suffix \*-d/a- < PIE \*-dh/o- (or \*-t/6- by Verner's law). The resulting \*tOd/a- regularly developed into \*to/a- by 1. the assimilation of d to I as in \*stalla- 'stand, stall, stable' < \*sthi-dh/o- and 2. the simplification of geminates in heavy syllables.

grammatical_label: No extra-Germanic etymology; the Pre-Gm. root \*dut- could be a formal variant of the more widespread \*duk- 'to pull' (see \*teuhan- and \*tukk/gon-), but the full-grade forms (see below) rather point to a root \*det-. The meaning 'to pull' must have developed into 'to pluck' at an early stage in view of e.g. ON, Ice!. toddi, m. 'tuft', MHG zotte, G Zotte 'id.', Du. todde c. 'rag' < \*tudda/on- OHG zota, G Zote f. 'hair; rag' < \*tud6n- and G Swab. Zotze f. 'tuft, brush' < \*tuttan-. The origin of the ablauting forms OHG zato m. 'shaggy hair' < \*tadan-; OHG zatta f. 'flax', G Zatte f. 'windrow, sheaf < \*tadd6n- and OE t<Ettec m. 'rag' < \*tattaka- is not entirely clear, but may point either to an ablauting n-stem \*tado, gen. \*tuttaz (cf. \*mapan-
first_attestations: \*muttan- 'moth') or to an otherwise unattested a-grade iterative \*tatt/don-. Note that the related OHG zaturra f. 'harlot' < \*tadur-jo(n)- and ON t9turr m. 'id.' < \*tatura- could theoretically even point to an old r/n-stem nom. \*d6t-r, gen. \*dt-n-6s, yielding PGm. \*dapur, \*duttaz. See also \*teudra- - \*tildra-.

An etymologically obscure word (cf. Sjolin 2006: 231) found only dialectally along the North Sea coast (cf. Opprel 1937). The distribution of the coastal deek vs. the more inland daak in the Dutch dialects points to a vowel \*e (rather than \*ai or \*a). In combination with WFri., NFri. teek, which implies an initial fricative, the word can confidently be reconstructed as PGm. \*jJeka-. ON urr m. 'id.', OE ur m. 'kind of OX, bison', OHG uro m. 'aurochs', MHG ure, ur m. 'id.', G Auer-ochs m. 'id.' (GM). A u-stem of disputed origin. It is formally and semantically close to the IE word for 'male animal', \*(u)rs-en- (for which see \*kursan- and \*urzan-), but it is impossible to explain the Germanic long u from this form. It has therefore been suggested that the word was adopted from a lost European language (Schrijver 2003a: 223). It seems likely, however, that the u-stem split off from a regular n-stem, just as ON Qrn 'eagle' < \*arnu- split off from PGm. \*aran- 1. If so, it must be assumed that the n-stems OHG uro and MHG ure < \*uran- are primary, which would bring the wo…

It continues a formal variant of PIE \*urs-en- 'male animal' (for which see \*kursan- 'calf) plus another unknown element. In view of the seemingly ablauting variants OE wor-hana, Du. dial. woer-haan < \*worzan- < \*ueHursen- and MHG ur-hane, G Auer-huhn < \*u(w)urzan- < \*uHrsen-(?), it is tempting the reconstruct this…

OE wasend m. 'windpipe; gullet', OFri. wasanda, wasenda m. 'wind-pipe', OHG weisunt, weisant f. 'wind-pipe; vein' (DRY). Usually assumed to be related to Skt. ve$ati 'to flow' < PIE \*ueis- (Pokorny JEW 1134) through an intermediate meaning 'the flowing one' (cf. the OHG meaning 'vein'). This etymology seems to be strengthened by Nw. veise f. 'rivulet; horsetail' < \*wais6n-, especially since the noded stalk of the horsetail is reminiscent of the windpipe. PGm. \*waisunda- contains the suffix \*-und- < PIE \*-nt-, for which cf. ON hprund n./f. 'flesh', vitund f. 'consciousness, skill' (cf. also OGutn. hugund f. 'need, nature's call'). Note that, in English, dial. wozzen ( = OE wasend) is acc…

OE war n. 'seaweed; sand', ME w6r 'seashore, beach; seaweed', MDu. wier n. 'id.', woer f. 'seaweed', EDu. Zeal. woer 'id.', Du. wier n. 'id.' (IE). The correspondence of OE war with Du. wier (with e < \*ai becoming ie in dialectal Dutch) points to \*waira- or \*waiza-. The coastal variant woer is an lngvaeonic form. The usually assumed link with ON vfrr, OE wir m. 'wire' < \*wira- is possible, but semantically improbable in view of the OE and ME meanings 'beach' and 'sand'. I therefore assume a connection with Icel. veisa f. 'moist, swamp, mud; filth; gutter' < \*wais6n-, derived from the PIE root \*ueis- 'to flow' (for which cf. \*waisunda/6-).

A root with an original Gm. meaning 'to roll (over), to toss'. The word was often used in the context of rolling waves cf. ON va/k, Far. valk n. 'swell (at sea)', OE wea/ca m. 'wave' < \*walka(n)-. On the IE level, the usual etymological connection with Skt. valgati 'to move up and down, boil up, spring' < \*uelg-e-(?) and Latv. vaigs m. 'rope' < \*uolg-o- (cf. LIV2), is improbable in view of the obvious intra-Gm. link with \*walk/gon- 'to roll', impers. 'to be nauseated'. It follows from this link that the k of \*walkan- must have arisen due to i…

'to shift, move' < \*wiko(ja)n- is semantically closer, however, to the Gothic meaning 'sequence' as well as to ON vfxl, OS, OHG wehsa/, Du. wisse/, G Wechsel m. 'shift' < \*wfh-sla-, and thus presupposes an original meaning 'shift'.

ON vfkva, ykva, vfkja s.v. 'id.', Far. vfkja s.v. 'id.', Elfd. waika s.v. 'id.', OE wfcan s.v. 'id.', OFri. wfka s.v. 'id.', OS wfkan s.v. 'id.', Du. wijken s.v. 'id.', OHG wfhhan s.v. 'id.', G weichen s.v. 'id.'

'id.', E to win, OFri. winna s.v. 'to achieve, win', OS winnan s.v. 'to suffer; to gain', Du. winnen s.v. 'to gain; to win', OHG winnan s.v. 'to labor; to win', G winnen s.v. 'to win'

Go. wintrus adj. 'id.', ON vetr m. 'id.', Far. vetur m. 'id.', Elfd. witter m. 'id.', OE winter m. 'id.', E winter, OFri. winter m. 'id.', OS wintar m. 'id.', Du. winter c. 'id.', OHG wintar m. 'id.', G Winter m. 'id.' (GM). A word with no certain etymology. The traditional derivation from a nasalized variant of the root \*wed- 'to be wet' (see \*weta-) is unconvincing: it is based on the wrong analysis of Lat unda f. 'wave' as continuing \*u-n-d-eh₂- rather than correct \*ud-neh₂- (with Thurneysen's law). The connection with Olr. find adj. 'white' < \*windo- is semantically weak.

Go. wair m. 'id.', ON verr m. 'id.', OE wer m. 'id.', OS wer m. 'id.', OHG wer m. 'id.' ::) \*uiH-r6- (IE) - Identical to Skt. virci- m. 'man, hero, son', Av. vira- m. 'man, human', MP wir 'man, hero', Lith. vjras, Latv. virs, OPru. wijrs m. 'man', Lat. vir m. 'id.', Olr. fer m. 'id.', OW gur, MW gwr 'id.'; ToA wir adj. 'young; fresh'(?). One of the IE words for 'man'. The Gm. form developed from \*ylr6- by Dybo's law (pretonic shortening).

Go. un-wiss adj. 'uncertain', ON viss adj. 'id.', OE wiss adj. 'id.', OFri. wis adj. 'id.', Du. wis adj. 'id.', on-gewis adj. 'uncertain', OHG gi-wis adj. 'certain', G ge-wifl adj. 'wisent', dial. iz(j)ubr 'red deer', Oss. dombaj 'aurochs', Abkh. a-dawp-ey 'id.'. 'horn') and \*ues- 'to consume' (Stiles 2004), but none of these IE-oriented explanations seem convincing. The various Balta-Slavic forms combine…

Go. wlaiton w.v. 'to look around', ON leita w.v. 'to search, look for', Far. leita w.v. 'id.', Elfd. Iieta w.v. 'to look for, search; to inspect', OE wliitian w.v. 'to gaze, look' (GM). An o-grade iterative to the strong verb \*wlftan- (q.v.). Note that the meaning 'to search' in ON is typically frequentative ('to look repeatedly').

OE wlacu, wlcec adj. 'id.', ME wlak adj. 'id.', MLG wlak adj. 'id.', MHG vlac adj. 'id.' (GM). An etymologically obscure adjective. The original paradigm, \*w/akwaz, gen. \*wlakwesa, developed into \*wlaku, gen. \*w/akkwes in West Germanic, with gemination in the genitive. Dialectal leveling can account for the difference between OE wlacu < \*w/ak- and MHG v/ac(k) < \*wlakk-.

OE wlanc, wlonc adj. 'proud, bold', ME wlonk adj. 'excellent, fine; haughty, arrogant', OS wlank adj. 'proud, arrogant' (GM). An adjective derived from an unattested strong verb \*wlinkan-. Also cf. the factitive OE wlencan, OS wlenkian w.v. 'to make arrogant' < \*wlankjan-.
ON /[ta s.v. 'id.', Far. lfta s.v. 'to cast a glance (in a certain direction)', OE wlftan s.v. 'to look, gaze'

MHG litzen w.v. 'to strive for, desire' (GM). The iterative of \*wlftan- 'to see' (q.v.). The meaning 'to strive for' developed from 'to look repeatedly', which is in agreement with the iterative formation.

MLG w/6m, wloum adj. 'turbulent', MDu. wloemich adj. 'confused, anxious' (GM). Perhaps from \*wl6tma-, cf. OE wlatian w.v. 'to be nauseated', MLG wlaten w.v. 'to be disgusted'. The primary meaning would then be 'to turn'.
Go. wads adj. 'possessed', ON 6or adj. 'frantic, furious', OE wad adj. 'insane', Du. obs. woed adj. 'frantic, wild, crazy' w

Olr. faith m. 'sooth-sayer, prophet'; Oir.fath m. 'prophesy', W gwawd 'poem, satire' < \*ueh₂-tu- (Lat. viites, -is m. 'prophet, seer' is probably a Celtic loanword, cf. Gaul. Ouateis pl. 'prophet'). A Germanic-Celtic isogloss. Also cf. OHG wuot, G Wut m. 'rage, frenzy', MDu. woet m./f. 'id.', Du. woede c. 'id.' < \*w6di- < \*ueh₂-tf- and ON 6or m. 'mind, feeling; song, poetry', OE woo f. 'sound, noise; voice, song' < \*wopa/6-.
MDu. woeg adj. 'fidgety, restive' (DRV). A V[ddhi-gerundive to \*wegan- (q.v.), or perhaps rather to a related, but unattested intensive verb \*wagan- < \*u6gh-e-, for which see \*wakk/gon-.
MHG wiiegen w.v. 'id.' (DRV). A secondary causative to \*wahan- (q.v.).

Go. wokrs m. 'interest, additional increment', ON okr n. 'usury', OE wocor f. 'offspring, interest', OFri. wok er m./n. 'interest; usury', Du. woeker c. 'rampant growth; usury', OHG wuohhar m.,n. 'fruit, harvest, gain', G Wucher m. 'usury'
OE wol m./f. 'pest, plague', OS wol m. 'pest, ruin', OHG wuol m. 'ruin, disaster' (DRV). A lengthened grade form, quasi PIE \*u6/h₃-o-, closely related to \*wa/a'the slain' (q.v.).
MDu. woene f. 'id.', MHG wuone f. 'id.', G Wuhne f. 'id.' (DRV). A V[ddhi-formation derived from \*wana- 1 (q.v.).
ON rerr adj. 'raging, furious', OE werig adj. 'tired', E weary, OFri. wirch adj. 'id.', WFri. wurch adj. 'id.', OS worig adj. 'worn out'
OE weste adj. 'id.', OS wosti adj. 'id.', Du. woest adj. 'id.', OHG wuosti adj. 'id.', G wiist adj. 'id.'
Go. wojJeis adj. 'id.', OE weoe adj. 'id.', OS wothi adj. 'id.', OHG wuodi adj. 'id.' (GM). Also cf. ON reori comp. 'preferably, better' < \*wojJizan-. No further etymology.
